CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported a gradient amplifier temperature warning on the Siemens MAGNETOM Skyra during high-demand scanning.
CCR.Cause: Verified warning at the operator console; room temperature and external airflow were acceptable, but the warning returned after cooldown and restart.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MRI from clinical service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the fault behavior, and escalated to Siemens service for gradient/cooling system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact warning message or error code
Scan type or sequence running when warning occurred
Whether warning appeared during startup, idle, prescan, or active scan
Room temperature and technical room conditions
Any visible leaks, condensation, heat, smell, or unusual noise
Whether HVAC or facility water issues were reported
Whether cooldown or controlled restart changed the behavior
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI System - Image artifact, RF noise, distortion, or failed phantom QA
description: Troubleshooting MRI image artifacts, RF noise, distortion, or phantom QA failures using external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the artifact, noise, distortion, or QA failure occurs during patient imaging, stop using the affected protocol or system for diagnostic imaging until the issue is understood. Move the patient off the table when safe and have clinical staff use another available MRI system if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected and no diagnostic study depends on unreliable image quality.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Image Quality Problem
steps.instructions: Review the reported issue with MRI staff and determine whether the problem is: RF noise; Ghosting; Geometric distortion; Signal dropout; Shading or nonuniformity; Failed phantom QA; Artifact only on one protocol, coil, body region, or sequence. Expected outcome: The artifact pattern is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues. If the issue was related to a single incorrect protocol selection or temporary scan setup error, correct it, repeat the test scan, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Check for Active Patient or Room Safety Concerns
steps.instructions: Inspect the scanner room for unusual smells, heat, water leaks, loud abnormal sounds, damaged cables, loose panels, or signs of equipment contact near the bore or table. Expected outcome: No immediate safety issue is present. If overheating, fluid leakage, smoke, burning smell, abnormal compressor/chiller behavior, or unsafe scanner condition is found, remove the system from service and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Artifact Is Reproducible
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff whether the issue occurs: Every scan; Only with one coil; Only with one table position; Only after a specific protocol; Only during certain times of day; Only after system startup or restart. Repeat a controlled phantom or localizer scan if allowed by site policy. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering can separate a repeatable system issue from a one-time scan event. If the issue cannot be reproduced and QA passes, return the system to service per facility policy and document the test results.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Phantom Setup
steps.instructions: For failed phantom QA, verify the correct phantom is being used, positioned correctly, filled properly if applicable, and placed according to the site’s standard QA procedure. Check that the phantom is not damaged, leaking, underfilled, expired, contaminated, or positioned off-center. Expected outcome: The QA failure is not caused by an incorrect or poorly positioned phantom. If correcting phantom setup resolves the failed QA, document the setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Coil Selection and Coil Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct coil was selected and physically positioned for the scan. Inspect the coil for visible damage, cracked housing, damaged cable strain relief, bent connectors, contamination, or evidence of fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: The coil is appropriate for the scan and shows no obvious external damage. If the artifact follows one specific coil, remove that coil from service, label it, and test with a known-good coil if available.

steps.title: Inspect Coil Connections and Table Interface
steps.instructions: Power down or follow local safe handling procedures before reseating coil connections if required by site policy. Check that coil connectors are fully seated, table connection points are clean and undamaged, and no cable is pinched under the patient table, pads, or accessories. Expected outcome: Coil communication and RF signal path issues caused by loose or damaged external connections are ruled out. If reseating or replacing the coil resolves the artifact, document the faulty coil or connection and stop.

steps.title: Check for External RF Noise Sources
steps.instructions: Verify the scanner room door closes fully and seals correctly. Look for items that should not be in the MRI room, including non-MRI accessories, chargers, loose cables, unauthorized electronic devices, damaged headphones, monitoring accessories, pumps, or communication equipment. Expected outcome: External RF noise sources are removed or ruled out. If removing a device or correcting the room door condition resolves the noise, document the source and stop.

steps.title: Inspect MRI-Compatible Accessories
steps.instructions: Check any accessories used during the failed scan, such as pads, patient monitors, respiratory sensors, ECG leads, headphones, audio equipment, contrast injector connections, or positioning aids. Confirm cables are routed properly, not looped unnecessarily, and not crossing the imaging area in a way that could contribute to artifact. Expected outcome: Accessory-related artifact sources are ruled out. If the artifact disappears when a specific accessory is removed, remove that accessory from service and document the finding.

steps.title: Review Protocol and Sequence Factors
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff whether the issue occurs only on a specific protocol, sequence, bandwidth setting, orientation, acceleration setting, or body region. Compare the affected protocol to a known-good protocol or standard QA sequence. Expected outcome: Protocol-related causes are identified before suspecting hardware failure. If only one edited or nonstandard protocol fails, have MRI leadership or applications support review the protocol before hardware escalation.

steps.title: Check Table Positioning and Landmarking
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient table moves smoothly, landmarking is accurate, and the scan area is centered properly in the bore. For distortion or signal dropout, verify the anatomy or phantom was not positioned too far off isocenter. Expected outcome: Positioning-related image quality problems are ruled out. If proper landmarking and centering resolves the issue, document table position or setup error and stop.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Check the equipment room and magnet room conditions for obvious environmental problems, including high temperature, airflow blockage, humidity concerns, water leaks, or recent HVAC issues. Expected outcome: Environmental causes that can affect scanner stability are ruled out. If environmental conditions are outside site limits or visibly abnormal, notify Facilities and remove the scanner from service until conditions are corrected.

steps.title: Check Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue started after: Coil replacement; Software update; Service visit; Room construction; New equipment added near the MRI suite; HVAC work; Power event; Network or console changes. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified as possible contributing factors. If the issue began immediately after a known change, include that detail in the escalation notes.

steps.title: Run or Request Standard Site QA
steps.instructions: Perform or observe the site-approved phantom QA or image quality test without deviating from approved procedure. Record whether the test passes or fails and note the specific failure mode. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed with a controlled test rather than only clinical scan feedback. If QA passes and clinical images are acceptable, return the system to service per policy and document the result.

steps.title: Review System Messages Without Performing Internal Repair
steps.instructions: Check the operator console or service notification area for active warnings related to RF, gradient, coil, reconstruction, image quality, cooling, or system readiness. Expected outcome: Any system-reported faults are captured for escalation. Do not perform internal adjustments, calibration, RF tuning, gradient service, or board-level repair unless authorized and trained.

steps.title: Escalate When External Causes Are Ruled Out
steps.instructions: If the artifact, RF noise, distortion, or failed phantom QA remains after checking coils, accessories, setup, environment, room door, protocol, and phantom positioning, treat the issue as a likely internal scanner, RF chain, gradient, shielding, reconstruction, calibration, or system performance problem. Expected outcome: The device is removed from clinical use and escalated appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported RF noise and signal dropout on MAGNETOM Skyra brain scans, with a failed daily phantom QA test.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering verified the artifact was reproducible during phantom QA after checking phantom setup, coil condition, coil seating, room door closure, accessories, and scan setup.
CCR.Resolution: System was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, QA failure details were documented, and Siemens MRI service was contacted for further evaluation.

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI System - Host, measurement system, reconstruction, or boot failure
description: Troubleshooting Skyra host startup, measurement, reconstruction, or boot failures before escalating for Siemens service evaluation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm there is no patient in the bore and no active scan in progress before troubleshooting. If the failure occurs during patient imaging, stop the exam safely, remove the patient from the scanner room, and move the patient to another MRI system if clinically necessary. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before system troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff what failed and when it occurred: Host will not boot; Exam software will not open; Measurement will not start; Reconstruction is delayed or failed; Scanner stopped during startup; Error appeared after power event, reboot, software lockup, or scan attempt. Record the exact error message, system state, and whether the issue is constant or intermittent. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before resets or escalation.

steps.title: Check for Active Patient or Exam Data Concerns
steps.instructions: Confirm whether any patient images, unsent studies, or incomplete reconstructions are present. Do not repeatedly reboot the host if staff are concerned about missing studies or interrupted reconstructions. Expected outcome: Clinical data risk is considered before power cycling or further action.

steps.title: Check Room Power and Visible System Status
steps.instructions: Verify that the MRI control room equipment, host computer, monitors, keyboard, mouse, reconstruction equipment, and scanner electronics indicators appear powered. Check for obvious facility power problems, tripped local power strips, disconnected cords, or failed UPS indicators. Expected outcome: External power loss or disconnected equipment is ruled out. If restoring power or reconnecting an external cord resolves the issue, confirm normal startup and stop.

steps.title: Check Host Workstation Peripherals
steps.instructions: Verify that the monitor is powered, the video cable is secure, and the keyboard and mouse are connected. A blank screen or unresponsive workstation may be a display or input issue rather than a full host failure. Expected outcome: The host workstation can be viewed and controlled. If the workstation was functioning but the display or input device was disconnected, correct it, confirm normal operation, and stop.

steps.title: Look for UPS or Power Conditioning Problems
steps.instructions: Check whether the host, reconstruction computer, or related control room equipment is connected to a UPS. Look for UPS alarm lights, overload warnings, low battery indicators, or outlets that are not supplying power. Do not bypass hospital-approved power protection unless directed by site policy or Siemens service. Expected outcome: Power backup or conditioning issues are identified before assuming system hardware failure.

steps.title: Check Network and System Communication Indicators
steps.instructions: Verify that network cables to the host, reconstruction system, scanner network equipment, and control room switches are seated and undamaged. Look for link lights where visible. Expected outcome: A loose network connection is ruled out as a cause of host-to-measurement or reconstruction communication failure. If reseating an external network cable restores communication, confirm system function and document the finding.

steps.title: Confirm Scanner Console Startup Sequence
steps.instructions: Observe where startup stops: Operating system does not load; Host loads but MRI application does not launch; Application launches but cannot connect to scanner; Measurement system does not become ready; Reconstruction server does not respond; Patient browser opens but images do not reconstruct. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to host boot, application startup, measurement communication, or reconstruction workflow.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Software Restart if Safe
steps.instructions: If no patient is present and no data loss concern exists, perform a normal software restart using the available shutdown or restart method. Avoid hard power cycling unless the system is frozen and local policy allows it. Expected outcome: Temporary software lockups or communication hangs may clear after a controlled restart. If the system returns to normal operation, perform a basic readiness check and stop.

steps.title: Check for Recent Events or Changes
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the failure followed: Facility power interruption; Network outage; Software update; System service; Coil or table issue; Failed scan sequence; Emergency stop or system shutdown; Unusual noise, odor, heat, or alarms. Expected outcome: A triggering event is identified that may help Siemens service or internal support.

steps.title: Check Reconstruction Behavior
steps.instructions: If acquisition appears to complete but images are missing, delayed, or stuck, determine whether the issue affects one exam, one sequence, or all exams. Check whether prior images can be opened and whether new images appear after waiting an appropriate amount of time. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into a single-study reconstruction delay versus a broader reconstruction system failure.

steps.title: Check Measurement System Readiness
steps.instructions: If the host boots but scans cannot start, check whether the scanner reports ready status, table status, coil status, door status, and system communication readiness. Do not assume the measurement system has failed until basic interlocks and connected accessories are checked. Expected outcome: External readiness conditions are ruled out before escalation.

steps.title: Confirm Error Persistence After Basic Recovery
steps.instructions: If the same boot, host, measurement, or reconstruction fault returns after a safe restart and external checks, stop troubleshooting. Repeated reboot attempts can delay patient care and may risk incomplete data handling. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as persistent and appropriate for service escalation.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported that the Siemens MAGNETOM Skyra would boot to the host console, but the measurement system would not become ready and exams could not be started.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering verified control room power, workstation peripherals, network connections, and performed a safe software restart, but the measurement communication fault returned.
CCR.Resolution: Unit was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, Siemens service was contacted, and error behavior with startup details was documented for follow-up.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient was on the table when the fault occurred
Exact host, measurement, reconstruction, or boot error message
Whether reconstruction was delayed, failed, or missing images
Whether the issue followed a power outage or network outage
Monitor, keyboard, mouse, and workstation power status
Any unusual sound, heat, smell, or alarm behavior
Whether a controlled restart was attempted

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Sola MRI System - BioMatrix Sensor, Respiratory Curve, or Patient Sensing Fault
description: Troubleshooting BioMatrix sensor, respiratory curve, or patient sensing issues using safe external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the respiratory curve, BioMatrix sensing, or patient signal is unreliable during an active MRI exam, do not continue using that signal for scan timing or patient-dependent acquisition. Pause the exam if clinically appropriate, communicate with the MRI technologist, and ensure the patient is stable and monitored according to MRI safety policy. Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on a questionable sensing signal. If this resolves the concern by switching to a safe alternate workflow, document and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what was observed: No respiratory curve displayed; Flat or noisy respiratory waveform; Intermittent respiratory triggering; Patient sensor not detected; BioMatrix signal appears delayed, unstable, or inaccurate; Issue occurs only with certain patients, coils, or exam types. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check That the Patient Is Properly Positioned
steps.instructions: Verify that the patient is positioned correctly on the table and within the expected sensing area for the exam setup. Poor patient positioning, excessive gap, unusual body habitus, or patient movement can affect sensing quality. Expected outcome: Patient position supports reliable sensor detection and respiratory signal acquisition. If repositioning restores a stable respiratory curve, return the system to clinical use and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Table Pads, Positioning Aids, and Accessories
steps.instructions: Check for pads, blankets, cushions, straps, or accessory items that may interfere with patient sensing or respiratory detection. Remove unnecessary materials between the patient and sensing surface when clinically safe and MRI-compatible. Expected outcome: The sensing path is not blocked or distorted by external accessories. If the curve returns after removing or adjusting accessories, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify Coil and Table Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct coil is being used, fully seated, and properly connected. Check that the table is docked correctly and that no coil cable, accessory cable, or patient monitoring lead is pinched, stretched, or routed across a sensing area. Expected outcome: Coil/table setup is normal and not interfering with patient sensing. If correcting the setup resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Check the Respiratory Signal Display and Scan Setup
steps.instructions: Have the technologist confirm that the intended respiratory or patient-sensing option is selected for the protocol. Verify whether the issue occurs in one protocol only or across multiple respiratory-triggered sequences. Expected outcome: The system is looking for the correct signal source and the issue is not protocol-specific. If a protocol selection or setup issue is found, have MRI staff correct the exam setup and stop.

steps.title: Look for External Patient Motion or Breathing Pattern Issues
steps.instructions: Observe whether the patient is moving, talking, coughing, holding breath unintentionally, or breathing too shallowly for a usable curve. Patient-related variation can appear as an equipment fault when the signal is actually too weak or inconsistent. Expected outcome: The signal behavior matches patient motion and breathing pattern. If the issue is patient-related, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Sensor Areas and Table Surface
steps.instructions: With the patient removed and the table available, visually inspect the table surface, pad condition, and accessible sensor-related areas for: Damaged table pads; Fluid contamination; Cracked covers; Loose trim; Objects trapped under pads; Signs of impact or misuse. Expected outcome: No obvious external condition is preventing proper sensing. If contamination or obstruction is found, remove the device from use until cleaned, dried, and evaluated according to facility policy.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: If the scanner is not supporting an active patient exam, coordinate with MRI staff and follow site-approved restart procedures for the host or affected workflow. Do not interrupt active scanning, patient care, or system cooling/support functions without proper coordination. Expected outcome: Temporary software or workflow communication issues clear after a controlled restart. If normal respiratory curve and sensing function return, document and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: When clinically appropriate, compare performance using a known-good protocol setup, standard positioning, and a cooperative test subject or approved test workflow. Do not create unsafe scanning conditions or bypass MRI safety procedures. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as either setup-specific or repeatable across normal configurations.

steps.title: Check for Pattern Across Rooms, Coils, or Tables if Applicable
steps.instructions: If the site has similar Siemens MRI systems or multiple coils/table accessories, compare whether the issue follows: A specific coil; A specific table; A specific protocol; A specific patient setup; The MAGNETOM Sola system itself. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Review System Messages and Error Logs Available to Clinical Engineering
steps.instructions: Record any displayed Siemens system messages, warnings, or fault codes related to BioMatrix, patient sensing, respiratory triggering, table communication, or coil/sensor communication. Expected outcome: Escalation includes useful evidence for Siemens service or advanced bench/system evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: MRI technologist reported no usable respiratory curve on the Siemens MAGNETOM Sola during respiratory-triggered imaging.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the patient positioning pad was folded under the torso and interfering with patient sensing.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned the pad, verified stable respiratory curve with MRI staff, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from scanner before troubleshooting
Exact BioMatrix or respiratory signal symptom
Whether issue was flat, noisy, intermittent, or absent
Protocol or sequence where the issue occurred
Any visible table pad damage or contamination
Whether fault followed a coil, table, protocol, or patient setup

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Sola MRI System - Dockable Table / eDrive Movement or Docking Fault
description: Troubleshooting dockable table movement, eDrive, table travel, or docking faults before escalating for MRI service repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the patient is on the MAGNETOM Sola table and table movement, docking, or eDrive control is unreliable, stop movement immediately. Support the patient, communicate with MRI staff, and follow site policy for safe patient removal or repositioning. Expected outcome: The patient is protected from unintended table motion, incomplete docking, or unsafe transfer. If patient safety is affected, stop troubleshooting and remove the system from clinical use.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Table or Docking Symptom
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff what occurred and when it occurred. Confirm whether the issue involves: Table will not dock; Table will not undock; eDrive does not respond; Table movement is slow, jerky, or intermittent; Docking indicator does not confirm locked position; Table movement fault appears on the console; Table position is not recognized by the MRI system. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to table motion, docking recognition, eDrive control, or position feedback.

steps.title: Verify the Scanner Is Not in Active Patient Operation
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not scanning, not preparing to scan, and not actively positioned for patient imaging. Do not test table movement during an active exam unless directed by MRI clinical leadership and it is safe to do so. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting occurs only when table movement will not place a patient, staff member, or accessory at risk.

steps.title: Check the Area Around the Dockable Table
steps.instructions: Inspect around the patient table, floor track area, docking interface, and bore entrance for visible obstruction. Look for: Coils hanging over the edge; Patient positioning pads caught under the table; Cables pulled tight; Linen, blankets, straps, or cushions interfering with movement; Foot pedals, carts, stools, or floor debris near the table path. Expected outcome: The table has a clear travel path. If an obstruction is found and removed, retest table movement. If normal operation returns, document the obstruction and stop.

steps.title: Check Table Docking Alignment
steps.instructions: Inspect whether the dockable table is physically aligned with the scanner docking point. Confirm the table is straight, centered, and fully seated in the expected docking position. Expected outcome: The table can mechanically align with the docking interface. If the table was misaligned and docking works after realignment, return the system to service after normal function is confirmed.

steps.title: Inspect the Docking Interface Externally
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the docking interface without removing covers or disassembling components. Check for: Bent or damaged external docking hardware; Loose visible connector areas; Foreign material at the dock; Damaged latch surfaces; Signs of impact from a stretcher, table, or accessory cart. Expected outcome: No obvious external damage is preventing docking. If visible damage is found, remove the system from service and escalate for MRI service evaluation.

steps.title: Check eDrive Enable Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the eDrive control is being used as intended and that staff are engaging the control properly. Verify the system is in a state where table movement is allowed and no safety interlock, emergency stop, or system message is preventing motion. Expected outcome: eDrive commands are accepted when the system permits table movement. If operation was blocked by workflow state or improper control use, verify normal operation with MRI staff and stop.

steps.title: Verify Emergency Stop and Safety Interlock Status
steps.instructions: Check whether any emergency stop, table stop, or safety stop function has been activated. Confirm the system is not displaying a safety-related stop condition. Expected outcome: No emergency stop or safety stop is preventing table motion. If a stop was activated, follow site policy and MRI staff workflow before resetting. Retest only after the area is safe.

steps.title: Check for Console or System Messages
steps.instructions: Review the MRI console or operator display for table, eDrive, docking, or position-related fault messages. Record the exact wording, fault code, and when it appears. Expected outcome: Any active fault information is captured for service documentation. If the system identifies a table/docking fault that cannot be cleared through normal user-level workflow, remove the scanner from service and escalate.

steps.title: Test Basic Table Movement Without a Patient
steps.instructions: With MRI staff approval and no patient present, test basic table movement using normal controls. Observe whether the table: Moves smoothly; Starts and stops normally; Responds consistently to controls; Reaches expected positions; Produces unusual noise, binding, or vibration. Expected outcome: Table motion is smooth, controlled, and repeatable. If movement is abnormal, stop testing and remove the system from service.

steps.title: Check Table Position Recognition
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the scanner recognizes the table position after docking or movement. Watch for mismatch between actual table position and what the system reports. Expected outcome: The table position is recognized correctly by the system. If position recognition is incorrect or intermittent, suspect a sensor, encoder, interface, or internal table communication issue and escalate.

steps.title: Inspect Table Accessories and Load Conditions
steps.instructions: Remove unnecessary accessories, pads, coils, and nonessential items from the table. Confirm there is no excessive load or uneven weight distribution during testing. Expected outcome: The table moves normally under safe, unloaded conditions. If the issue only occurs with a specific coil, accessory, pad, or setup, inspect that item and remove it from use if damaged.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If MRI staff confirm it is appropriate, perform a normal system-level restart or table-related reset using approved site workflow. Do not perform service-level resets, software procedures, or internal adjustments unless trained and authorized. Expected outcome: A temporary control or communication issue may clear after a normal restart. If the fault returns, document it as recurring and escalate.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is External or Internal
steps.instructions: If the area is clear, the table is aligned, accessories are removed, controls are used correctly, and the fault persists, the issue is likely internal to the table, docking hardware, sensor system, drive system, or scanner communication path. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting stops before unnecessary disassembly or unsafe testing.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported that the Siemens MAGNETOM Sola dockable table would not complete docking and the eDrive movement control was intermittent.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no obstruction, accessories were removed, docking alignment was verified, and the table still failed to confirm docked status.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MRI system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the table/docking fault behavior, and escalated to Siemens MRI service for further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the issue occurred
Exact table or eDrive fault message
Whether table movement was slow, jerky, noisy, or completely inactive
Whether the docking indicator confirmed locked status
Whether the scanner recognized table position correctly
Accessories, coils, pads, or straps present during the fault
Floor or table path obstruction check
Emergency stop or safety stop status
Whether the fault was intermittent or repeatable

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Sola MRI System - Coil Positioning, Coil ID, or Coil Communication Issue
description: Troubleshooting coil detection, positioning, ID, or communication issues using external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the issue occurs during an active scan or patient setup, stop troubleshooting at the scanner until the patient is safe. Remove the patient from the bore if coil detection, scan setup, or communication errors prevent safe imaging. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a coil or scanner configuration with unresolved communication errors.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Coil Issue
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff what error or behavior was observed. Check whether the problem involves: Coil not detected; Incorrect coil shown; Coil ID mismatch; Coil element failure message; Coil communication error; Coil position not accepted; Scan cannot proceed after coil selection. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified before parts are swapped or the system is escalated.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Coil Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm the coil matches the selected exam, anatomy, and protocol. Make sure staff are not using a coil intended for a different body region, table setup, or scanner platform. Expected outcome: The coil and protocol are compatible. If the wrong coil or protocol was selected, correct the setup, repeat coil recognition, and stop if resolved.

steps.title: Check Coil Placement and Patient Positioning
steps.instructions: Inspect the physical coil setup. Confirm the coil is properly seated, aligned, closed, and positioned according to normal clinical use. Check for obvious misalignment, incomplete latching, excessive cable tension, or coil movement during table positioning. Expected outcome: The coil is positioned correctly and is not mechanically preventing recognition or scan setup.

steps.title: Inspect Coil Cable Routing
steps.instructions: Check that coil cables are routed cleanly and are not pinched, sharply bent, stretched, twisted, or trapped under the patient, pads, table, or accessories. Expected outcome: Coil cabling is not under stress and does not interfere with table movement or coil communication. If cable routing was corrected and the coil is recognized normally, return the system to clinical use after functional verification.

steps.title: Check Coil Connector Seating
steps.instructions: Inspect the external coil connector and scanner/table connection point. Reseat the coil connector fully and confirm it locks or seats as expected. Look for bent pins, damaged connector shells, looseness, contamination, cracked strain relief, or signs of liquid exposure. Expected outcome: The coil connection is secure and free of obvious damage. Do not force a damaged connector into place.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Coil Setup Through Normal Workflow
steps.instructions: Remove the coil from the setup, wait briefly, reconnect it, and allow the scanner software to detect it again through the normal operator workflow. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the coil correctly after reconnection. If the coil ID or communication issue clears, document the reseat and verify the coil can be used successfully.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Coil, If Available
steps.instructions: Use another compatible coil for the same scanner and exam type, if available. Do not substitute an incompatible coil just to bypass the error. Expected outcome: A known-good coil helps determine whether the issue follows the original coil or remains with the scanner/table connection. If the known-good coil works, remove the suspect coil from service for evaluation.

steps.title: Test the Suspect Coil on a Known-Good Setup, If Available
steps.instructions: If operationally appropriate and allowed by site process, test the suspect coil on a known-good compatible scanner or table connection. Expected outcome: The issue either follows the coil or remains isolated to the original scanner setup. If the same coil fails in multiple setups, suspect coil or coil cable failure.

steps.title: Check for Table or Docking-Related Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient table is docked, latched, and positioned normally. Check whether the issue appears only after table movement, table docking, or specific table positions. Expected outcome: The table connection and coil communication path are not being interrupted by poor docking or positioning.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after: Coil cleaning; Coil drop or impact; Room turnover; Software update; Table service; Protocol change; New coil installation; Liquid spill or contrast incident. Expected outcome: Recent events help identify whether the failure is likely accessory-related, workflow-related, or system-related.

steps.title: Check for Visible Coil Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the coil housing, padding, cable, connector, latches, and strain relief. Look for cracks, exposed wiring, loose sections, worn labels, fluid residue, or deformation. Expected outcome: No obvious physical damage is present. If damage is found, remove the coil from service and do not return it to patient use.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check
steps.instructions: With MRI staff assistance, attempt a basic coil recognition and setup check using the affected coil and a known appropriate protocol. Confirm whether the scanner identifies the coil correctly and allows setup to proceed. Expected outcome: Coil ID, coil element recognition, and communication are stable during setup. If the coil is recognized and the issue cannot be repeated, return to service only after documenting the test conditions.

steps.title: Escalate if Communication Errors Persist
steps.instructions: If the coil is correctly positioned, properly connected, externally undamaged, and still fails detection or communication checks, stop external troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The issue is treated as a possible internal coil, connector, table interface, or system communication fault requiring advanced service.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported that the MAGNETOM Sola would not recognize the selected body coil setup and displayed a coil communication issue during exam preparation.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the coil connector was not fully seated at the table connection point, and the coil ID was not detected until the connector was reseated.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the coil connector, verified proper coil recognition with MRI staff, confirmed scan setup could proceed, and returned the scanner to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact coil name or type involved
Whether the coil was detected incorrectly or not detected at all
Whether the issue followed the coil
Whether the issue occurred before or after table movement
Any recent coil drop, cleaning, spill, or service event

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Sola MRI System - Image Artifact, SliceAdjust, Distortion, or Failed QA Scan
description: Troubleshooting MRI image artifacts, SliceAdjust issues, distortion, or QA failures caused by setup, coils, environment, or system conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If image distortion, artifacts, or QA failure occurs during a patient scan, stop using the affected sequence or scanner workflow until image quality is verified. Remove the patient from the scan room if troubleshooting requires repeated scans, phantom work, coil changes, table movement, or system checks. Expected outcome: Patient care is not continued on a scanner producing unreliable diagnostic images.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Review what was reported by MRI staff: Image artifact, geometric distortion, SliceAdjust error, failed daily QA, failed phantom scan, failed coil QA, or issue on one sequence or all sequences. Expected outcome: The problem is clearly defined before troubleshooting begins. If the issue was a single poor patient image caused by motion, positioning, or anatomy-specific conditions, document the finding and stop if repeat QA is normal.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is Patient-Specific or System-Wide
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff whether the artifact appears on one patient only, on multiple patients, with one coil only, with one protocol only, during daily QA or phantom testing, or across multiple sequences and coils. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to patient setup, coil, protocol, or scanner-wide performance. If the issue only occurred with one patient and QA passes afterward, the scanner may not require removal from service.

steps.title: Verify the Scanner Is Safe and Stable for Testing
steps.instructions: Confirm there are no active system alarms, quench warnings, helium-related alerts, cooling alarms, abnormal equipment room conditions, or unusual sounds from the magnet room or equipment room. Expected outcome: No environmental or system safety condition is present before additional scanning is attempted. If there is an active magnet, cryogen, cooling, RF, gradient, or system fault, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Confirm Daily QA Procedure Was Performed Correctly
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct QA phantom, holder, coil, table position, landmark, protocol, and scan setup were used. Check that the phantom was positioned consistently and allowed to settle if it was recently moved or filled. Expected outcome: QA failure is not due to incorrect phantom setup or operator workflow. If correcting the phantom setup allows QA to pass, document the setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the QA Phantom and Accessories
steps.instructions: Check the phantom for visible damage, leaks, air bubbles, incorrect fluid level, cracked housing, missing holder pieces, or improper positioning aids. Expected outcome: The phantom and QA accessories are in usable condition. If the phantom is damaged or questionable, remove the phantom from use and repeat QA with an approved known-good phantom if available.

steps.title: Repeat QA Under Controlled Conditions
steps.instructions: Have MRI staff or qualified personnel repeat the QA scan using the standard daily QA workflow with no unnecessary changes. Expected outcome: QA either passes, fails consistently, or fails intermittently. If QA passes and no artifact is seen on follow-up testing, document the successful repeat test and return the scanner to normal workflow per site policy.

steps.title: Check Coil Selection and Coil Connection
steps.instructions: Verify the correct coil was selected for the test or clinical scan. Inspect the coil connection points, coil latches, table connectors, and visible cable strain areas for obvious damage, contamination, bent pins, poor seating, or loose connection. Expected outcome: The coil is properly connected and recognized by the system. If reseating or replacing the coil resolves the artifact or QA issue, document the coil-related cause and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Coil When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the artifact appears linked to one coil, repeat testing with another compatible known-good coil or body coil workflow if clinically and technically appropriate. Expected outcome: The issue follows the suspect coil or remains with the scanner. If the issue follows one coil, remove that coil from service and send it for evaluation.

steps.title: Look for External RF Interference Sources
steps.instructions: Confirm the scan room door is fully closed during testing. Check for unauthorized or unusual items in the MRI room, including non-MRI accessories, loose cables, portable electronics, unapproved monitoring equipment, damaged patient leads, or improperly routed coil cables. Expected outcome: External RF or setup-related causes are ruled out. If removing an item or correcting cable routing resolves the artifact, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check for Recent Environmental or Facility Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes near the MRI suite, such as construction, new electrical equipment, door service, RF door work, HVAC issues, power work, chiller service, or equipment room alarms. Expected outcome: A possible environmental cause is identified or ruled out. If the issue began after facility work or RF enclosure service, escalate to MRI service and facilities support.

steps.title: Check Table Positioning and Landmark Consistency
steps.instructions: Verify that the table moves smoothly, the landmark is set correctly, and the patient or phantom is positioned at isocenter as required for the protocol. Expected outcome: Distortion or QA failure is not caused by incorrect positioning. If correcting positioning resolves the issue, document the positioning error and stop.

steps.title: Review Sequence and Protocol Scope
steps.instructions: Determine whether the artifact or distortion occurs on one sequence only, one protocol only, one body region, all protocols, or all scans after SliceAdjust. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to protocol setup, application behavior, or scanner performance. If only one modified protocol is affected, compare it to a known-good Siemens or site-approved baseline protocol and escalate to the MRI applications team if needed.

steps.title: Check SliceAdjust Behavior
steps.instructions: If SliceAdjust fails or appears to contribute to distortion, confirm whether the issue occurs repeatedly on the same protocol, same coil, same anatomy, or same patient setup. Expected outcome: SliceAdjust failure is reproducible or isolated. If the issue is isolated to one protocol or setup, document the details and escalate to Siemens applications or MRI service if the site cannot safely correct it.

steps.title: Check for Obvious System Condition Changes
steps.instructions: Review whether the scanner recently had software update, coil replacement, gradient service, RF service, magnet service, table service, power event, emergency shutdown, or failed startup or reboot. Expected outcome: Recent service or events are identified as possible contributors. If the artifact began immediately after service or a power event, escalate to Siemens service with the timeline.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only If Site Policy Allows
steps.instructions: If the scanner has no active safety alarms and MRI operations allow it, perform a normal controlled system restart according to site procedure. Do not perform emergency shutdowns, magnet-related actions, or service-level procedures unless authorized. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication-related image issues clear after a normal restart. If the restart resolves the issue and QA passes, document the action and final status.

steps.title: Evaluate Final QA and Image Quality Status
steps.instructions: After any correction, repeat the appropriate QA or phantom scan and confirm with MRI staff that image quality is acceptable. Expected outcome: The scanner passes QA and no visible artifact, distortion, or SliceAdjust-related issue remains. If image quality cannot be verified, do not return the scanner to service.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported repeated image distortion and SliceAdjust-related artifact during MAGNETOM Sola QA and clinical scanning.
CCR.Cause: QA phantom setup and coil connections were checked; artifact remained present on repeat phantom testing, suggesting a scanner-level image quality issue.
CCR.Resolution: MAGNETOM Sola was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Siemens MRI service for image quality and system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurred during patient scan, phantom QA, or both
Exact artifact description and affected anatomy or protocol
Whether SliceAdjust failed, completed, or produced distortion
QA phantom used and setup verified
Coil used and whether a known-good coil was tested
Whether issue followed one coil or remained scanner-wide
Scan room door status during testing
Any recent construction, power events, service, or HVAC issues
Any system alarms, abnormal sounds, heat, smell, or equipment room alerts
Final device status: in service, limited use, or out of service

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Sola MRI System - Numaris X / Host / Reconstruction / Startup Fault
description: Troubleshooting Numaris X startup, host, reconstruction, or workstation faults before escalating MRI system service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is inside the bore and no exam is actively running before troubleshooting the MAGNETOM Sola. If the system fault occurs during patient care, stop the exam only when clinically safe, remove the patient from the scanner room, and have staff use another available MRI system or approved downtime process. Expected outcome: The patient is safe, the scan room is controlled, and troubleshooting is not performed during active scanning.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Fault and System State
steps.instructions: At the Numaris X console, document the exact message, error code, screen behavior, and when the fault occurs. Check whether the issue appears during: System startup; User login; Patient registration; Sequence loading; Image reconstruction; Transfer to PACS or post-processing; Full system shutdown or restart. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified as a startup, host, reconstruction, or workflow fault. Why it matters: Siemens Numaris X systems use system messages and message history to summarize software, scanner, and MR View&GO-related issues, so exact message capture is important before escalation.

steps.title: Check the Console and Workstation Power Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the operator console, monitors, keyboard, mouse, and any connected workstation components are powered normally. Look for: Blank monitor; Frozen login screen; No keyboard or mouse response; Monitor powered off while the host remains on; UPS or power strip alarm; Loose power cord at the console area. Expected outcome: The issue is not caused by a simple console display, input, or local power problem. If reseating an external power or display connection restores normal operation, confirm successful startup and stop.

steps.title: Check for Facility Power or UPS Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the MRI console room, equipment room, and associated workstation power sources are stable. Ask whether there was a recent power outage, emergency power transfer, electrical work, UPS alarm, or breaker event. Expected outcome: External power instability is either identified or ruled out. If power instability is suspected, notify Facilities and do not continue repeated restart attempts until the power source is verified.

steps.title: Review System Messages and Message History
steps.instructions: Open the system message area or message history if the system allows access. Document any messages related to: Host; MaRS / reconstruction; Database; Disk space; Network connection; Scanner communication; MR View&GO; Failed startup component; Previous incomplete shutdown. Expected outcome: The fault category is better defined for service documentation. Siemens identifies Numaris X system messages as being summarized in message history, with different message areas depending on single- or dual-monitor configuration.

steps.title: Ask Clinical Staff What Changed Before the Fault
steps.instructions: Check whether the issue followed: Software update; Protocol import; Power event; Network outage; PACS outage; Failed exam reconstruction; Full storage condition; User login change; Peripheral replacement; Recent vendor service visit. Expected outcome: A likely trigger is identified or ruled out. If the issue began after a known software update or vendor service action, document that clearly before escalation.

steps.title: Check Local Storage and Reconstruction Behavior
steps.instructions: If the system starts but reconstruction fails, verify whether the issue affects one exam, one protocol, or all studies. Ask the technologist whether: Raw data appears present; Images are delayed; Only advanced reconstructions fail; All sequences fail to reconstruct; The patient browser is accessible; Prior studies can be opened; New exams can be registered. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into a single-study reconstruction problem or a broader host/reconstruction system fault. If only one exam or sequence is affected, preserve the study data and escalate to Siemens support with the exam details.

steps.title: Check Network-Dependent Functions
steps.instructions: Determine whether the fault is truly host/reconstruction related or actually a network/archive issue. Verify whether the MAGNETOM Sola can: Send images to PACS; Query worklist; Reach configured destinations; Open local patient browser studies; Complete exams without transfer errors. Expected outcome: PACS, worklist, and network transfer issues are not mistaken for a host or reconstruction failure. If other imaging systems are also having PACS or worklist problems, escalate through imaging IT or network support rather than treating this as an isolated MRI scanner failure.

steps.title: Check External Network Connections in the Console Area
steps.instructions: Visually inspect accessible network connections at the console, workstation area, and approved external patch points. Do not enter restricted MRI equipment cabinets or disturb vendor-controlled internal connections. Expected outcome: No obvious disconnected external network cable or patch issue is found. If a loose external network connection is corrected and communication returns, verify normal workflow with clinical staff and stop.

steps.title: Perform an Orderly Restart Only if Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is active and the system is in a safe state, perform only the approved shutdown or restart process available to the site. Do not force power-cycle MRI cabinets, reconstruction hardware, or equipment room components unless directed by Siemens service or site policy. Expected outcome: The system restarts cleanly and startup errors clear. Siemens Healthineers has Numaris X shutdown/restart guidance for MAGNETOM systems, including Sola, and notes that the host computer and MaRS are included in the computer system shutdown process. If the restart clears the issue, confirm login, patient browser access, test workflow readiness, and image transfer status before returning the system to clinical use.

steps.title: Verify the System Reaches a Clinically Ready State
steps.instructions: After startup, confirm with MRI staff that the system reaches the normal home screen and is ready for standard workflow. Check for: No active critical messages; Successful user login; Patient browser available; Worklist available if used; Protocol selection available; No reconstruction queue backlog; No recurring host or MaRS fault; Successful image display or transfer if a test workflow is permitted. Expected outcome: The system is not returned to service based only on a cleared screen; basic operational readiness is confirmed.

steps.title: Do Not Clear, Delete, or Modify Study Data Without Approval
steps.instructions: If storage, reconstruction queue, or patient browser problems are suspected, do not delete exams, raw data, or reconstruction files unless directed by Siemens service and approved by site policy. Expected outcome: Patient data is protected and vendor support can review the fault condition.

steps.title: Escalate Repeated or Persistent Startup/Reconstruction Faults
steps.instructions: If the MAGNETOM Sola repeatedly fails startup, freezes, cannot access host functions, cannot reconstruct images, or shows recurring MaRS/reconstruction faults, remove it from clinical use. Expected outcome: The system is treated as a service-level MRI failure, not a routine user workflow issue.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported the Siemens MAGNETOM Sola would not complete Numaris X startup and displayed a host/reconstruction fault at the operator console.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering verified console power, monitor/input operation, external network connections, and system message history; fault returned after an orderly restart, indicating a likely internal host or reconstruction system issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MRI system from service, labeled it Out of Service, notified MRI leadership, and escalated to Siemens Healthineers service with documented fault messages and startup behavior.

helpfulDetails: Exact Numaris X error message or code
Whether fault occurred during startup, scan, reconstruction, or transfer
Host, MaRS, database, or reconstruction messages
Whether other imaging systems had network issues
Whether an orderly restart was attempted
Whether the fault returned after restart
Any unusual sound, heat, odor, or equipment room alarm

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Vida MRI System - BioMatrix sensor or patient respiratory signal fault
description: Troubleshooting BioMatrix sensor or respiratory signal issues caused by patient setup, sensor placement, accessories, or connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the respiratory signal is being used for gated imaging, pause the scan before troubleshooting. Confirm the patient is stable, comfortable, and able to continue the exam. If patient monitoring or respiratory gating is unreliable, do not continue gated sequences until the issue is corrected. Expected outcome: The patient is safe, and troubleshooting does not interfere with active imaging or monitoring.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Review the operator-reported issue at the MRI console. Confirm whether the problem is: No respiratory waveform; Weak or inconsistent respiratory signal; BioMatrix sensor not detected; Respiratory gating not triggering; Signal dropouts during scanning; Fault occurring only on certain exams or patient positions. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before accessories or hardware are suspected.

steps.title: Check Whether the Exam Requires Respiratory Gating
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the selected protocol or sequence depends on respiratory triggering or motion sensing. If the current scan does not require respiratory gating, verify whether the fault is informational or actually preventing the exam. Expected outcome: The issue is matched to the clinical workflow, and unnecessary troubleshooting is avoided.

steps.title: Verify Patient Positioning on the Table
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is positioned correctly on the MAGNETOM Vida table and within the expected sensing area for the BioMatrix system. Reposition the patient if they are shifted too far superior, inferior, or off-center relative to the sensor area. Expected outcome: Proper positioning improves signal detection if the issue was caused by poor patient alignment.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Patient Motion
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist whether the patient is moving, talking, coughing, shallow breathing, or unable to follow breathing instructions. Respiratory motion sensing may become unreliable if the breathing pattern is irregular or too shallow. Expected outcome: The signal stabilizes when patient motion and breathing pattern are controlled.

steps.title: Inspect External Respiratory Accessories
steps.instructions: If an external respiratory belt, cushion, sensor, or connection accessory is being used, inspect it for: Incorrect placement; Loose connection; Damaged cable jacket; Bent connector; Strain at the connector; Contamination or liquid exposure; Incorrect accessory for the scanner or protocol. Expected outcome: Any obvious accessory issue is corrected before internal scanner failure is suspected.

steps.title: Reseat the Respiratory Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Remove and reconnect the respiratory accessory or sensor cable at the approved external connection point. Make sure the connector is fully seated and not under tension when the table moves. Expected outcome: The respiratory signal returns if the fault was caused by a loose or partially seated connection. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the corrected connection.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Respiratory Accessory
steps.instructions: Swap the respiratory belt, cushion, or patient sensor accessory with a known-good compatible accessory. Do not use damaged or questionable MRI accessories on a patient. Expected outcome: If the signal returns with the known-good accessory, the original accessory should be removed from use and replaced.

steps.title: Inspect the Table Area for Obstruction or Misrouting
steps.instructions: Check that sensor cables are not pinched, stretched, trapped under pads, routed through moving table areas, or interfering with coils. Confirm table movement does not pull on the accessory connection. Expected outcome: Signal dropouts stop if the issue was caused by cable strain or movement-related interruption.

steps.title: Check Coil and Pad Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the receive coils, table pads, and patient positioning aids are installed correctly and not preventing proper respiratory signal detection. Make sure no unnecessary padding or accessory placement is blocking the expected sensing area. Expected outcome: Respiratory signal quality improves after the patient setup is corrected.

steps.title: Restart the Patient Setup Workflow
steps.instructions: Have the technologist exit the affected patient setup or gating workflow and re-enter the setup process. Reconfirm patient position, respiratory signal display, and gating readiness before restarting the scan. Expected outcome: A software workflow or setup recognition issue clears without deeper service intervention.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the issue persists and the room is not in active patient use, follow the site-approved process to restart the scanner console or affected workflow system. Do not interrupt active scanning or emergency workflow without clinical approval. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software recognition issues clear after restart.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Is Reproducible
steps.instructions: Test the BioMatrix or respiratory signal function using a controlled setup, known-good accessory, and standard patient positioning. Determine whether the issue occurs: With all patients; With one specific accessory; With one table position; Only during table movement; Only with certain coils or protocols; After system restart. Expected outcome: The fault is isolated to setup, accessory, workflow, or scanner-side behavior.

steps.title: Review System Messages and Error History
steps.instructions: Record any scanner messages, fault codes, timestamps, or respiratory signal warnings reported by the console. Do not clear useful diagnostic information before documentation or escalation. Expected outcome: Service escalation has enough detail to identify whether the fault is accessory-related, software-related, table-related, or internal.

steps.title: Remove Any Failed Accessory from Service
steps.instructions: If a respiratory accessory, belt, or cable is found damaged or unreliable, remove it from clinical use and label it appropriately. Replace it with a verified compatible accessory. Expected outcome: The scanner can continue clinical use only if respiratory sensing is reliable with a known-good accessory.

CCR.Complaint: MRI technologist reported that the Siemens MAGNETOM Vida was not detecting a usable BioMatrix respiratory signal during gated abdominal imaging.
CCR.Cause: Checked patient setup and found the respiratory accessory cable partially seated and under tension during table movement.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and rerouted the respiratory accessory cable, verified stable respiratory waveform with table movement, and returned the scanner to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact BioMatrix or respiratory fault message
Whether the signal was missing, weak, or intermittent
Whether table movement affected the signal

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Vida MRI System - Dockable table / eDrive / table communication fault
description: Troubleshooting dockable table, eDrive, docking, movement, or table communication faults before escalating for MRI service repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the table fault occurs with a patient on the table, stop table movement and follow the site’s MRI patient safety procedure. Remove the patient from the scanner only when it is safe to do so and with clinical/MRI staff assistance. Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff what happened and when the fault appeared. Check whether the issue involves: Table will not dock; Table will not undock; eDrive does not respond; Table movement is slow, interrupted, or unavailable; Scanner does not recognize the table; Error appears at the console during table positioning. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before further checks.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Table and Docking Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the table base, floor path, docking rails, table lock area, and scanner entrance for physical obstructions. Look for linens, pads, coils, straps, patient positioning aids, cables, or floor debris interfering with docking or movement. Expected outcome: Table path and docking area are clear. If obstruction removal resolves the issue, return the system to service after confirming normal table operation and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Table Is Properly Aligned for Docking
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is straight, centered, and positioned correctly at the magnet docking point. A dockable MRI table may fail to communicate or lock if it is mechanically misaligned. Expected outcome: Table docks smoothly and is recognized by the system. If proper alignment resolves the fault, document and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Contacts and External Connection Points
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the table docking interface for obvious damage, contamination, bent pins, loose covers, or foreign material. Do not probe internal contacts or disassemble the docking assembly. Expected outcome: Docking interface appears clean, intact, and mechanically normal. If contamination is safely removable using approved site practice, clean externally and retest.

steps.title: Check Table Locking and Latching Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the table physically locks into the docked position. Listen and feel for normal engagement without forcing the table. Expected outcome: Docking latch engages normally and the console recognizes the docked table. If the latch does not engage, do not force the table. Remove it from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify eDrive Controls Are Not Damaged or Stuck
steps.instructions: Inspect the eDrive controls, handles, buttons, and table movement controls for damage, fluid intrusion, stuck switches, or abnormal resistance. Expected outcome: Controls move normally and are not physically damaged. If a control is stuck, damaged, or contaminated, remove the table from service.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Table Stop Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm no emergency stop, table stop, or safety stop has been activated. Reset only according to approved site procedure and MRI department workflow. Expected outcome: Safety stop condition clears and table movement becomes available. If the fault clears after resetting a stop condition, verify normal movement before returning to service.

steps.title: Check Console Messages and System Status
steps.instructions: Review the MRI operator console for table, docking, eDrive, communication, or positioning messages. Record the exact wording of the error. Expected outcome: Error message is documented and helps determine whether the issue is table-side, docking-side, or system communication related.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If MRI staff confirms the scanner is not in use and no exam is active, perform an approved system/table restart workflow according to site procedure. Do not interrupt an active scan or patient exam. Expected outcome: Temporary communication faults may clear after a controlled restart. If the fault clears, verify docking, undocking, table movement, and console recognition before return to service.

steps.title: Try a Controlled Dock / Undock Test
steps.instructions: With no patient present, test normal docking and undocking. Confirm: Table moves smoothly; eDrive responds normally; Docking latch engages; Console recognizes table position; No new error appears. Expected outcome: Table communication and movement functions operate normally. If the table fails during testing, remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Recent Events or Environmental Causes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after: Table impact; Moving the table between rooms; Coil or accessory change; Floor cleaning; Fluid spill; Power interruption; Recent service activity. Expected outcome: Recent events may explain an external or mechanical cause.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Use if Table Position Is Unreliable
steps.instructions: If the table position, docking state, or communication status cannot be trusted, do not release the system for patient scanning. Expected outcome: MRI staff are not relying on a table that may fail during positioning or patient handling.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported the MAGNETOM Vida dockable table would not dock and the console displayed a table communication fault.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no obstruction in the table path, but the table was not consistently recognized after repeated dock/undock testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MRI system/table from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the console error, and escalated to Siemens service for table communication evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the table failed to dock, undock, move, or communicate
Whether the table was loaded or unloaded
Whether emergency stop or table stop was active
Visible damage to docking contacts or table base
Recent table impact, cleaning, spill, or power interruption
Whether a restart changed the fault

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Vida MRI System - Coil Not Detected, Coil Element Dropout, Or RF Chain Fault
description: Troubleshooting coil recognition, missing coil elements, poor signal, or suspected RF receive chain issues before escalating for MRI service repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop scanning if coil detection errors, missing coil elements, or image quality issues occur during an active exam. Remove the patient from the scanner when clinically safe and move the exam to another MRI system if patient care cannot wait. Expected outcome: The patient is not scanned with an unreliable coil or questionable RF receive path.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Coil or RF Issue
steps.instructions: Review the exact system message, coil name, coil element status, and exam step where the issue occurred. Check whether the problem is: Coil not detected at all; Specific coil element dropout; Poor SNR or image artifact; Intermittent coil recognition; RF chain or receiver-related system fault. Expected outcome: The failure is clearly identified before parts or accessories are blamed.

steps.title: Check the Coil Position and Proper Clinical Setup
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct coil is being used for the selected exam and that it is positioned correctly on the table or patient. Confirm the coil is seated flat, not twisted, and not mechanically stressed by patient positioning aids. Expected outcome: The coil is physically set up in a way that allows normal recognition and signal reception. If repositioning the coil resolves the issue, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Coil Connector and Docking Area
steps.instructions: Remove the coil from service briefly and inspect the coil connector, table port, and docking contact area. Look for bent pins, damaged contacts, debris, fluid residue, cracked plastic, loose strain relief, or signs of impact. Expected outcome: No obvious physical damage or contamination is found at the coil or table connection. If damage, fluid, or debris is found, remove the coil from service and escalate for cleaning, evaluation, or replacement.

steps.title: Disconnect and Reconnect the Coil
steps.instructions: With the patient off the table, disconnect the coil and reconnect it firmly using normal handling technique. Confirm that the system recognizes the coil and displays the expected coil name and elements. Expected outcome: A loose or incomplete connection is corrected. If the coil is detected normally after reseating, perform a quick verification scan or approved image quality check before returning to use.

steps.title: Test the Coil on a Different Compatible Port or Setup, If Available
steps.instructions: If the MAGNETOM Vida setup allows alternate positioning or another compatible table connection, test the same coil in a known-good configuration. Expected outcome: The issue either follows the coil or changes with the connection point. If the issue follows the coil, suspect the coil or coil cable. If the issue stays with one table connection, suspect the system-side receive path or connector area.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Coil
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible Siemens MRI coil and connect it to the same table position or coil port. Confirm whether the system detects the known-good coil and whether all expected elements are available. Expected outcome: A known-good coil helps separate a coil issue from a scanner-side RF chain issue. If the known-good coil works normally, remove the suspect coil from service for repair or replacement evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Coil Cable Stress or Intermittent Behavior
steps.instructions: Gently inspect the coil cable and strain relief areas without bending or twisting aggressively. Observe whether detection drops out when the cable is moved slightly during normal handling. Expected outcome: The coil remains detected and stable during normal movement. If detection is intermittent, remove the coil from service. Do not continue using a coil with a suspected internal cable or connector failure.

steps.title: Review Coil Element Status
steps.instructions: Check the system coil element display or scan setup screen for disabled, missing, or failed elements. Confirm whether the dropout is limited to one element, multiple elements, or an entire coil section. Expected outcome: The pattern helps identify whether the issue is localized to the coil or broader RF receive chain. If only one coil consistently shows element dropout, suspect the coil. If multiple coils show similar dropout on the same system path, suspect scanner-side RF hardware or table interface.

steps.title: Check Recent System Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after: Coil drop or impact; Table service; MRI software update; Room shutdown or power event; Coil relocation from another scanner; Recent cleaning or fluid exposure. Expected outcome: A recent change may point toward the likely cause.

steps.title: Restart the MRI System Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the issue appears to be a communication or recognition error and no patient is in the scanner, follow the site-approved restart process. After restart, reconnect the coil and confirm detection. Expected outcome: A temporary software or communication state is cleared. If the fault returns after restart, continue troubleshooting and do not repeatedly reboot as a substitute for repair.

steps.title: Perform an Approved Phantom or Image Quality Check
steps.instructions: If the coil is detected but image quality is questionable, perform a site-approved phantom scan or service-level image quality verification. Look for low signal, banding, missing anatomy coverage, ghosting, or consistent artifact in the same coil region. Expected outcome: The coil either produces acceptable image quality or shows repeatable signal problems. If image quality remains abnormal, remove the coil or scanner from clinical use depending on whether the failure follows the coil.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Scanner or Coil Should Be Removed From Service
steps.instructions: If one coil fails but other coils work normally, remove only the affected coil from service. If multiple coils fail on the same port, table connection, or RF path, remove the MRI system from service until Siemens service or qualified MRI support can evaluate it. Expected outcome: Clinical use is limited only when appropriate, but unsafe or unreliable scanning is prevented.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported that the MAGNETOM Vida did not detect the head/neck coil and displayed a coil recognition fault before the exam could begin.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the coil intermittently lost detection when the connector strain relief was moved during normal handling, while a known-good coil worked on the same table connection.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the affected coil from service, labeled it Out of Service, verified scanner operation with a known-good coil, and escalated the coil for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact coil name and part description
Coil serial number or asset tag
Table port or connection location used
Whether the coil was not detected or only certain elements failed
Whether the issue followed the coil
Any recent coil drop, impact, cleaning, or fluid exposure
Error message or RF chain fault code
Image artifact or low signal description
Phantom or image quality check result

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Vida MRI System - Image Artifact, RF Noise, Distortion, or Failed QA Scan
description: Troubleshooting MRI image artifacts, RF noise, distortion, or QA scan failures caused by external, setup, coil, room, or environmental issues on Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Vida MRI systems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the artifact or distortion is discovered during an active patient exam, do not continue scanning until image quality is clinically acceptable. Move the patient off the table if troubleshooting requires coil changes, table movement checks, room entry, or repeated test scans. Expected outcome: Patient care is not continued on a system producing unreliable image quality.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Artifact or QA Failure
steps.instructions: Review the reported image artifact, failed QA result, or technologist description. Identify whether the issue appears as: RF noise or zipper artifact; Geometric distortion; Shading or signal dropout; Ghosting or motion-like artifact; Coil element dropout; Failed phantom QA; Intermittent image noise. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Affects All Scans or One Exam Type
steps.instructions: Ask whether the artifact appears on all patients, one specific coil, one protocol, one sequence, or only during QA. Run a simple approved phantom or QA scan if appropriate and safe. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to system-wide, coil-specific, protocol-specific, or QA setup-related behavior.

steps.title: Verify the MRI Room Is Clear of Unauthorized Equipment
