Room control panel not communicating
Footswitch, hand control, or joystick issue
System does not recognize table position
Movement stops during positioning
Intermittent communication between room controls and system

Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check for Active Collision or Motion Inhibit Warnings
steps.instructions:
Review the ARTIS Q operator display, room control indicators, and any system messages related to collision protection, table position, movement inhibit, or safety interlock.

Do not bypass or ignore collision-related warnings.

Expected outcome: A valid safety inhibit is identified before controls or hardware are suspected.

If a collision protection warning is active, correct any external obstruction first before attempting movement again.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient and Equipment Area for Obstructions
steps.instructions:
Check around the table, base, floor rails if applicable, C-arm travel path, detector, tube housing, monitor suspension, IV poles, anesthesia equipment, leads, cables, and accessory carts.

Remove any object that could interfere with table or system movement.

Expected outcome: The movement path is clear and collision protection is not being triggered by equipment placement.

If clearing the obstruction restores normal movement, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify Table Locks, Brakes, and Positioning State
steps.instructions:
Confirm the table is properly positioned, not physically blocked, and not left in an incomplete movement or locked state.

Check whether tabletop float, longitudinal movement, height movement, tilt, or cradle motion is affected.

Expected outcome: The table is in a valid operating state and not mechanically restricted by an external condition.

steps.title: Check Table Controls and Room Control Inputs
steps.instructions:
Test the affected control only when the patient is safe and the movement path is clear.

Compare operation from available control points, such as:

Table-side control module
Room control panel
Control room console
Footswitch or hand control if involved
Emergency stop reset state if applicable

Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one control input, one control location, or the entire motion/control system.

If one control works but another does not, the fault may be isolated to that control device, cable, connector, or communication path.

steps.title: Verify Emergency Stop and Safety Stop Status
steps.instructions:
Confirm no emergency stop, safety stop, or motion inhibit button is engaged at the table, room control area, or nearby system control location.

Reset only according to site procedure and only after confirming it is safe to do so.

Expected outcome: A simple safety stop condition is ruled out before escalating the issue.

If resetting the safety stop restores function, document the cause and verify normal control operation.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions:
Look for loose, damaged, pinched, contaminated, or stressed cables connected to table controls, room controls, footswitches, hand controls, or accessory control modules.

Do not open covers or disturb internal wiring.

Expected outcome: External cable or connector problems are identified without invasive repair.

If reseating an external accessory connector restores operation, perform a safe functional check and document the corrective action.

steps.title: Check for Fluid, Damage, or Contamination
steps.instructions:
Inspect the control modules, table-side controls, footswitches, room panels, and nearby cable areas for spilled contrast, saline, blood, cleaning fluid, cracked housings, sticky buttons, or damaged keypads.

Expected outcome: Control input failure caused by contamination or physical damage is identified.

If fluid intrusion or damaged controls are found, remove the system or affected accessory from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Not Procedure Setup Related
steps.instructions:
Verify that the ARTIS Q is in the correct operating mode and not waiting for a specific system state, table position, C-arm position, parking condition, or room configuration before allowing movement.

Ask staff whether the issue began after a room setup change, table accessory installation, system restart, or procedure workflow change.

Expected outcome: A workflow or setup condition is ruled out before assuming a communication fault.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Reset Only If Safe
steps.instructions:
If no patient is on the table and the procedure is not active, follow the site-approved restart or reset process for the angiography system and room controls.

Avoid repeated resets if the issue returns immediately.

Expected outcome: A temporary communication lockup clears, or the fault repeats and supports escalation.

If the issue resolves after one safe reset, verify movement, collision protection behavior, and room control response before returning the system to service.

steps.title: Verify Function After Any Correction
steps.instructions:
Test table movement, control response, collision protection status, and room control communication through a limited, controlled functional check.

Confirm there are no active warnings and that all movement stops respond normally.

Expected outcome: The system operates predictably and is safe for clinical use.

If any movement remains intermittent, delayed, blocked, or unpredictable, do not return the room to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the ARTIS Q table controls were intermittently unresponsive and a collision protection warning appeared during room setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found an IV pole and accessory cable positioned in the table and C-arm movement path, triggering motion inhibit behavior.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared the obstruction, verified table-side and room control response, confirmed no active collision warnings, and returned the ARTIS Q room to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient on table at time of fault
Emergency stop or safety stop status

title: Siemens Healthineers ARTIS Q Series Angiography System - Acquisition Workstation, 3D Workflow, DICOM, or Network Transfer Failure
description: Troubleshooting acquisition workstation, 3D workflow, DICOM send, PACS, archive, or network transfer problems using safe external checks.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the ARTIS Q system is actively being used for a patient procedure. If the issue occurs during an active case, do not restart the workstation, network equipment, or imaging system unless directed by the clinical team and site procedure. Expected outcome: Patient care is not interrupted by troubleshooting activity. If imaging, fluoro, or image review is required for an active procedure, clinical staff should continue using the safest available workflow or move to downtime procedure.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what failed and when it failed. Determine whether the issue involves: Acquisition workstation frozen or slow; 3D reconstruction not launching; 3D spin acquired but not processing; Images not transferring to PACS; DICOM send failure; Modality worklist not populating; Images stuck in local queue; Network destination unavailable; Archive or export failure. Expected outcome: The failure is clearly separated into workstation, 3D workflow, DICOM, or network transfer behavior.

steps.title: Check for Active Procedure or Unsaved Image Data
steps.instructions: Before any restart, confirm whether there are unsaved images, incomplete acquisitions, active 3D reconstructions, or pending transfers. Ask the technologist or physician whether images have been reviewed, saved, or exported. Expected outcome: Patient images and procedural data are protected before troubleshooting continues. If there is any concern about losing clinical data, stop and escalate to Siemens support or the site imaging/PACS team.

steps.title: Verify Workstation Power and Display Status
steps.instructions: Check that the acquisition workstation, review monitors, keyboard, mouse, and related display components are powered on. Confirm there are no blank displays, loose monitor cables, disconnected keyboard/mouse cables, or powered-off peripherals. Expected outcome: The workstation interface is available and not simply affected by a display or peripheral issue. If reconnecting or powering an external peripheral restores control, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Workstation Lockup
steps.instructions: Observe whether the mouse moves, keyboard responds, system clock updates, and applications can be selected. Do not force a reboot during a patient case unless approved through the clinical escalation process. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either a full workstation lockup or an application-specific problem.

steps.title: Confirm the Imaging System Is Otherwise Functional
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether fluoro, acquisition, table control, C-arm movement, and image display are functioning normally. If acquisition itself is failing, this may not be a network-only problem. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to image processing or transfer instead of the full angiography system.

steps.title: Check the 3D Workflow Step Where the Failure Occurs
steps.instructions: If the issue involves 3D imaging, identify the exact failure point: 3D protocol cannot be selected; Rotational acquisition does not complete; Images acquire but reconstruction does not start; Reconstruction starts but fails; 3D image cannot be displayed; 3D image cannot be sent to PACS or workstation. Expected outcome: The failing step is documented clearly enough for service or applications support.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Study Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct patient, accession number, study, and procedure are selected. A wrong or incomplete patient context can cause DICOM routing, archive, or worklist issues. Expected outcome: The image data is associated with the correct patient and exam. If correcting the study selection allows transfer, verify receipt in PACS and stop.

steps.title: Check Modality Worklist Availability
steps.instructions: If worklist is not populating, confirm whether other imaging systems in the department can access worklist. Ask whether registration, RIS, Epic, or scheduling systems are having downtime. Expected outcome: The issue is separated from a hospital-wide worklist or interface outage. If multiple systems are affected, escalate to imaging IT or PACS support rather than treating the ARTIS Q as failed.

steps.title: Review DICOM Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct PACS, archive, 3D workstation, or external destination is selected. Look for operator selection errors, wrong destination, old destination, or an inactive send target. Expected outcome: Images are being sent to the intended DICOM destination. If selecting the correct destination restores transfer, confirm image receipt and stop.

steps.title: Check the Local Send Queue
steps.instructions: Review whether images are pending, failed, retrying, or stuck in the local transfer queue. Note the number of studies affected and whether only one study or all studies are failing. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as a queue issue, single-study issue, or general transfer failure.

steps.title: Confirm Network Cable and Jack Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible network cables at the workstation, wall jack, equipment cabinet, or network drop if safely reachable. Do not move or unplug cables inside restricted system cabinets unless authorized and trained. Expected outcome: No obvious disconnected, damaged, loose, or mislabeled network cable is found. If a clearly loose external network cable is reseated and transfer resumes, confirm operation and document the finding.

steps.title: Check for Network or PACS Outage
steps.instructions: Contact imaging IT, PACS, or the hospital help desk to ask whether there is an active network, PACS, DICOM, interface, or storage outage. Expected outcome: Site-wide issues are ruled out before suspecting ARTIS Q hardware or software failure.

steps.title: Compare With Another Imaging Modality
steps.instructions: Ask whether CT, MRI, X-ray, ultrasound, or another angiography room can send images to the same destination. Expected outcome: A broader DICOM or PACS issue is identified if multiple modalities are affected.

steps.title: Check for Firewall, IP, or Destination Changes
steps.instructions: Ask imaging IT whether any recent changes were made to: Network switch ports; VLANs; IP address assignments; PACS AE title; DICOM port; Firewall rules; Server replacement; Worklist configuration. Expected outcome: Recent infrastructure changes are considered before replacing or servicing the angiography workstation.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Resend
steps.instructions: If patient care is not active and the study is safely stored locally, attempt a resend to the correct DICOM destination per site workflow. Expected outcome: The study either transfers successfully or fails again with the same behavior. If resend works, verify receipt in PACS and stop.

steps.title: Check Whether the Failure Is Study-Specific
steps.instructions: Determine whether only one patient study fails or whether all new studies fail. A single failed study may involve patient demographics, accession mismatch, corrupted series, or unsupported image object. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is focused on either one study or the entire transfer function.

steps.title: Verify External Export Media Only If Applicable
steps.instructions: If staff report USB, CD/DVD, or external export failure, confirm the media is approved, available, and not full or damaged. Do not connect unauthorized USB devices to the ARTIS Q system. Expected outcome: External media problems are ruled out without creating cybersecurity risk.

steps.title: Perform Only Approved Restarts When Safe
steps.instructions: If no patient is active, no unsaved data is at risk, and staff approve downtime, perform only the standard user-level restart or application restart allowed by site policy. Do not perform service-level resets, configuration changes, or operating system changes without Siemens or qualified support direction. Expected outcome: Temporary workstation or application faults may clear without deeper intervention. If the workstation, 3D workflow, or DICOM transfer returns to normal, verify image acquisition, 3D processing, and transfer before returning to use.

steps.title: Document Exact Error Messages
steps.instructions: Record the exact workstation message, DICOM error, failed destination, time of failure, patient/study impact, and whether images remained locally available. Expected outcome: Escalation has enough detail for Siemens, PACS, or imaging IT to act efficiently.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens ARTIS Q Series acquired images but DICOM transfer to PACS failed and studies remained in the local send queue.
CCR.Cause: Verified correct patient selection and DICOM destination, found no loose external network cabling, and confirmed imaging IT was investigating a PACS destination outage affecting multiple modalities.
CCR.Resolution: ARTIS Q remained clinically limited until PACS service was restored; transfer was reattempted successfully and image receipt in PACS was confirmed with radiology staff.

helpfulDetails: Procedure active or system idle when reported
Exact workstation, 3D, DICOM, or transfer message
Whether fluoro and acquisition still worked
Whether 3D reconstruction failed before or after acquisition
Other modalities affected or not affected

title: Siemens Healthineers MAMMOMAT Inspiration Mammography System - Compression paddle, compression force, or release fault
description: Troubleshooting compression paddle detection, compression movement, compression force, or release problems using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is currently compressed or positioned for imaging before troubleshooting. If the fault occurs during an active exam and the patient is under compression, immediately involve mammography staff and use the approved compression release method according to site policy. Expected outcome: The patient is safely released before equipment troubleshooting continues. If the compression system cannot release normally, stop troubleshooting and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Compression Issue
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: Compression paddle not recognized; Compression paddle will not lock in place; Compression movement will not start; Compression force appears incorrect; Compression releases unexpectedly; Compression will not release; Error appears during positioning, exposure setup, or startup. Expected outcome: The fault type is clearly identified before accessories or the compression mechanism are suspected.

steps.title: Inspect the Compression Paddle
steps.instructions: Remove the paddle only if the system is idle and safe to access. Check the paddle for cracks, warped plastic, damaged locking tabs, contamination, or anything preventing full seating. Expected outcome: The paddle is physically intact and can seat properly. If the paddle is cracked, damaged, or does not latch securely, remove it from service and test with an approved spare paddle if available.

steps.title: Reseat the Compression Paddle
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the paddle fully into the receptor or paddle holder. Confirm it clicks, locks, or seats as expected and does not feel loose. Expected outcome: The system detects the paddle and allows normal compression setup. If reseating resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Paddle
steps.instructions: If available, test with another approved MAMMOMAT Inspiration-compatible compression paddle. Use the same exam setup only if safe and appropriate. Expected outcome: A known-good paddle helps determine whether the issue follows the accessory or remains with the system. If the fault disappears with another paddle, remove the suspect paddle from service.

steps.title: Check for Debris or Obstruction at the Paddle Mount
steps.instructions: Inspect the paddle receiver, locking area, detector surface area, and compression mount for debris, tape, labels, cleaning residue, or small objects. Do not force the paddle into place. Expected outcome: The paddle mount is clean and unobstructed. If debris is found and removed, retest paddle detection and compression movement.

steps.title: Verify Compression Controls Are Not Stuck or Damaged
steps.instructions: Inspect the hand controls, foot controls, gantry controls, and release controls used by staff. Check for stuck buttons, damaged cords, crushed footswitch cables, or controls left pressed under equipment. Expected outcome: No external control is continuously commanding compression, release, or preventing movement. If a damaged control or cable is found, remove the device from use and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Check Emergency Release and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: Confirm that emergency release controls are not engaged, physically damaged, or stuck. Make sure staff did not activate a release function that requires a system reset before normal compression operation returns. Expected outcome: Safety controls are in normal operating condition and not preventing compression. If an emergency release control appears faulty, remove the system from clinical use.

steps.title: Check System Messages at the Acquisition Workstation
steps.instructions: Review the displayed error or warning message. Record the exact wording, code if shown, and whether the fault appears during: System startup; Paddle installation; Compression movement; Exposure preparation; Automatic decompression; Manual release. Expected outcome: The failure point is documented clearly for service evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Normal System Startup State
steps.instructions: Confirm the system completed startup without other gantry, detector, exposure, or workstation faults. A broader system startup problem may affect compression control or paddle recognition. Expected outcome: Compression troubleshooting is not masking a larger mammography system fault. If multiple unrelated system faults are present, stop and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Binding or Unusual Movement
steps.instructions: With no patient present, observe compression movement only through normal operator controls. Look for hesitation, uneven travel, abnormal noise, scraping, vibration, sudden release, or inconsistent movement. Do not force movement or attempt internal adjustment. Expected outcome: Compression movement is smooth, controlled, and predictable. If movement is abnormal, remove the system from service.

steps.title: Verify Reported Force Concern Safely
steps.instructions: If staff report excessive, weak, or inconsistent compression force, do not attempt patient testing. Confirm whether the concern is based on displayed force, patient complaint, failed QC, or inconsistent technologist experience. Expected outcome: The force concern is documented without placing a patient at risk. If compression force is questionable, remove the system from clinical use until evaluated.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Is Paddle-Specific or Reproducible
steps.instructions: Compare behavior using different approved paddles, if available. Note whether the issue occurs with all paddles, one paddle size, one exam mode, or one operator control location. Expected outcome: The problem pattern is narrowed to an accessory, control, or likely internal compression system issue.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check Only if Site Policy Allows
steps.instructions: With no patient present, confirm paddle detection, compression movement, hold, and release using approved clinical controls. Do not perform calibration, internal adjustment, or service-level compression force testing unless qualified and authorized. Expected outcome: The system either returns to normal operation or the fault is confirmed. If the compression fault repeats, stop troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that the MAMMOMAT Inspiration did not recognize the compression paddle during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the paddle not fully seated in the paddle holder; no damage was seen on the paddle or mount.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the compression paddle, verified paddle recognition and normal compression/release operation with no patient present, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient was safely released or no patient was present
Exact compression fault message or code
Paddle inspected for cracks or damaged locking tabs
Paddle mount checked for debris or obstruction
Hand controls and foot controls inspected
Any abnormal noise, vibration, or binding

title: Siemens Healthineers MAMMOMAT Inspiration Mammography System - Detector Calibration, Flat-Field, or Image Receptor Fault
description: Troubleshooting detector calibration, flat-field, image receptor, artifact, or acquisition faults using safe external checks before service escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is positioned at the mammography system before troubleshooting detector, calibration, or image receptor faults. If the issue occurs during a patient exam, stop use of the affected unit and have clinical staff move the patient to another approved imaging workflow if needed. Expected outcome: No patient is exposed using a system with unresolved detector or image quality concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask mammography staff what occurred and when the fault appeared. Check whether the issue involves: Detector calibration failure; Flat-field correction failure; Image receptor not ready; Detector communication fault; Repeated image artifact; Failed quality control image; Image not acquired or not displayed; Error after startup, detector warm-up, or compression positioning. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or internal detector issues are suspected.

steps.title: Check for Active Patient, Compression, or Exposure Interlocks
steps.instructions: Verify that the system is not waiting on a normal safety interlock, such as compression position, gantry position, exposure readiness, door status, or acquisition workstation readiness. Expected outcome: The detector fault is not actually caused by a normal mammography exposure interlock. If correcting the interlock clears the fault, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify System Startup and Detector Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm the MAMMOMAT Inspiration completed normal startup and that the acquisition workstation is fully loaded. Allow adequate time for detector initialization or warm-up if the unit was just powered on. Expected outcome: The detector and acquisition system reach a ready state before calibration or imaging is attempted. If the fault clears after proper startup and readiness, return the system to clinical staff according to site policy and stop.

steps.title: Check Power and Room Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the system is connected to the proper dedicated power source and that there are no signs of recent power interruption. Check the room for abnormal temperature, humidity, HVAC failure, or visible environmental concerns around the detector and workstation. Expected outcome: External power or room-condition problems are ruled out before suspecting detector failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Image Receptor Area Externally
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the image receptor surface, detector cover, bucky area, and compression area for obvious obstruction, contamination, fluid, physical damage, or foreign material. Do not remove covers or open internal detector assemblies. Expected outcome: No external obstruction or contamination is interfering with detector response or flat-field imaging. If contamination or obstruction is found and safely corrected, repeat the appropriate user-level check with mammography staff.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Clinical Setup
steps.instructions: Verify staff are using the expected imaging mode, receptor configuration, paddle, compression plate, and exam protocol. Incorrect setup can create apparent detector or flat-field problems if the system expects a different acquisition condition. Expected outcome: The system setup matches the intended mammography exam or QC procedure.

steps.title: Review Recent QC or Calibration Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether the fault appeared after routine QC, detector calibration, flat-field correction, software restart, power outage, service visit, or failed phantom image. Expected outcome: The timing helps determine whether the issue is isolated, environmental, setup-related, or recurring.

steps.title: Attempt Only Approved User-Level Calibration or QC Workflow
steps.instructions: If site policy and Siemens-provided instructions allow clinical staff or qualified users to perform a detector calibration, flat-field, or QC acquisition, have the appropriate trained user repeat the approved workflow. Clinical Engineering should observe and document the result, not bypass calibration safeguards. Expected outcome: The approved calibration or QC process either completes successfully or reproduces the fault. If calibration completes and image quality/QC passes, document the resolution and stop.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Artifacts or Receptor Pattern Issues
steps.instructions: Review whether artifacts appear in the same location across images or QC exposures. A repeated fixed pattern, line artifact, dead area, unusual shading, or persistent flat-field failure may indicate a detector, image receptor, correction, or acquisition chain issue. Expected outcome: The artifact pattern is identified as either setup-related or likely receptor-related.

steps.title: Verify Workstation and Network Display Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the image is acquired but not displayed, acquired but not transferred, or not acquired at all. Check the acquisition workstation for frozen software, storage warnings, login issues, or image transfer errors. Expected outcome: A workstation or workflow issue is separated from a true detector acquisition fault.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If there is no patient involved, no exposure in progress, and site policy allows it, perform a normal system shutdown and restart. Do not perform repeated resets if the same detector or flat-field fault immediately returns. Expected outcome: Temporary software or initialization faults clear after one controlled restart. If the fault clears and QC/image checks pass, document the restart and final status, then stop.

steps.title: Remove From Clinical Use if Image Quality Is Questionable
steps.instructions: If the detector fault persists, calibration fails again, QC fails, or image quality cannot be verified, do not return the unit to patient use. Expected outcome: The mammography system remains unavailable for clinical imaging until evaluated and cleared.

CCR.Complaint: Mammography staff reported that the Siemens MAMMOMAT Inspiration failed detector flat-field calibration and would not allow normal image acquisition.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering verified proper startup, checked the image receptor area, confirmed correct setup, and observed the flat-field fault repeat during approved QC workflow.
CCR.Resolution: Unit was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Siemens service for detector/image receptor evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector, flat-field, or image receptor error message
Whether the fault occurred during startup, QC, calibration, or patient imaging
Whether the image receptor reached ready status
Recent power outage or system restart
Paddle, compression plate, or receptor setup used
Whether artifacts repeat in the same image location
Whether QC or phantom image passed or failed
Any visible receptor damage, contamination, or obstruction

title: Siemens Healthineers MAMMOMAT Inspiration Mammography System - Exposure abort, tube warmup, AEC, or X-ray inhibit issue
description: Troubleshooting exposure aborts, tube warmup interruptions, AEC problems, or X-ray inhibit conditions caused by setup, safety, accessory, or workflow issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is not compressed or positioned for imaging before troubleshooting. If the exposure abort or inhibit occurs during an active exam, have clinical staff release compression and move the patient away from the system before checks continue. Expected outcome: The patient is no longer dependent on a system with unreliable exposure control.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Exposure abort during acquisition; X-ray inhibit before exposure; Tube warmup not completing; AEC not detecting correctly; No exposure after pressing the exposure switch; System message related to compression, detector, paddle, door, or safety interlock. Expected outcome: The symptom is clearly identified before assuming an X-ray generator or tube issue.

steps.title: Check for Active System Messages or Inhibit Conditions
steps.instructions: Review the operator console for warnings, inhibit messages, ready status, tube warmup prompts, or exposure errors. Document the exact wording, code, and workflow step where the message appears. Expected outcome: The system identifies whether the inhibit is related to setup, safety, tube readiness, detector readiness, or exposure control. If the message clears after correcting the displayed condition, perform an approved test exposure or clinical workflow check and stop.

steps.title: Verify the System Is Fully Ready for Exposure
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is powered on, booted normally, and not still initializing. Verify the workstation, gantry, detector, and generator status indicators show ready or normal operation. Expected outcome: Exposure is not being attempted while the system is still in startup, standby, or initialization.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: Inspect the emergency stop buttons and confirm none are engaged. Check that the exposure switch is intact, connected, and not physically damaged. Verify staff are using the correct exposure switch and following the normal press-and-hold exposure process. Expected outcome: The exposure chain is not being interrupted by an engaged safety stop or faulty external control. If releasing an engaged emergency stop resolves the issue and the system returns to ready status, document and stop.

steps.title: Verify Room Door and Radiation Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Confirm the exam room door is fully closed if the site uses a door interlock. Check for any visible door interlock warning, room X-ray enable indicator, or control booth issue. Expected outcome: The system is not inhibiting exposure due to radiation safety interlock status.

steps.title: Confirm Compression and Paddle Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the compression paddle is installed correctly and recognized by the system. Check that compression is applied appropriately for the selected view and that there are no paddle seating, latch, or accessory recognition warnings. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the correct compression accessory and does not inhibit exposure due to incomplete setup.

steps.title: Check Detector and Bucky Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm the detector area is clear, the bucky is seated correctly, and any selected imaging accessory is properly installed. Look for detector readiness, detector communication, grid, or bucky position messages. Expected outcome: The detector path is ready to receive the exposure. If reseating the external accessory clears the ready status issue, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Review AEC Selection and Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected imaging mode, AEC field, view, and breast positioning are appropriate for the exam. AEC problems may occur if tissue is not positioned over the selected sensing area or if the system setup does not match the clinical view. Expected outcome: The AEC field is properly covered and the system can calculate exposure correctly.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Positioning or Collimation Problems
steps.instructions: Confirm the gantry angle, paddle, detector, and selected view match the exam being performed. Make sure there is no unintended object in the field, incorrect accessory, or setup mismatch that could trigger exposure abort or AEC failure. Expected outcome: The system setup matches the selected acquisition workflow.

steps.title: Confirm Tube Warmup Requirements
steps.instructions: If the system requests tube warmup, allow the warmup process to complete according to the on-screen workflow. Do not bypass warmup prompts or repeatedly attempt clinical exposures while warmup is incomplete. Expected outcome: The X-ray tube reaches the required operating condition before imaging. If warmup completes and exposure operation returns to normal, document and stop.

steps.title: Check for Overheat or Cooling Delay Messages
steps.instructions: Look for tube heat, generator cooling, or exposure delay messages. Ask whether repeated exposures, QC images, or recent high-use imaging occurred before the fault. Expected outcome: Temporary thermal limits are recognized and not mistaken for hardware failure. If the system returns to ready after the indicated cooling delay and passes normal function checks, document and stop.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Visible Connections
steps.instructions: Check accessible external cables at the workstation, exposure switch, gantry area, and detector interface for loose connections, strain, or obvious damage. Do not remove covers or access internal generator, tube head, or high-voltage components. Expected outcome: External connection issues are ruled out without unsafe disassembly.

steps.title: Restart Only When Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is involved and the system is not in the middle of an exam, perform a controlled restart according to site policy. After restart, check whether the same exposure abort, tube warmup, AEC, or inhibit message returns. Expected outcome: A temporary software or readiness state clears, or the fault repeats and supports escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: If the system returns to ready status, have qualified mammography staff perform an approved non-patient test exposure or QC check according to department procedure. Verify exposure completes, no inhibit appears, and image acquisition is successful. Expected outcome: The system demonstrates safe exposure readiness before returning to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Siemens MAMMOMAT Inspiration aborted exposure during mammography acquisition and displayed an X-ray inhibit message.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the compression paddle was not fully seated and the system did not show exposure-ready status.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the paddle, confirmed system ready status, verified successful non-patient test exposure with mammography staff, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact exposure abort or inhibit message
Whether issue occurred before exposure or during acquisition
Any overheat or cooling delay message

title: Siemens Healthineers MAMMOMAT Inspiration Mammography System - Image artifact, failed phantom QC, or poor image quality
description: Troubleshooting image artifacts, failed phantom QC, poor contrast, noise, or image quality concerns using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is positioned at the mammography system before troubleshooting. If poor image quality is discovered during a patient exam, stop clinical use until image quality is verified acceptable by the site’s mammography quality process. Expected outcome: No patient images are acquired on a system with unresolved image quality concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Image Quality Issue
steps.instructions: Ask mammography staff what was observed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves failed phantom QC, repeating artifacts in the same image location, noise or grainy image appearance, poor contrast, blurring, detector dropout, grid or line artifact, exposure inconsistency, or compression-related positioning concern. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Review the Most Recent Phantom QC Result
steps.instructions: Check whether the phantom QC failure was a single event or part of a trend. Review whether the failure involved image score, contrast, artifacts, density, noise, or positioning. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering understands whether the issue is isolated, repeatable, or worsening. If QC passed after a documented repeat using the correct procedure and staff confirm image quality is acceptable, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify Correct Phantom and QC Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct mammography phantom was used and that it was clean, undamaged, and positioned correctly. Check that the compression paddle, detector, and imaging field were set up according to the site’s approved QC workflow. Expected outcome: Setup error, phantom damage, or incorrect positioning is ruled out. If correcting the phantom setup resolves the failed QC, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Compression Paddle and Breast Support Surface
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the compression paddle, bucky surface, detector cover, and breast support area. Look for cracks, scratches, adhesive residue, dust, fluid marks, tape, paper, positioning markers, or foreign material. Expected outcome: External objects or surface contamination are removed as possible artifact sources. If cleaning or removing debris resolves the artifact, document the finding and return the device to use only after acceptable QC.

steps.title: Check for Repeating Artifact Location
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the artifact appears in the same place on every image or changes with positioning. A fixed-location artifact may suggest detector, grid, paddle, cover, or processing concern. A moving artifact may suggest positioning, object contamination, or accessory-related cause. Expected outcome: The artifact pattern helps separate external setup problems from likely system faults.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Paddle, Magnification Stand, and Accessories
steps.instructions: Verify the correct compression paddle and imaging accessory are installed for the selected exam type. Check that the paddle is fully seated, undamaged, and recognized if applicable. Expected outcome: Incorrect or loose accessories are ruled out. If changing to the correct accessory resolves the image issue, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Check Detector and Bucky Area for Obstruction or Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the detector/bucky area without removing covers or opening the system. Look for visible damage, loose external components, abnormal gaps, or anything trapped between the paddle, detector cover, and patient contact area. Expected outcome: Obvious external detector-area issues are identified without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Verify Workstation Display and Image Processing Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the issue is visible on more than one approved review location if possible. Check whether the artifact appears on the acquisition workstation, PACS, printed image, or only one display. Expected outcome: The issue is separated between acquisition/image generation and display or viewing workstation problems. If the image is acceptable on the acquisition system but poor only on one display, escalate the display, PACS, or workstation concern separately.

steps.title: Check Exposure Mode, Technique, and Exam Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm staff used the correct exam view, breast thickness range, exposure mode, target/filter selection, and clinical protocol. Do not change clinical protocols unless directed by the mammography quality lead, physicist, or manufacturer support. Expected outcome: Incorrect technique selection or workflow error is ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Cleaning, or Environmental Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after room cleaning, detector/bucky cleaning, paddle replacement, software update, power event, recent service, phantom replacement, or room temperature or humidity change. Expected outcome: A recent change is identified that may explain the new image quality issue.

steps.title: Verify System Power and Startup Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the system completed startup normally and has no active warnings, exposure errors, detector messages, generator messages, or calibration prompts. Expected outcome: The system is not being used while reporting a condition that could affect image acquisition. If active system faults are present, remove the device from clinical use and escalate for service.

steps.title: Repeat a Controlled QC Image Only If Safe and Authorized
steps.instructions: If site policy allows, have qualified mammography staff repeat the phantom QC using the approved procedure. Clinical Engineering should support the equipment checks but should not substitute for required mammography QC interpretation. Expected outcome: The image quality concern is confirmed or cleared through the site’s approved QC process.

steps.title: Document the Final Image Quality Status
steps.instructions: Record whether the artifact was resolved, QC passed, QC failed again, or the system remains clinically unacceptable. Include exact symptoms, accessory changes, cleaning performed, phantom condition, error messages, and whether the issue repeated. Expected outcome: The work order clearly supports return to service or escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Mammography staff reported failed phantom QC and a repeating artifact on MAMMOMAT Inspiration images.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found residue on the compression paddle surface that appeared in the imaging field during phantom testing.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned the paddle and breast support surface, verified correct phantom setup with mammography staff, and unit passed repeat QC per site process.

helpfulDetails: Whether phantom QC passed or failed
Exact artifact description and image location
Whether artifact was fixed or moving
Any system warnings or error messages
Recent service, cleaning, or power events
Whether issue appeared on acquisition workstation or PACS only
Environmental factors such as room temperature, humidity, cleaning, or recent power events
Indicator lights, detector messages, generator messages, calibration prompts, or workstation warnings
Accessories swapped, cleaned, reseated, or removed during troubleshooting

title: Siemens Healthineers MAMMOMAT Inspiration Mammography System - AWS workstation, DICOM send, printer, or archive communication failure
description: Troubleshooting AWS workstation, DICOM transfer, printer, archive, or network communication failures using external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is no longer being positioned, compressed, or actively imaged before troubleshooting. If the issue occurs during an active exam, do not repeat exposures only to test communication. Make sure acquired images are safely visible and preserved on the AWS workstation before proceeding. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected, and no unnecessary radiation exposure is performed for troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: AWS workstation application problem; DICOM send failure; PACS/archive unavailable; Printer not receiving images; Modality worklist unavailable; Images stuck in queue; Network or destination error message; Failed transfer after the exam was completed. Expected outcome: The failure is clearly identified as workstation, network, DICOM, printer, or archive-related before deeper troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify Images Are Still Available Locally
steps.instructions: Check whether the exam images are present on the AWS workstation. Confirm that the images can be opened, reviewed, and associated with the correct patient and accession. Expected outcome: Local image data is intact before attempting resend, reboot, or escalation. If images appear missing or patient data is incorrect, stop and escalate to imaging service/PACS support before attempting further changes.

steps.title: Check for Obvious AWS Workstation Issues
steps.instructions: Observe the AWS workstation display, keyboard, mouse, application status, and any active error messages. Confirm whether the application is frozen, logged out, displaying a warning, or waiting for user action. Expected outcome: A simple workstation lockup, login issue, or pending prompt is identified. If the AWS application resumes normally and communication restores, document and stop.

steps.title: Check AWS Workstation Power and Peripherals
steps.instructions: Verify the AWS workstation, monitors, keyboard, mouse, and any connected local peripherals are powered and responsive. Check that no power strip, UPS, or workstation cable has been bumped, disconnected, or turned off. Expected outcome: The workstation is fully powered and usable. If restoring power or reconnecting a loose cable resolves the issue, confirm successful image transfer or print output, document, and stop.

steps.title: Check Network Cable and Wall Jack Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the network cable at the AWS workstation and wall jack. Make sure the cable is fully seated and not damaged, stretched, or unplugged. If the workstation uses a labeled network jack, verify it is connected to the correct port and has not been moved to an unknown outlet. Expected outcome: The AWS workstation has a secure physical network connection. If reconnecting the network cable restores DICOM, worklist, printer, or archive communication, document and stop.

steps.title: Check for Network Link Indicators
steps.instructions: If visible, check the network port link lights at the workstation or network device. A missing link light may indicate a disconnected cable, inactive wall jack, switch issue, or workstation network adapter problem. Expected outcome: Network link status supports whether the problem is local connection-related or beyond the workstation.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Other Imaging Devices Are Affected
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether other modalities, workstations, PACS viewers, printers, or DICOM destinations are also failing. If multiple devices cannot send to PACS or print, the issue is more likely network, PACS, printer, or archive-side rather than the MAMMOMAT Inspiration itself. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to one mammography system or a broader network/PACS issue.

steps.title: Check DICOM Queue or Transfer Status
steps.instructions: Review the AWS workstation’s send queue, transfer status, or exam export status if accessible without advanced service access. Look for failed, pending, retrying, or blocked studies. Expected outcome: The system shows whether images are queued locally but not reaching the destination. If a resend option is available through normal operator workflow, attempt one resend only after confirming patient data and destination are correct.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Destination Was Selected
steps.instructions: Confirm the technologist selected the correct DICOM destination, PACS archive, printer, or export route. Check for incorrect destination selection, test destination use, or sending to an offline printer/archive. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by sending to the wrong configured destination. If selecting the correct destination resolves the issue, confirm receipt at PACS or printer, document, and stop.

steps.title: Check Modality Worklist Separately
steps.instructions: If the issue is worklist-related, confirm whether scheduled patients are unavailable, partially loading, or showing outdated information. Worklist failure may occur even when image send still works, so separate worklist communication from DICOM storage failure. Expected outcome: The affected communication path is isolated. If worklist is down but DICOM send works, escalate to PACS/RIS/interface support with that distinction documented.

steps.title: Check Printer Status
steps.instructions: If printing is the issue, inspect the mammography printer or print destination. Verify power, ready status, paper/film supply, error lights, jam indicators, and network connection. Expected outcome: Printer-side problems are ruled in or out before blaming the AWS workstation. If correcting printer power, media, or an obvious printer fault restores printing, document and stop.

steps.title: Confirm PACS or Archive Availability
steps.instructions: Ask PACS or imaging IT support whether the archive destination is online and receiving studies from other systems. Provide the affected modality, AE title if known, exam time, patient accession if appropriate, and the error message. Expected outcome: Archive-side downtime, rejected studies, or destination unavailability is identified quickly.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether any recent changes occurred, including: Network maintenance; PACS upgrade; Printer replacement; IP address change; Firewall change; Workstation replacement; Room move or cable work; New DICOM destination; User account or password change. Expected outcome: The failure is linked to a likely external configuration or infrastructure change.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Application Restart if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is active and images are confirmed locally available, follow site-approved procedure to close and reopen the AWS application or restart the workstation if allowed. Do not power-cycle acquisition hardware or interrupt image processing unless approved by site procedure or service support. Expected outcome: A temporary workstation communication or software hang is cleared. If communication restores after restart, confirm DICOM send, worklist, or printer function, document, and stop.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Test Transfer Only When Safe
steps.instructions: Use a non-patient test study, phantom study, or approved test workflow if available. Do not expose a patient or repeat patient images just to test DICOM communication. Expected outcome: Communication can be verified without unnecessary radiation exposure.

steps.title: Document Exact Error Information
steps.instructions: Record the exact AWS message, failed destination, time of failure, affected study, and whether the images remain stored locally. Include whether worklist, DICOM send, printing, and archive functions are affected separately. Expected outcome: Imaging IT, PACS support, or Siemens service receives enough detail to evaluate the failure efficiently.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the MAMMOMAT Inspiration AWS workstation could not send completed mammography images to PACS.
CCR.Cause: Images were present locally on the AWS workstation, but DICOM transfers remained queued after the workstation network cable was found loose at the wall jack.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated network cable, confirmed link restored, resent queued study to PACS successfully, and verified AWS communication returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient was removed from compression before troubleshooting
Images confirmed present locally on AWS
Other modalities checked for similar PACS issues
Queue status before and after resend

title: Siemens Healthineers Luminos Agile Max Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Table Tilt, Lift, Movement, or Locking Fault
description: Troubleshooting table tilt, lift, movement, brake, locking, or positioning faults using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the table or actively positioned for fluoroscopy. If the table tilt, lift, brake, or lock is unreliable, stop all table movement immediately. Expected outcome: The patient and staff are protected from unintended table motion, pinch points, or loss of positioning control. If the patient cannot be safely moved or the table is stuck in a clinically unsafe position, escalate immediately.

steps.title: Remove the Patient Before Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: If the issue occurred during an exam, have clinical staff move the patient to another table, stretcher, or approved imaging room before troubleshooting continues. Do not test table motion, tilt, lift, or locks with a patient on the table. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed only when the table is unloaded and safe to operate.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Movement Fault
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: Table tilt not moving; Table lift not raising or lowering; Tabletop movement not responding; Longitudinal or lateral movement restriction; Table lock or brake not engaging; Table lock or brake not releasing; Unexpected movement stop; Collision, limit, or positioning message; Hand control, foot control, or console command not responding. Expected outcome: The affected table function is clearly identified before checks begin.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Table
steps.instructions: Inspect under, beside, and behind the table for stools, step platforms, cables, detector accessories, linens, patient supports, footrests, or room equipment. Fluoroscopy tables may stop or inhibit movement if physical clearance is blocked. Expected outcome: The table path is clear and movement is not being blocked by external objects. If removing an obstruction restores movement, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Verify Table Load and Accessory Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not overloaded and that accessories are properly installed. Check side rails, compression devices, footboards, straps, patient supports, and removable table accessories for misalignment or interference. Expected outcome: Accessories are secure and not preventing tilt, lift, lock release, or tabletop travel. If correcting the accessory setup resolves the issue, document the cause and return the system to service after functional verification.

steps.title: Check the Emergency Stop and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: Inspect all accessible emergency stop buttons, table stop switches, and room safety controls. Confirm none are pressed, stuck, partially engaged, or obstructed. Expected outcome: Safety stop controls are released and the system is allowed to enable table motion. If an emergency stop was engaged, reset it according to site policy, verify table function, and document the finding.

steps.title: Review System Messages at the Operator Console
steps.instructions: Check the fluoroscopy console, room control panel, and system display for table, lock, collision, tilt, lift, brake, or position-related messages. Record the exact message before clearing or restarting anything. Expected outcome: The reported fault is matched to the system’s displayed status.

steps.title: Confirm System Readiness
steps.instructions: Verify the Luminos Agile Max is fully powered on, initialized, and not in startup, shutdown, service, standby, or interlock state. Some table functions may be disabled until the system completes initialization or recognizes the table position. Expected outcome: The system is ready for controlled table motion.

steps.title: Check Power to the System and Room Controls
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is receiving normal facility power. Check that the operator console, room controls, table controls, and system indicators are powered and responsive. Do not reset breakers repeatedly if a breaker trips or the system loses power again. Expected outcome: The system and table control circuits appear powered and stable. If power is unstable, remove the system from service and escalate to Facilities or Siemens service as appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect External Control Devices
steps.instructions: Check the hand control, table-side controls, console controls, and foot controls if applicable. Look for loose connectors, damaged cables, stuck buttons, fluid intrusion, cracked housings, or controls wedged under equipment. Expected outcome: External controls are connected, physically intact, and not mechanically stuck. If one control does not work but another control operates the same movement normally, suspect the affected control device or its connection.

steps.title: Test One Table Function at a Time
steps.instructions: With the table unloaded and the area clear, test only one function at a time: Raise/lower; Tilt; Tabletop longitudinal movement; Tabletop lateral movement; Lock engage/release; Brake hold/release. Use small controlled movements only. Expected outcome: The failed function is isolated without creating additional safety risk.

steps.title: Check Whether Movement Stops at a Limit
steps.instructions: Determine whether the table is already at a mechanical or programmed travel limit. Try moving the table slightly in the opposite direction if safe. Expected outcome: A normal limit condition is distinguished from a true movement fault. If movement resumes only away from the limit, verify operation and document the limit condition.

steps.title: Check Table Locks and Brakes
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the reported issue is failure to move or failure to hold position. A table that will not move may have a lock, brake, or inhibit still engaged. A table that will not hold position may have a locking or brake fault. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into “motion inhibited” or “locking/holding failure.” If the table does not reliably lock or hold position, remove it from service.

steps.title: Listen and Observe During a Safe Movement Attempt
steps.instructions: During a short unloaded test, observe for abnormal sounds, jerky movement, hesitation, clicking, grinding, unusual heat, odor, or sudden stops. Expected outcome: Any signs of mechanical drive, brake, lift, or table motion abnormality are identified. If abnormal sound, smell, heat, or erratic movement is present, stop testing and escalate.

steps.title: Restart Only If Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If there is no patient on the system and no urgent clinical use is underway, perform a controlled system restart according to site policy. After restart, allow the system to fully initialize before retesting table movement. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or initialization faults may clear. If the table fault returns after restart, do not continue repeated resets.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: If the issue appears resolved, verify the affected table function several times through a safe limited range. Confirm table movement is smooth, stops normally, locks correctly, and no fault messages return. Expected outcome: The table is safe and predictable before being returned to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Luminos Agile Max table would not tilt and displayed a table movement fault during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the table path obstructed by a footboard accessory and confirmed the emergency stop was not engaged.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the obstruction, verified unloaded table tilt and lift operation, confirmed table locks engaged correctly, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the issue occurred
Obstructions found near or under the table
Accessory setup or patient support involved
Any abnormal noise, heat, smell, or jerky motion
Whether the table locked and held position

title: Siemens Healthineers Luminos Agile Max Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Fluoro Pedal, Hand Switch, or Exposure Control Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting fluoro pedal, hand switch, exposure button, or X-ray control response issues using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is positioned on the table or an exam is actively in progress. If fluoro or exposure control is unreliable during an exam, stop troubleshooting and have clinical staff move the patient to another approved imaging workflow if needed. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on a system with unreliable exposure control.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Control That Is Not Responding
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist which control failed and when it failed. Identify whether the issue involves: Fluoro foot pedal not initiating fluoroscopy; Hand switch not initiating exposure; Exposure button not responding; Intermittent response from the control; Control works in one mode but not another; System shows X-ray inhibit, generator not ready, or exposure unavailable. Expected outcome: The affected control and failure condition are clearly identified before parts are suspected.

steps.title: Check for System-Ready or X-ray Inhibit Messages
steps.instructions: Review the operator display, acquisition workstation, and system status messages. Look for messages related to: X-ray inhibit; Door or room safety interlock; Tube or generator not ready; Detector not ready; Collimator or SID position; Table or tube stand position; Emergency stop active; Exposure mode not selected. Expected outcome: Any system condition preventing exposure is identified before assuming the pedal or hand switch is faulty. If clearing the system-ready condition restores fluoro or exposure response, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Exam Mode and Workflow State
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is in a valid fluoro or radiographic acquisition workflow. Make sure the exam is selected, patient/study information is active, and the system is not waiting for positioning, calibration, confirmation, or image processing. Expected outcome: The control is being tested while the system is actually ready to accept an exposure command.

steps.title: Inspect the Fluoro Pedal Externally
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the foot pedal for damage, contamination, fluid intrusion, stuck buttons, cracked housing, loose cable strain relief, or crushed cable sections. Press the pedal and confirm it returns normally. Expected outcome: The pedal is physically intact, dry, unobstructed, and not mechanically stuck. If the pedal is damaged, wet, or unsafe to use, remove it from service and escalate for replacement or repair.

steps.title: Check the Foot Pedal Cable and Connection
steps.instructions: Trace the fluoro pedal cable from the pedal to its connection point. Confirm the connector is fully seated, not bent, not cross-threaded, and not under tension from table or room movement. Expected outcome: The pedal connection is secure and the cable is not visibly damaged. If reseating a loose external connector restores response, test several activations, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Hand Switch or Exposure Switch
steps.instructions: Check the hand switch for cracked housing, damaged cord, sticky button action, bent connector pins, loose plug, or evidence of being dropped. Confirm the switch is not trapped under accessories, table pads, drapes, or cable bundles. Expected outcome: The hand switch is externally intact and able to actuate normally.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Control Is Assigned or Enabled
steps.instructions: Check whether the system allows different exposure controls or room controls to be active depending on workflow, room setup, or selected mode. Confirm staff are using the correct pedal, hand switch, or console control for the selected fluoro or radiographic function. Expected outcome: The reported control matches the selected acquisition function.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Inspect visible emergency stop buttons and room safety controls. Confirm no emergency stop is pressed and that any door, table, tube stand, or collision safety condition has been resolved. Expected outcome: Exposure is not being blocked by a safety interlock. If an emergency stop or safety condition is found active, reset only according to site policy, verify system readiness, and retest.

steps.title: Check Room Power and Control Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm the system, generator cabinet, operator console, monitor workstation, and room control components are powered normally. Look for abnormal indicator lights, blank displays, startup errors, warning tones, or communication messages. Expected outcome: The exposure control issue is not caused by a broader system power or communication problem.

steps.title: Test With the Patient Area Clear
steps.instructions: With no patient on the table and the room clear, ask qualified imaging staff to attempt a controlled system-ready check according to site policy. Confirm whether the pedal, hand switch, or exposure control responds consistently. Expected outcome: The control response is verified safely without patient risk. If the control works normally after external checks, return the unit to service only after clinical staff confirm acceptable operation.

steps.title: Compare With Another Approved Control If Available
steps.instructions: If the system has another approved exposure control option, have qualified staff test whether fluoro or exposure can be initiated from that alternate control. Do not bypass safety systems or use unapproved adapters. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one external control, one connection path, or a broader system inhibit. If one control fails but another approved control works, remove the suspect control from service and escalate for evaluation or replacement.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only If Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the system is not in clinical use and site policy allows, perform a normal shutdown and restart of the Fluoroscopy / Interventional System and workstation. Do not power cycle during an active patient procedure unless directed by the clinical team and site downtime procedure. Expected outcome: Temporary software or control communication faults may clear after a controlled restart. If control response returns after restart, verify repeated operation and document the event.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Exposure Attempts
steps.instructions: Avoid repeated fluoro or exposure attempts if the control is intermittent, delayed, or unreliable. Intermittent exposure control is a patient safety concern because staff may not be able to predict when X-ray will initiate. Expected outcome: The device is not used clinically until exposure control reliability is confirmed.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that the Luminos Agile Max fluoro pedal did not initiate fluoroscopy during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the fluoro pedal connector partially seated at the room connection point, causing no response from the pedal.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the pedal connector, verified system-ready status, confirmed pedal response with staff, and returned the Fluoroscopy / Interventional System to service.

helpfulDetails: Which control failed: fluoro pedal, hand switch, console exposure button, or room control
Whether the issue was no response, delayed response, or intermittent response
Any system messages or X-ray inhibit warnings
Whether the system was in fluoro mode or radiographic exposure mode
Whether emergency stop or door interlock was active
Pedal and hand switch cable condition

title: Siemens Healthineers Luminos Agile Max Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Collimator, Tube Stand, SID, or Positioning Error
description: Troubleshooting collimator, tube stand, SID, table, or positioning errors using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the fluoroscopy table or positioned for an exam. Stop all table, tube stand, detector, and collimator movement if positioning is unreliable, restricted, or unexpected. Expected outcome: Patient, staff, lines, tubing, contrast equipment, and room accessories are protected from unintended movement. If the system cannot move safely or the patient cannot be repositioned safely, stop troubleshooting and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Positioning Issue
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: Collimator blades not moving; Light field not working; Field size not matching expected image area; Tube stand movement fault; SID value not updating; Table positioning error; Detector or image receptor alignment issue; Positioning lock, brake, or limit message; Movement inhibited during fluoro or radiography setup. Expected outcome: The affected function is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check for Active System Messages or Inhibits
steps.instructions: Review the operator display, system console, and workstation messages for positioning, collimator, SID, table, or movement-related alerts. Do not clear or dismiss messages without recording them. Expected outcome: The fault message is captured and used to guide safe troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the System Is Not in an Unsafe or Restricted Movement State
steps.instructions: Confirm the table, tube stand, detector, and room equipment are not at a mechanical limit, collision zone, parked position requirement, or workflow-restricted position. Move only through normal operator controls and only when the area is clear. Expected outcome: The system either resumes normal movement or confirms a repeatable positioning fault. If movement returns to normal after moving out of a limit or restricted position, verify operation and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Area Around the Table, Tube Stand, and Detector
steps.instructions: Look for physical obstructions around the table base, tabletop, foot area, detector carriage, tube stand, wall stand if applicable, and ceiling or floor-mounted accessories. Check for stools, foot pedals, cables, IV poles, contrast injector lines, monitor arms, step platforms, or stored supplies blocking movement. Expected outcome: No external object is preventing normal system positioning. If an obstruction is removed and positioning works normally, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: Verify that any emergency stop buttons, movement release controls, or room safety switches are not engaged. Reset only according to site policy and normal operator workflow. Expected outcome: The system is not being intentionally held in a safety-stop condition. If resetting a safety stop restores movement, confirm full safe operation before returning to use.

steps.title: Verify Operator Control Inputs
steps.instructions: Test the affected movement using the appropriate normal control, such as table controls, tube stand controls, collimator controls, or room-side controls. If more than one control location is available, compare operation from each location. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to the device function itself or to a specific control station. If one control station works and another does not, remove from service or limit use according to site policy until the defective control is evaluated.

steps.title: Check Footswitches, Hand Controls, and Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect external cables, connectors, control pendants, footswitches, and hand controls for damage, loose connections, liquid exposure, pinching, or strain. Reseat accessible external connectors only if they are intended to be user-accessible and can be handled safely. Expected outcome: External control accessories are connected, intact, and not causing intermittent movement commands or inhibits.

steps.title: Verify Collimator Function Externally
steps.instructions: Check whether the collimator light field turns on, shutters respond, field size changes, and displayed collimation information appears reasonable. Do not attempt internal collimator repair or adjustment. Expected outcome: Collimator movement and light field behavior are confirmed through normal operator checks. If the collimator is stuck, noisy, misaligned, or does not match the displayed field, remove the system from clinical use and escalate.

steps.title: Confirm SID and Position Readout Behavior
steps.instructions: Check whether the SID value changes appropriately when the tube stand, table, or detector position changes. Compare the displayed SID with the expected physical position from the exam setup. Expected outcome: SID indication updates consistently and matches expected positioning. If the SID is frozen, clearly incorrect, or intermittently changes, suspect a position sensor, encoder, or communication issue and escalate.

steps.title: Check Table and Tube Stand Locks or Brakes
steps.instructions: Verify the table, tabletop, and tube stand are not locked in a position that prevents motion. Confirm brakes release and reapply normally through standard controls. Expected outcome: The system can move and lock securely without drifting, sticking, or unexpected release. If brakes fail to hold, fail to release, or make abnormal noise, remove the system from service.

steps.title: Review Recent Room or Workflow Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after cleaning, accessory change, software restart, power event, room move, detector change, service visit, or exam protocol change. Expected outcome: A recent external change may explain the positioning issue. If the issue began immediately after an accessory or workflow change, reverse the change if safe and verify operation.

steps.title: Restart the System Only if Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is active and the clinical team agrees, perform a normal system restart using the site-approved shutdown and startup process. Do not power-cycle during an active exam unless directed by site emergency policy. Expected outcome: Temporary control or communication faults may clear after a controlled restart. If the fault returns after restart, continue escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Limited Functional Check
steps.instructions: With no patient present, verify basic safe movement and setup functions: Table movement; Tube stand positioning; Detector alignment; SID readout; Collimator response; Light field operation; Movement locks and brakes; Any displayed positioning messages. Expected outcome: The system either passes a basic operational check or the fault is confirmed as repeatable. If all functions pass and no error returns, document the checks completed and return to service only according to site policy.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Luminos Agile Max displayed a SID positioning error and would not allow normal fluoroscopy setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the tube stand was at a movement limit with the detector not aligned to the expected exam position.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared the positioning setup, verified table and tube stand movement, confirmed SID readout updated normally, and returned the system to service after functional check.

helpfulDetails: Patient present or no patient present during failure
Exact error message or code displayed
Table position and tube stand position at time of failure
Any movement limit, brake, or lock message
Footswitch, hand control, or room control behavior
Obstructions found near table, detector, or tube stand
Abnormal noise, sticking, drifting, heat, or smell

title: Siemens Healthineers Luminos Agile Max Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Detector Not Ready, Image Acquisition Fault, or Failed Calibration
description: Troubleshooting detector readiness, acquisition faults, calibration failures, or image receptor issues using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is positioned on the table and no fluoroscopy or radiographic exposure is being attempted. If the issue occurs during an active exam, clinical staff should stop imaging and move the patient to another approved imaging workflow if needed. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting begins only when the patient is safe and the system is not being used clinically.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what message appeared and when it occurred.
