Determine whether the issue involves: Detector not ready; Image acquisition failed; Calibration failed; No image displayed after exposure; Image receptor not initialized; Flat detector communication fault; Error during startup, exam setup, or system readiness check. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before resets or accessory checks are performed.

steps.title: Check System Readiness at the Operator Console
steps.instructions: Review the system status on the Luminos Agile Max workstation or console. Confirm whether the system is fully booted, logged in, and not waiting for a startup sequence, detector initialization, or room readiness condition. Expected outcome: The system either shows ready status or provides a specific fault message to guide the next step.

steps.title: Verify Table and Detector Position
steps.instructions: Confirm the table, detector, tube assembly, and imaging chain are in a valid position for the selected exam or calibration. A detector may show not ready if the imaging geometry is outside the expected position, the table is not properly parked, or the system cannot confirm alignment. Expected outcome: The detector and imaging components are positioned correctly for the selected workflow. If correcting the position clears the fault, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Detector and Table
steps.instructions: Inspect the table area, detector travel path, compression/accessory area if applicable, cables, foot controls, and nearby equipment. Remove any objects that may block table movement, detector positioning, or system reference movement. Expected outcome: No physical obstruction prevents detector positioning or system initialization.

steps.title: Confirm Detector Area Is Free of Fluid, Debris, or Damage
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the tabletop, detector cover, detector housing, and nearby seams for signs of fluid intrusion, impact damage, cracks, loose covers, or contamination. Do not open covers or attempt internal inspection. Expected outcome: No obvious external condition suggests damage or contamination affecting detector readiness. If fluid intrusion, burning smell, unusual heat, or physical damage is present, remove the system from service and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Check External Cables and Visible Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external workstation, monitor, network, power, and peripheral cables. Verify cables are seated, not pulled tight, kinked, pinched, or damaged. Expected outcome: No loose or damaged external connection is causing acquisition or communication failure.

steps.title: Verify Room Power and Workstation Power
steps.instructions: Confirm the Fluoroscopy / Interventional System, acquisition workstation, monitors, and related equipment have power. Check for tripped power strips, disconnected plugs, failed monitor power, or local outlet issues where applicable. Expected outcome: All required system components are powered and stable.

steps.title: Restart Only Through the Approved User-Level Shutdown Process
steps.instructions: If the system is not in clinical use and the fault appears software-related, perform a controlled shutdown and restart using the site-approved procedure. Do not perform hard power cycling unless approved by service documentation or site policy. Expected outcome: The system reboots cleanly and detector initialization completes. If the detector becomes ready and acquisition works normally after restart, document the reset and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Exam and Acquisition Workflow
steps.instructions: Verify that the selected exam, image mode, detector configuration, and acquisition workflow are appropriate for the procedure. Incorrect workflow selection may prevent acquisition or calibration from starting correctly. Expected outcome: The selected protocol supports the intended detector and acquisition mode.

steps.title: Check Calibration Conditions
steps.instructions: If calibration failed, confirm the calibration was attempted with the correct setup: No patient on the table; No objects in the X-ray field; Table and detector in required position; Detector warmed up if required by normal workflow; System not reporting motion, alignment, or safety interlock faults. Expected outcome: Calibration conditions are valid before assuming detector failure.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue started after: System reboot; Power outage; Room cleaning; Network maintenance; Workstation update; Detector/table impact; Failed prior exam; Recent service activity. Expected outcome: Any likely trigger is identified and included in the service documentation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Test Acquisition Only If Allowed
steps.instructions: If the system appears ready after checks, have qualified imaging staff perform a test acquisition or approved QC check according to site policy. Clinical Engineering should not create unnecessary radiation exposure or bypass clinical imaging controls. Expected outcome: Image acquisition completes successfully and image display is normal. If the test acquisition passes and no faults return, return the device to service per site policy.

steps.title: Check for Repeating Faults
steps.instructions: If the detector becomes ready temporarily but the acquisition fault returns, document the exact message, timing, and whether the fault occurs during startup, positioning, fluoro, radiographic acquisition, or calibration. Expected outcome: The pattern is captured for service escalation.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Resets
steps.instructions: If detector readiness, acquisition, or calibration faults continue after basic checks and one controlled restart, stop troubleshooting. Repeated resets can delay patient care and may hide an intermittent detector, acquisition computer, communication, or calibration issue. Expected outcome: The system is escalated appropriately instead of being forced back into service.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Siemens Luminos Agile Max displayed “detector not ready” and would not acquire images during room setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no patient present, verified room power, checked visible workstation and system connections, confirmed detector positioning, and observed the detector fault returned after a controlled restart.
CCR.Resolution: System was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Siemens service for detector/acquisition system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector or acquisition fault message
Whether issue occurred at startup, calibration, fluoro, or radiographic acquisition
Patient present or no patient present
Test acquisition or QC result, if completed
Any unusual heat, smell, noise, fluid, or physical damage

title: Siemens Healthineers Luminos Agile Max Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Syngo Workstation, DICOM Send, Archive, or Printer Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting syngo workstation, DICOM send, archive, printer, or study transfer failures using external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient exam is complete or safely paused before troubleshooting image transfer, archive, or printer communication issues. Do not restart the workstation, acquisition system, or network-connected components during an active fluoroscopy procedure unless clinical leadership confirms it is safe. Expected outcome: Patient care is not interrupted by workstation or network troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Ask staff what failed and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: syngo workstation not responding; Images not appearing at PACS; DICOM send failure; Archive queue stuck; Printer not receiving images; RIS/worklist issue; Study stuck in pending, failed, or retry status; Network connection error; One study affected or all studies affected. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to workstation, DICOM destination, printer, worklist, or network communication.

steps.title: Check Whether Imaging and Local Review Still Work
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Luminos Agile Max can still acquire images and whether prior images can be reviewed locally on the syngo workstation. If images are present locally but not reaching PACS, archive, or printer, the issue is likely communication-related. If images cannot be acquired, displayed, or saved locally, stop communication troubleshooting and escalate as an acquisition/workstation fault. Expected outcome: Local image storage and review are confirmed before focusing on external transfer paths.

steps.title: Verify the Workstation Is Powered and Responsive
steps.instructions: Check that the syngo workstation, monitor, keyboard, mouse, and any connected console components are powered and responsive. Look for frozen screens, login prompts, system warnings, or disconnected peripherals. If the workstation is frozen, follow site-approved restart procedures only when no patient care is active. Expected outcome: The workstation is available for queue, destination, and study checks.

steps.title: Check Network Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible Ethernet connections at the syngo workstation, network wall jack, printer, and any local network device used by the imaging room. Confirm cables are fully seated and not damaged, kinked, unplugged, or pulled tight behind the workstation or printer. If a loose cable is found, reseat it and retest the failed send or printer function. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the corrected connection. Expected outcome: Obvious physical network disconnections are ruled out.

steps.title: Check Network Link Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: If visible and safe to access, check for link/activity lights on the workstation network port, wall-connected device, or printer Ethernet port. No link light may indicate a disconnected cable, inactive network jack, failed printer network port, or network switch issue. Expected outcome: Basic physical network activity is confirmed or identified as absent.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Other Devices in the Area Are Affected
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether nearby imaging rooms, printers, PACS workstations, or modality worklists are also having communication problems. If multiple devices are affected, the issue may involve hospital network, PACS, RIS, print server, or DICOM destination availability. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as device-specific or part of a larger network/system outage.

steps.title: Review the DICOM Send or Archive Queue
steps.instructions: On the workstation, check the send/archive queue or job status if available to Clinical Engineering. Look for failed, pending, paused, rejected, or retrying jobs. Confirm whether failures affect: One study only; One destination only; All PACS sends; Printer jobs only; Worklist only. Expected outcome: The failed communication path is narrowed by queue behavior.

steps.title: Retry a Failed Send Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the study is present locally and the destination appears available, retry the failed DICOM send according to site workflow. Do not delete studies or clear queues unless the images are verified safely archived elsewhere and the action is approved by site policy. If the retry succeeds, stop troubleshooting and document the successful resend. Expected outcome: A temporary communication interruption is cleared without risking image loss.

steps.title: Verify Correct Patient and Study Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are attempting to send, archive, or print the correct study, series, or image set. Some issues are workflow-related, such as unsent images, incomplete exams, wrong destination selection, or a study not finalized. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by incomplete exam workflow or incorrect destination selection.

steps.title: Check DICOM Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the intended destination is selected, such as PACS, archive, printer, CD/export station, or another configured DICOM node. If only one destination fails while others work, the problem may be with that specific destination, routing rule, printer, AE title, IP address, port, or receiving system. Expected outcome: The affected destination is isolated.

steps.title: Check RIS or Modality Worklist Behavior
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves patient orders not appearing, verify whether the modality worklist is unavailable for all patients or only one order. Ask staff to confirm the order exists in the scheduling/RIS system and is assigned to the correct modality/location. If other modalities also cannot retrieve worklists, escalate to PACS/RIS/IT support. Expected outcome: Worklist failure is separated from image send or printer failure.

steps.title: Check Printer Status
steps.instructions: If the issue involves film or paper printing, inspect the printer for external issues. Check for: Power on; Network connection; Error messages; Empty film or paper; Jam condition; Offline status; Door open; Low consumables; Printer queue errors. Correct obvious supply or offline issues if allowed by site policy. If the printer resumes printing, stop troubleshooting and document the printer condition corrected. Expected outcome: Printer-side faults are ruled out before blaming the Fluoroscopy / Interventional System.

steps.title: Confirm Destination Availability with IT, PACS, or Imaging Informatics
steps.instructions: If the workstation appears functional and local images are intact, contact the appropriate PACS, network, or imaging informatics team. Ask them to verify: PACS node availability; Printer node availability; DICOM listener status; AE title/IP/port reachability; Network jack or VLAN status; Recent PACS, RIS, or network changes; Storage/archive capacity issues. Expected outcome: External enterprise system problems are confirmed or ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Recent Configuration or Network Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to PACS, network switches, IP addressing, printer replacement, firewall rules, workstation updates, or RIS interface settings. Communication failures often appear after configuration changes outside the fluoroscopy room. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified as possible causes.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only if Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: If the workstation is unresponsive or communication services appear stuck, perform a controlled restart only when: No active patient exam is in progress; Images are not at risk of being lost; Staff agree the room can be unavailable; Site restart procedure allows it. After restart, verify workstation login, local image access, worklist availability, and DICOM send/printer function. If communication restores after restart, stop troubleshooting and document the result. Expected outcome: A temporary workstation communication lockup is cleared safely.

steps.title: Verify Final Function
steps.instructions: Confirm the affected workflow now works before returning the room to normal use. Depending on the issue, verify: Test or prior study sends successfully to PACS; Study appears in PACS; Printer receives and completes job; Worklist populates correctly; Archive queue is clear or actively processing; No new communication alarms appear. Expected outcome: The failed communication path is verified operational.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that Luminos Agile Max fluoroscopy images were stuck in the syngo DICOM send queue and were not appearing in PACS.
CCR.Cause: Found local image review working, but the PACS DICOM destination was unreachable while the workstation and room network cable connections appeared secure.
CCR.Resolution: Verified images remained stored locally, notified PACS/IT for DICOM destination review, labeled the room limited use per site workflow, and documented final status for follow-up.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or error message displayed
Accessories swapped or destination selected, including PACS, archive, printer, or worklist
Power behavior at the workstation, printer, monitor, keyboard, mouse, and connected console components
Environmental factors, including whether other rooms, PACS workstations, printers, or modality worklists were affected
Indicator lights, including network link/activity lights at the workstation, wall jack, printer, or local network device

Final device status, including whether patient care was active or completed, whether images were stored locally, whether PACS/IT was contacted, whether restart was performed, and whether the room was returned to use, limited use, or labeled Out of Service

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph mCT PET / CT System - PET Detector Normalization, Calibration, or Daily QC Failure
description: Troubleshooting PET daily QC, detector normalization, calibration, or sensitivity-related failures using safe external checks before service escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the PET/CT table or scheduled for immediate scanning on the affected system. If the PET daily QC or detector calibration failed before patient imaging, do not proceed with clinical PET acquisition until QC status is acceptable per site policy. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not performed on a PET system with unresolved calibration or detector readiness concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Failed Process
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist or physicist which process failed and when it failed. Identify whether the issue involves: PET daily QC failure; Detector normalization failure; Calibration failure; Blank scan or source-related QC failure; High count-rate variation; Detector block or ring error; SUV, sensitivity, or uniformity concern; Failure after startup, reboot, service, or environmental change. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Review the System Message and QC Result
steps.instructions: Check the operator console or syngo workstation for the displayed error message, failed test name, detector warning, or QC result summary. Record whether the failure points to a detector, source, acquisition, motion, environmental, or processing issue. Expected outcome: The reported failure is documented accurately instead of described only as “PET QC failed.”

steps.title: Verify the System Is Not in an Unsafe or Incomplete Startup State
steps.instructions: Confirm the Biograph mCT completed normal startup and that PET, CT, gantry, table, acquisition computer, and workstation functions are available. If the system recently restarted, allow the normal initialization process to complete before repeating QC. Expected outcome: QC is not being attempted while the system is still initializing or partially unavailable.

steps.title: Check Room and System Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the PET/CT room temperature and humidity are within the facility’s normal imaging room range. Look for recent HVAC issues, temperature swings, water leaks, unusual heat, or airflow obstruction around system cabinets. PET detector performance can be sensitive to environmental instability. Expected outcome: No obvious room condition is contributing to detector or calibration instability.

steps.title: Confirm No Obstruction or Unapproved Object Is in the PET Bore
steps.instructions: Inspect the scanner bore, patient table, table pad, head holder, positioning aids, straps, coils, and accessories. Remove any non-required items before QC or calibration. Expected outcome: Nothing in the field of view is interfering with PET QC, blank scan, normalization, or calibration activity. If removing the obstruction resolves the issue after repeating the appropriate QC, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Patient Table Is Properly Positioned
steps.instructions: Confirm the table moves normally, indexes correctly, and is positioned according to the QC or calibration workflow. Check for table limit messages, positioning errors, or incomplete table travel. Expected outcome: PET QC is not failing because the table is mispositioned, blocked, or not reaching the expected scan position.

steps.title: Check the PET Calibration or QC Source Setup
steps.instructions: If the workflow uses a PET calibration, normalization, or QC source, confirm the correct source is available, seated, and handled according to site radiation safety policy. Do not handle radioactive sources unless authorized and trained under the facility’s radiation safety program. Expected outcome: The correct source and setup are confirmed by qualified staff.

steps.title: Confirm Source Identity, Activity, and Expiration Status With Qualified Staff
steps.instructions: Ask Nuclear Medicine, Physics, or Radiation Safety to confirm that the expected source is not expired, decayed below usable activity, incorrectly selected, or mismatched to the QC workflow. Low activity, wrong source selection, or expired calibration data can cause QC or normalization failures. Expected outcome: Source-related causes are ruled out by qualified personnel.

steps.title: Verify the Correct QC or Calibration Workflow Was Selected
steps.instructions: Confirm the technologist selected the correct daily QC, normalization, calibration, or blank scan procedure for the Biograph mCT. Check whether the wrong protocol, incorrect scanner configuration, or wrong source option was selected. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by an incorrect operator workflow selection.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Software, or Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether any recent service activity, software update, detector calibration, PET electronics work, network change, or workstation change occurred. A failure immediately after a change may indicate the system requires vendor-level verification or recalibration. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified and included in escalation notes.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Accessible Connections
steps.instructions: Without opening system covers, visually inspect accessible workstation, acquisition computer, gantry-side, and network connections. Look for disconnected cables, loose network patch cords, damaged connectors, tripped power strips, or disturbed equipment after cleaning or service. Expected outcome: No obvious external disconnection is causing PET acquisition, processing, or detector communication failure. If reconnecting an obvious external cable restores communication and QC passes, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Check Workstation and Acquisition Computer Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the acquisition computer and syngo workstation are powered, responsive, logged in correctly, and not displaying storage, database, application, or communication errors. PET QC can fail if acquisition data is collected but not processed correctly. Expected outcome: The PET acquisition and processing chain is available.

steps.title: Check for DICOM, Storage, or Disk Space Warnings
steps.instructions: Review whether the workstation reports low disk space, archive queue errors, database errors, or failed image storage. While not always the root cause of detector QC failure, processing and storage problems can interrupt QC completion. Expected outcome: The QC failure is not caused by a workstation storage or processing limitation.

steps.title: Repeat the QC Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: After external setup, source, room, table, and workstation checks are complete, have qualified imaging staff repeat the appropriate QC or calibration workflow. Do not repeatedly run failed calibration routines without physics or vendor guidance. Expected outcome: The test either passes after correcting an external condition or fails consistently with a documented symptom.

steps.title: Review for Repeating Detector or Ring Failure Patterns
steps.instructions: If the repeated test fails, note whether the same detector block, ring, module, or channel is repeatedly identified. A repeatable detector-specific failure is more likely to require Siemens service evaluation. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented for escalation.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Internal Detector or Electronics Repair
steps.instructions: Do not open PET gantry covers, reseat internal detector electronics, adjust calibration files, modify normalization data, or perform internal board-level troubleshooting unless authorized and trained for that system. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains within safe Clinical Engineering limits.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported that the Siemens Biograph mCT failed PET daily QC with a detector normalization/calibration error before patient imaging.
CCR.Cause: External checks found no bore obstruction, table positioning issue, workstation fault, or obvious source setup problem; repeat QC continued to fail with the same detector-related message.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PET/CT system from clinical PET service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented QC failure details, and escalated to Siemens service and medical physics for further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact PET QC, normalization, or calibration test that failed
Whether the issue occurred before patient scanning
Source type, activity status, and expiration confirmation by qualified staff
Whether table movement and indexing were normal
Whether any object was found in the bore
Whether workstation or acquisition computer errors were present
Whether a reboot or repeat QC was attempted
Whether the same detector area failed repeatedly
Recent service, software, or environmental changes

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph mCT PET / CT System - CT Gantry, Tube, Generator, or Cooling Fault
description: Troubleshooting CT gantry, tube, generator, exposure abort, tube heat, or cooling-related faults using safe external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the table or positioned in the scanner bore. If the CT fault occurs during an active PET/CT exam, stop troubleshooting until the patient is safely removed or clinical staff confirm it is safe to continue. Expected outcome: The patient is not left dependent on a scanner with unreliable gantry, tube, generator, or cooling performance.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what message appeared and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: CT exposure abort; Tube heat or tube cooling warning; Generator not ready; Gantry rotation fault; Tube warmup failure; CT acquisition unavailable; System cooling or temperature warning; Fault during scout/topogram, CT portion, or PET/CT workflow. Expected outcome: The failure type and timing are clearly understood before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Is Workflow-Related
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct exam protocol was selected and the CT portion of the PET/CT study was properly initialized. Verify the system was not interrupted during patient registration, table positioning, CT setup, or acquisition preparation. Expected outcome: A workflow interruption or setup error is ruled out before assuming equipment failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Gantry Area for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually inspect around the gantry, table, bore opening, covers, floor area, and accessory zone. Look for IV poles, patient lines, positioning aids, blankets, straps, phantoms, or other objects that may interfere with gantry or table movement. Expected outcome: The gantry and scan path are physically clear. If an obstruction is found, remove it, reset the workflow as appropriate, and retest only when safe. If the fault clears, return the system to service after verifying normal operation.

steps.title: Verify Room Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Check whether the CT room feels unusually warm or poorly ventilated. Confirm that HVAC is operating and that airflow around equipment cabinets, gantry vents, and cooling-related areas is not blocked. Cooling and temperature faults can occur when room conditions are outside normal operating expectations. Expected outcome: No obvious room temperature, airflow, or blocked-vent condition is contributing to the fault.

steps.title: Check for Visible Cooling System Concerns
steps.instructions: Inspect externally for signs of leaks, water on the floor, unusual condensation, blocked vents, abnormal fan noise, or overheating smell near the gantry or equipment cabinets. Do not open covers or bypass cooling safeguards. Expected outcome: No visible external cooling hazard is present. If fluid, burning smell, excessive heat, or abnormal noise is present, remove the system from service and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify Main Power and Facility Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the PET/CT system, operator console, gantry, and associated equipment cabinets have normal power indications. Ask whether the department recently experienced a power outage, generator transfer, emergency power event, breaker trip, HVAC failure, or room shutdown. Expected outcome: The fault is not tied to an obvious facility power or environmental event.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Interlock Status
steps.instructions: Verify that no emergency stop button is engaged in the scan room, control room, or equipment area. Confirm doors, covers, access panels, and safety-related interlocks appear normally closed from the outside. Expected outcome: The system is not being inhibited by an engaged emergency stop or obvious safety interlock condition.

steps.title: Review Tube Warmup or Tube Conditioning Status
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist whether tube warmup was completed according to the site’s normal startup process. If the system indicates tube warmup is required, have the operator follow the approved operator workflow. Expected outcome: CT exposure is not being inhibited by an incomplete warmup or normal readiness requirement. If tube warmup fails repeatedly or aborts, stop and escalate.

steps.title: Allow Proper Cooling Time if Tube Heat Is Reported
steps.instructions: If the system reports tube heat, cooling, or exposure delay after heavy CT use, allow the system to cool according to the displayed system guidance. Do not repeatedly force acquisitions against cooling warnings. Expected outcome: The scanner becomes available after normal cooling time. If the warning does not clear after reasonable cooling time or returns immediately, remove the system from service and escalate.

steps.title: Check Console and System Readiness
steps.instructions: Review the operator console for system-ready status, CT-ready status, gantry-ready status, generator-ready status, and any active fault messages. Document the exact message, code, and when it appears. Expected outcome: The active fault is captured accurately for service review.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Restart Only If Safe and Site-Approved
steps.instructions: If no patient is on the system and the fault appears software or communication-related, perform only the site-approved restart process for the operator console/system. Do not cycle main power, gantry power, or equipment cabinet power unless that is part of local policy or directed by qualified service support. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or readiness faults may clear after a normal restart. If the CT subsystem returns to ready and test acquisition passes, verify operation with staff before returning the system to clinical use.

steps.title: Run Only Approved Operational Checks
steps.instructions: If the system appears ready after checks, have qualified staff perform an approved non-patient test, such as a scout/topogram readiness check or site-approved QC workflow. Do not expose a patient to confirm repair. Expected outcome: CT operation is verified without patient risk.

steps.title: Stop if Faults Repeat
steps.instructions: If gantry rotation faults, generator faults, tube faults, cooling warnings, exposure aborts, or CT-not-ready messages repeat after basic checks, stop troubleshooting. Repeated CT subsystem faults may involve internal gantry components, X-ray tube, generator, cooling loop, detector communication, power distribution, or service-level calibration. Expected outcome: The device is not repeatedly reset or forced back into use without repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported Biograph mCT CT exposure aborted during the CT portion of a PET/CT exam with a tube cooling warning displayed.
CCR.Cause: Verified no patient was on the table, gantry area was clear, emergency stops were released, room HVAC was operating, and the warning returned after normal cooling time.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Biograph mCT from service, labeled system Out of Service, documented fault message and timing, and escalated to Siemens service for CT tube/generator/cooling evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact CT fault message or code
Whether issue occurred during scout, CT acquisition, PET/CT workflow, warmup, or startup
Patient on table: yes or no
Visible leaks, unusual heat, smell, or noise
Whether approved QC or test acquisition passed

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph mCT PET / CT System - Patient Table Movement, Limit, or Positioning Error
description: Troubleshooting patient table movement, travel limits, indexing, height, docking, or scan positioning errors using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the PET / CT table. If table movement is unreliable, unexpected, blocked, or not responding normally, stop all table motion immediately. Expected outcome: The patient, staff, IV lines, positioning aids, and accessories are protected from unintended movement or collision. If patient safety is affected, remove the system from clinical use and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Table Symptom
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: Table will not move; Table stops before reaching the expected position; Table limit message; Table height issue; Incorrect scan range; Patient positioning mismatch; Table indexing error; Gantry/table collision warning; Motion failure during PET, CT, or combined PET/CT acquisition. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Table and Gantry
steps.instructions: Inspect the table travel path, floor area, gantry opening, tabletop, table base, and accessory area. Look for stools, step platforms, cables, blankets, positioning aids, patient belongings, IV poles, or trash containers blocking movement. Expected outcome: The table path is clear and no external obstruction is limiting motion. If clearing an obstruction resolves the issue, verify normal table motion and stop.

steps.title: Check Patient Setup, Weight, and Positioning Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm that the patient is centered appropriately and that positioning devices are not interfering with gantry clearance or table travel. Check that arm supports, head holders, immobilizers, straps, cushions, radiation therapy positioning devices, and blankets are properly secured. Expected outcome: Patient setup does not create a clearance, balance, or movement restriction.

steps.title: Verify Table Load and Site Policy Limits
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient and accessories are within the system’s approved table load limits according to site documentation. Do not attempt repeated table movement if the system is struggling, stopping, or reporting limit errors under load. Expected outcome: The system is not being operated outside approved patient table conditions.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Motion Enable Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that no emergency stop button is pressed at the gantry, operator console, table controls, or room control area. Confirm that motion enable controls are being used correctly and that the system is not waiting for a required operator confirmation. Expected outcome: Table movement is not inhibited by an active emergency stop or motion safety condition.

steps.title: Inspect External Table Controls
steps.instructions: Check the table control buttons, hand control, gantry-side controls, and operator console controls for stuck buttons, physical damage, contamination, or inconsistent response. Test only when safe and with no patient risk. Expected outcome: The control source responds normally and no control appears stuck or damaged. If one control works but another does not, document the affected control and remove the system from service if clinical use depends on that control.

steps.title: Confirm the Table Is Properly Homed or Referenced
steps.instructions: Check whether the system is requesting a table home, reference, or initialization movement. Follow the normal operator-level table reset or homing workflow available to Clinical Engineering and site imaging staff. Expected outcome: The table position is recognized by the system and motion limits clear. If the table cannot home or reference correctly, stop and escalate.

steps.title: Review the Selected Exam Protocol and Scan Range
steps.instructions: Confirm the technologist selected the intended PET/CT protocol and patient orientation. Check whether the planned scan range exceeds table travel limits or conflicts with the current patient position. Expected outcome: The requested scan range is appropriate for the patient orientation, table position, and selected protocol.

steps.title: Check Patient Orientation and Registration Setup
steps.instructions: Verify head-first vs feet-first, supine vs prone, and PET/CT acquisition setup. Incorrect patient orientation can make table positioning appear wrong even when table motion is mechanically normal. Expected outcome: The table position and planned scan range match the intended clinical setup.

steps.title: Check for Gantry or Table Collision Warnings
steps.instructions: Review displayed system messages related to gantry clearance, table position, scan range, or collision protection. Confirm that no accessory, patient extremity, IV pole, oxygen tubing, or immobilization device is near the gantry path. Expected outcome: Collision protection is not being triggered by an external setup issue.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the table remains in a faulted state with no patient on the table, coordinate with imaging staff before performing an approved system restart or console reboot. Do not power cycle during active imaging, active patient positioning, or while the patient depends on the system. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software state faults may clear without internal repair.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check
steps.instructions: With no patient on the table, test basic table movement, height adjustment, in/out travel, and positioning response within normal safe limits. Confirm smooth movement, correct stopping, no abnormal sounds, and no repeated limit messages. Expected outcome: Table motion is normal and repeatable before the system is returned to service.

steps.title: Verify Clinical Readiness Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: Confirm with imaging staff that table movement, positioning, and scan range setup are functioning normally. If required by site policy, document any functional test or QC check performed after the table issue. Expected outcome: The system is safe for clinical use and the reported table issue is resolved.

CCR.Complaint: PET/CT technologist reported the Siemens Biograph mCT patient table stopped during positioning and displayed a table limit message before acquisition.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no obstruction, emergency stop, or accessory interference, but the table continued to report a positioning fault after restart and functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Biograph mCT from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented repeated table positioning failure, and escalated to Siemens service for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the issue occurred
Exact table error or limit message
PET, CT, or PET/CT workflow affected
Table height and in/out movement behavior
Whether emergency stop buttons were checked
Abnormal sounds, vibration, or jerky motion

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph mCT PET / CT System - Image reconstruction, acquisition computer, or syngo workstation fault
description: Troubleshooting PET/CT acquisition, reconstruction, host computer, syngo workstation, display, or workflow faults using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is currently on the PET/CT table or in the middle of an active scan. If acquisition, reconstruction, or workstation control becomes unreliable during patient care, stop nonessential troubleshooting and notify imaging staff immediately. Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on an unstable acquisition or reconstruction system. If the patient is still positioned in the scanner, prioritize safe table removal, scan cancellation per site procedure, and clinical handoff before technical troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: PET acquisition not starting; CT acquisition not starting; PET data acquired but not reconstructing; Reconstruction stuck, failed, or delayed; syngo workstation frozen or unresponsive; Patient/study information mismatch; Images not loading or displaying; Acquisition computer communication fault; Console error after startup, scan completion, or image transfer. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to acquisition, reconstruction, display, or workflow communication before checks begin.

steps.title: Check for Active Clinical Use or Pending Data
steps.instructions: Before restarting anything, ask staff whether a study is actively acquiring, reconstructing, transferring, or awaiting physician review. Do not power-cycle the workstation or acquisition computer if unsaved patient data may be at risk. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not cause avoidable loss of imaging data.

steps.title: Review the On-Screen Error Message
steps.instructions: Read and document the exact syngo, acquisition, reconstruction, or communication message. Take a photo or screenshot if allowed by site policy and if no protected patient information is exposed. Expected outcome: The fault is documented accurately for escalation instead of relying on a vague report such as “computer down.”

steps.title: Check Workstation Responsiveness
steps.instructions: Verify whether the mouse, keyboard, monitors, and user interface are responding. If only the mouse or keyboard is unresponsive, check the USB connections, docking hardware, KVM if used, and any loose peripheral cables. Expected outcome: A simple peripheral problem is corrected without unnecessary system restart. If the workstation responds normally after reconnecting a loose peripheral, verify normal function with staff and stop.

steps.title: Check Monitor and Display Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm all workstation monitors are powered on and showing the correct input. Check for loose video cables, power cables, or display power strips. Expected outcome: Blank-screen complaints are separated from actual workstation or acquisition computer failures. If the display returns and the workstation is functional, document the loose display or power connection and stop.

steps.title: Verify System Power and UPS Status
steps.instructions: Check that the syngo workstation, acquisition computer, reconstruction hardware, network equipment, monitors, and associated UPS units have power. Look for tripped power strips, unplugged cords, UPS alarms, bypass lights, low-battery indicators, or unusual beeping. Expected outcome: Power-related causes are ruled out before assuming a software or hardware failure. If a UPS is alarming, overheated, or not supporting the system correctly, remove the affected equipment from clinical use and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Network Connectivity Indicators
steps.instructions: Inspect visible network cables at the workstation, acquisition computer, network drop, switch, or equipment cabinet if accessible without disassembly. Confirm link lights are present where visible. Expected outcome: Obvious disconnected or failed network links are identified. If a network cable was disconnected and reconnecting it restores function, verify acquisition/reconstruction workflow with staff and stop.

steps.title: Confirm RIS/PACS and Modality Worklist Status
steps.instructions: Ask whether the workstation is receiving patient worklist data and whether completed studies can still be viewed or transferred. A worklist or archive issue may look like a workstation problem but may actually involve network, RIS, PACS, or DICOM communication. Expected outcome: The fault is separated from a broader hospital network or imaging-system issue.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Affects PET, CT, or Both
steps.instructions: Ask staff if CT acquisition works independently, PET acquisition works independently, or both are unavailable. If only PET reconstruction fails, focus on PET acquisition/reconstruction workflow. If only CT acquisition fails, the issue may be CT-side acquisition or scanner control related. If both fail, suspect shared workstation, host, network, power, or system communication issues. Expected outcome: The affected subsystem is identified before escalation.

steps.title: Review Recent System Events
steps.instructions: Ask whether anything changed shortly before the fault appeared, such as: Power outage; Network maintenance; PACS/RIS downtime; Software update; Restart or forced shutdown; Failed daily QC; Storage warning; Prior reconstruction delay; Room temperature or cooling concern. Expected outcome: Recent environmental or workflow changes are considered before assuming equipment failure.

steps.title: Check for Storage or Queue Problems
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether reconstruction, archive, or send queues are backed up. If available to Clinical Engineering without service-level access, review visible queue status for stuck studies or repeated failed jobs. Expected outcome: Workflow backlog is identified as a possible cause of delayed image availability. Do not delete studies, clear queues, or modify patient data unless directed by approved site procedure or vendor support.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the equipment room or console area is not unusually hot and that vents around computers or cabinets are not blocked. Listen for abnormal fan noise and check for unusual heat or odor. Expected outcome: Cooling or environmental concerns are identified before continued operation. If there is burning smell, excessive heat, smoke, or electrical odor, power down only if safe, remove from service, and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled User-Level Recovery if Safe
steps.instructions: If the workstation is responsive and no scan or reconstruction is actively running, have the technologist close only the affected application or workflow using normal software controls. Expected outcome: A minor application lockup clears without interrupting system data or hardware communication. If the application recovers and acquisition/reconstruction returns to normal, verify with a non-patient workflow or staff-approved test and stop.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the workstation or acquisition computer is frozen and staff confirm no active acquisition, reconstruction, or unsaved patient workflow is at risk, follow the site-approved shutdown/restart process. Do not hard-power-cycle the system unless normal shutdown is impossible and escalation guidance supports it. Expected outcome: The system restarts cleanly and communication with scanner, reconstruction, and storage components is restored. If normal operation returns, confirm with staff that the system is ready before clinical use resumes.

steps.title: Verify System Readiness After Recovery
steps.instructions: After any restart or recovery, confirm: syngo workstation opens normally; Patient browser or worklist is available; Acquisition interface loads; PET/CT scanner communication is normal; Reconstruction workflow is available; Prior study data is visible as expected; No recurring error messages appear. Expected outcome: The system is not returned to service based only on the desktop loading.

steps.title: Confirm Required QC or Readiness Checks
steps.instructions: Ask imaging staff whether PET daily QC, CT readiness checks, or site-required acceptance checks must be repeated after the fault or restart. Clinical Engineering should not override nuclear medicine or radiology quality requirements. Expected outcome: The system is returned to service only after the required clinical quality process is satisfied.

steps.title: Document and Monitor for Recurrence
steps.instructions: If the issue clears, document the symptom, action taken, and final system status. Ask staff to report whether the fault returns during the next acquisition, reconstruction, or transfer. Expected outcome: Intermittent workstation or acquisition faults are tracked instead of treated as isolated complaints.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Siemens Biograph mCT syngo workstation froze after PET/CT acquisition and the study would not reconstruct.
CCR.Cause: Workstation peripherals, power, UPS status, network cabling, display connections, and visible queue status were checked; no loose external connection was found, and the reconstruction fault returned after controlled restart.
CCR.Resolution: System was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Siemens service and imaging IT for acquisition/reconstruction system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the fault occurred
Exact syngo or acquisition error message
Alarm behavior or recurring workstation messages
Whether PET, CT, or both were affected
Whether reconstruction failed, stalled, or never started
Whether DICOM send or archive was also affected
Power behavior, UPS status, and alarms
Environmental factors such as recent power outage, network maintenance, software update, room heat, fan noise, smell, or shutdown behavior
Indicator lights on network, UPS, workstation, monitor, or equipment cabinet if visible

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph mCT PET / CT System - DICOM send, archive, RIS/PACS, or network communication failure
description: Troubleshooting PET/CT DICOM send, archive, RIS/PACS, worklist, and network communication failures using safe external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient exam is actively in progress. Do not interrupt an active PET/CT acquisition, reconstruction, or patient transfer unless directed by clinical leadership or imaging staff. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not disrupted by network troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Problem
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: DICOM send failure; Images stuck in queue; PACS archive unavailable; RIS worklist not populating; Study mismatch or accession issue; PET, CT, or fused image series not transferring; Workstation cannot reach archive; Network login or host communication error. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly defined before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify Whether the Issue Affects One Study or All Studies
steps.instructions: Check if only one patient/study failed to send or if all studies are failing. A single failed study may indicate demographics, accession, study status, or image series issue. Multiple failed studies may indicate network, destination, AE title, archive, or workstation communication failure. Expected outcome: The scope of the problem is identified.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Workstation or Console Messages
steps.instructions: Review the acquisition workstation, reconstruction workstation, and send/archive queue for error messages. Look for messages involving destination unavailable, association rejected, timeout, invalid AE title, network unreachable, or storage commitment failure. Expected outcome: The visible error helps narrow the fault before deeper checks.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Destination Was Selected
steps.instructions: Verify that the technologist selected the correct PACS, archive, MIM, syngo.via, nuclear medicine workstation, or destination node. Confirm whether the failed send was manual, automatic, or part of a routing rule. Expected outcome: The study is not being sent to the wrong destination. If the study sends successfully after selecting the correct destination, document the issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Whether Images Are Still Reconstructing
steps.instructions: Confirm that PET, CT, attenuation correction, fused, MIP, or secondary capture series have completed reconstruction before sending. Incomplete reconstruction may cause missing series or delayed transfer. Expected outcome: The study is complete enough to transmit. If the send completes after reconstruction finishes, document the delay and stop.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Study Demographics
steps.instructions: Compare the study information on the Biograph mCT workstation with RIS/PACS information. Check: Patient name; MRN; Accession number; Date of birth; Study description; Modality; Exam date and time. Expected outcome: The study identifiers match the order and archive expectations. If demographics are incorrect, involve imaging leadership, PACS support, or the appropriate correction workflow before resending.

steps.title: Check RIS Worklist Availability
steps.instructions: If the issue is worklist related, confirm whether new orders are visible on the PET/CT system. Ask staff whether other modalities can retrieve worklist from RIS. Expected outcome: You determine whether the issue is isolated to the PET/CT system or affects multiple imaging systems.

steps.title: Confirm Network Connectivity at the Workstation Area
steps.instructions: Check for obvious external network issues: Network cable connected; Cable not damaged; Wall jack not loose; Link lights present where visible; No recent cable moves or room work; No disconnected network switch or patch cable near the console. Expected outcome: Basic physical network connectivity is confirmed. If a loose or disconnected cable is found and reconnecting restores communication, test a send and document the resolution.

steps.title: Check for Power or Hardware Issues at Related Network Equipment
steps.instructions: If there is local network equipment in the room or console area, confirm it has power and does not show obvious fault indicators. Do not reset hospital network switches without approval from IT or the site network team. Expected outcome: No obvious local network equipment failure is found.

steps.title: Confirm Other Network Functions
steps.instructions: Check whether the workstation can perform other communication tasks, such as opening network resources, reaching connected imaging software, or sending to another approved destination. Expected outcome: You determine whether the failure is destination-specific or system-wide.

steps.title: Try Sending a Known Completed Study or Test Study if Site Policy Allows
steps.instructions: Use a completed non-active study or approved test workflow to confirm whether DICOM send is functioning. Do not create, alter, or send patient data outside approved site policy. Expected outcome: The send either completes successfully or confirms an active communication fault.

steps.title: Review the DICOM Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether studies are pending, failed, paused, or repeatedly retrying. A backed-up queue may delay new studies from sending. Expected outcome: Queue status is understood before repeat sends are attempted. If the queue is paused or blocked by one failed study, follow site procedure for clearing or reordering the queue.

steps.title: Check for Storage Destination Availability
steps.instructions: Ask PACS or imaging informatics whether the archive, router, or destination node is online. A PET/CT system may appear faulty when the receiving archive or DICOM router is unavailable. Expected outcome: Receiving-system availability is confirmed.

steps.title: Confirm No Recent IT, PACS, or RIS Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to: PACS server; DICOM router; RIS interface; IP address; AE title; Network VLAN; Firewall rule; Workstation replacement; Archive migration. Expected outcome: Recent infrastructure changes are identified as possible causes.

steps.title: Check Date and Time Consistency
steps.instructions: Confirm the Biograph mCT workstation date and time appear reasonable and match the site’s expected time zone. Significant time mismatch may affect worklist, study matching, authentication, or archive acceptance. Expected outcome: Date and time are not an obvious communication problem source.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Software Restart Only if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is active, no reconstruction is running, and imaging staff agrees, perform the approved workstation or application restart process. Do not power-cycle scanner hardware, gantry systems, or acquisition components during active clinical workflow unless directed by service or site policy. Expected outcome: Temporary workstation communication faults may clear. If communication restores after restart, perform a verified test send and document the action.

steps.title: Escalate to IT, PACS, or Siemens Support Based on Findings
steps.instructions: If the issue appears network-wide, involve IT. If the issue involves routing, archive rejection, worklist, or study matching, involve PACS/RIS support. If the Biograph mCT workstation, DICOM service, acquisition software, or scanner-side configuration appears faulty after external checks, escalate to Siemens service. Expected outcome: The correct support path is engaged without unnecessary internal repair attempts.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that Biograph mCT PET/CT studies were failing DICOM send to PACS after reconstruction completed.
CCR.Cause: DICOM queue showed multiple failed sends, and external checks confirmed the workstation was powered, network cable was connected, and the selected PACS destination was correct.
CCR.Resolution: Verified basic network connectivity, confirmed the issue was not limited to one study, notified PACS/IT for archive communication review, and left the system Out of Service for PET/CT archiving until communication was restored.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected one study or all studies
Whether PET, CT, and fused series were complete
Network cable and link light status
Any recent IT, PACS, RIS, or network changes

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph Horizon PET / CT System - PET Daily QC, Detector Calibration, or Normalization Failure
description: Troubleshooting PET daily QC, detector calibration, normalization, or PET readiness failures using safe external checks before service escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the PET/CT table or scheduled for immediate imaging on the affected system. If the issue is discovered during clinical workflow, stop PET imaging until QC readiness is confirmed acceptable. Expected outcome: No patient study is performed on a PET system with unresolved detector, calibration, or QC status concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact PET QC Failure
steps.instructions: Review the reported message at the acquisition workstation or PET system console. Identify whether the issue involves: Daily PET QC failure; Detector block or crystal issue; Normalization failure; Calibration not current; PET acquisition not ready; Failed blank scan, setup scan, or system check; Coincidence, count rate, or detector uniformity concern. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify the System Is Not in Clinical Use or Warm-Up Delay
steps.instructions: Confirm the PET/CT system has completed startup and is not still initializing PET electronics, gantry communication, or acquisition software. Allow the system to reach normal ready state before repeating QC. Expected outcome: QC is not attempted while the system is still booting, initializing, or recovering from a prior fault. If the system becomes ready and QC passes, document the startup timing issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Room and System Status Indicators
steps.instructions: Verify that the PET gantry, CT gantry, acquisition workstation, and operator console show normal ready status. Check for active error messages, warning icons, interlock messages, or environmental alerts. Expected outcome: The system status supports running PET QC without unresolved system warnings.

steps.title: Confirm Required QC Source Is Available and Correct
steps.instructions: Verify the correct PET QC source, phantom, or manufacturer-approved daily QC accessory is being used according to site procedure. Confirm the source is not expired, missing, damaged, incorrectly stored, or substituted with the wrong activity/source type. Expected outcome: QC is being performed with the correct external source or phantom required for the test. If the wrong or expired source is found, replace it with the correct approved source and repeat QC.

steps.title: Verify Source or Phantom Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the source or phantom is positioned exactly as required by the site’s PET QC workflow. Check for incorrect height, off-center placement, table position error, gantry misalignment, or use of the wrong holder. PET QC and normalization checks can fail if the system sees poor geometry or unexpected count distribution. Expected outcome: The source or phantom is centered, stable, and positioned correctly for the selected QC procedure. If placement correction resolves the QC failure, document the positioning issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Patient Table Position and Indexing
steps.instructions: Verify the table can move normally to the PET QC position and that no table indexing, range, or limit message is active. Confirm the tabletop is not blocked by accessories, straps, coils, pads, or objects in the bore. Expected outcome: The table reaches the expected QC position without obstruction or position mismatch.

steps.title: Inspect PET Bore and Table Area for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the PET/CT bore, table, source holder, and surrounding area. Remove loose objects, patient positioning devices, metal items, contaminated absorbent pads, or anything that could interfere with source geometry or table motion. Expected outcome: The QC field is clear and the source or phantom is the only intended object in the scan area.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct QC Workflow Was Selected
steps.instructions: Verify the operator selected the correct PET daily QC, detector calibration, normalization, or system setup procedure for the Biograph Horizon workflow. Make sure a PET QC procedure is not confused with CT calibration, PET/CT alignment, blank scan, normalization, or service-level calibration. Expected outcome: The selected workflow matches the reported requirement and site QC schedule.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, and Study/Protocol Context
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation date and time are correct. Check that the system is not attempting to use an outdated QC record, incorrect calibration file, or previous failed QC state. Expected outcome: The system’s QC status and timestamps are consistent with the current date and clinical schedule.

steps.title: Repeat QC One Time After Correcting External Issues
steps.instructions: After confirming correct source, positioning, table movement, workflow selection, and system ready status, repeat the PET daily QC or applicable calibration check one time. Expected outcome: The test completes successfully and the PET system returns to clinical-ready status. If QC passes, document what was corrected and return the system to service according to site policy.

steps.title: Review Failure Pattern
steps.instructions: If QC fails again, determine whether the same detector, normalization, count rate, or calibration message repeats. A repeated failure after correct setup suggests a true system issue rather than a user setup problem. Expected outcome: The fault pattern is identified for escalation.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Facility Concerns
steps.instructions: Verify there are no obvious room condition issues such as abnormal temperature, humidity, power disturbance, recent outage, water leak, unusual odor, or equipment overheating. PET detector and electronics stability can be affected by environmental or facility problems. Expected outcome: No external environmental condition explains the repeated QC failure.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart Only if Allowed by Site Policy
steps.instructions: If the system is not actively supporting patient care and site policy allows, perform a controlled restart of the affected acquisition workstation or system software workflow. Do not perform repeated power cycling or service-level resets. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues clear and QC can be repeated once. If QC passes after restart, document the restart and final successful QC result.

steps.title: Do Not Override QC Failure for Routine Clinical Use
steps.instructions: Do not return the PET/CT system to routine clinical use if PET daily QC, detector calibration, or normalization remains failed. Expected outcome: Clinical imaging is not performed with unresolved PET detector readiness or calibration status.

CCR.Complaint: PET technologist reported the Biograph Horizon failed daily PET QC and would not clear PET readiness for patient imaging.
CCR.Cause: Verified correct QC source was available, source placement was corrected, table indexing was normal, but PET daily QC failed again with a repeated detector calibration/normalization-related message.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PET/CT system from service, labeled unit Out of Service, notified imaging leadership, and escalated to Siemens service for PET detector and normalization evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or fault code displayed
Whether the issue was daily QC, detector calibration, normalization, or PET readiness
Bore and tabletop checked for obstructions
Any detector block, count rate, or uniformity message
Room temperature, humidity, power event, or facility concern

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph Horizon PET / CT System - CT Exposure Abort, Tube Warmup, Gantry, or Generator Fault
description: Troubleshooting CT exposure aborts, warmup interruptions, gantry faults, or generator-ready issues using safe external checks before service escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the PET/CT table or positioned inside the gantry. If the CT exposure abort, gantry fault, or generator issue occurs during an active exam, stop the scan and have clinical staff assess whether the patient can be safely removed or repositioned. Expected outcome: The patient is not left dependent on a system with unreliable CT exposure, gantry motion, or scan completion.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when the fault appeared. Identify whether the issue involves: CT exposure abort; Tube warmup failure; CT generator not ready; Gantry rotation fault; Table or gantry coordination issue; X-ray inhibit; Error during scout/topogram, helical scan, or CT attenuation correction; Fault after system startup or idle period. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check for Active System Messages
steps.instructions: Review the operator console for displayed fault messages, warning codes, tube heat messages, interlock warnings, gantry status messages, or generator-ready indicators. Do not clear or reset messages until they are documented. Expected outcome: The reported fault is captured accurately for repair escalation if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Exam Is Not in an Unsafe or Incomplete State
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the scan was aborted before, during, or after X-ray exposure. If patient images may be incomplete or improperly acquired, notify imaging staff so they can determine whether repeat imaging is clinically appropriate. Expected outcome: Clinical staff understand whether the exam was completed, partially completed, or failed.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: Inspect the PET/CT room for activated emergency stop buttons, gantry stop controls, table stop controls, or other safety switches. If an emergency stop is engaged, determine why it was activated before resetting anything. Expected outcome: The system is not being inhibited by an engaged safety stop, and any safety concern is addressed first.

steps.title: Inspect the Gantry Area for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Look around the CT gantry, patient table, tabletop, mattress, positioning accessories, straps, cables, IV lines, injector tubing, and floor area. Remove anything that could interfere with gantry clearance, table travel, or patient positioning. Expected outcome: No external object is blocking gantry operation, table motion, or scan range movement.

steps.title: Verify Patient Table Position and Scan Range Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is properly positioned, the patient is within the intended scan range, and the CT portion of the PET/CT study was planned correctly. Incorrect positioning or scan planning can appear like a system fault when the system is actually preventing an unsafe or invalid acquisition. Expected outcome: The CT scan range and table position are appropriate for the selected protocol.

steps.title: Check Room Door and Interlock Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the CT room door is fully closed and that any site-specific door, radiation warning, or room safety interlock is satisfied. Expected outcome: CT exposure is not being blocked by a room safety interlock.

steps.title: Confirm System Power and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Check that the Biograph Horizon system, operator console, CT gantry, acquisition workstation, and supporting equipment appear powered normally. Look for signs of power disturbance, tripped room power, UPS alarms, unusual heat, burning smell, or abnormal fan noise. Expected outcome: No obvious facility power or environmental issue is contributing to the fault.

steps.title: Verify Tube Warmup Requirements
steps.instructions: If the issue involves tube warmup, confirm whether the system had been powered off, idle for an extended time, recently restarted, or had heavy CT use. Follow the normal operator workflow for tube warmup only if the system allows it and no safety faults are present. Expected outcome: Tube warmup completes normally, or the failure is confirmed as repeatable. If tube warmup completes and CT exposure becomes available, return the system to clinical staff for appropriate image quality verification and stop.

steps.title: Allow Appropriate Cooling Time if Heat Is Indicated
steps.instructions: If the console indicates tube heat, generator heat, or cooling-related delay, allow the system to cool according to the displayed system guidance. Do not repeatedly force exposure attempts during heat-related inhibit conditions. Expected outcome: Exposure becomes available after cooling, or the heat/cooling fault remains and requires escalation.

steps.title: Check CT Protocol and Workflow Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected CT protocol is appropriate for the exam and that the workflow has not been left in an incomplete state from a prior failed acquisition. Have the technologist attempt a known appropriate non-patient workflow only if site policy allows and no patient safety issue exists. Expected outcome: The issue is not caused by an invalid protocol selection, incomplete exam state, or workflow mismatch.

steps.title: Confirm Injector or Accessory Status if Tied to the Exam
steps.instructions: If the CT acquisition depends on a contrast injector, respiratory monitor, ECG gating accessory, or other external device, verify that the accessory is ready or intentionally disabled for the protocol. A connected accessory fault may prevent the scan from proceeding. Expected outcome: External accessories are not causing the CT acquisition to abort or inhibit.

steps.title: Restart Only Through Approved Operator Workflow
steps.instructions: If the system appears locked in a software-ready state and no patient is involved, coordinate with imaging staff to perform an approved application logout/restart or system restart workflow. Avoid power cycling gantry or generator components at breakers unless directed by service procedure or vendor support. Expected outcome: A basic workstation or application lockup is cleared without unsafe equipment handling.

steps.title: Repeat a Safe Readiness Check
steps.instructions: After any reset, warmup, or workflow correction, confirm that the console, gantry, table, and CT acquisition status return to normal ready condition. If site policy allows, imaging staff may perform the appropriate daily QC, air calibration, or test workflow before returning the system to patient use. Expected outcome: CT readiness is restored and verified by the appropriate clinical imaging process.

steps.title: Stop if the Fault Repeats
steps.instructions: If the CT exposure abort, tube warmup failure, gantry fault, or generator fault returns after external checks, do not continue repeated exposure attempts. Repeated faults may indicate a gantry drive, generator, tube, cooling, detector timing, interlock, or system communication problem requiring service evaluation. Expected outcome: The device is removed from clinical use before repeated failures affect patient care.

CCR.Complaint: CT technologist reported the Biograph Horizon aborted CT exposure during the scout scan and displayed a generator-not-ready message.
CCR.Cause: Verified no patient was on the table, checked emergency stops, room door interlock, gantry clearance, scan setup, and tube warmup status; fault repeated during readiness check.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the PET/CT system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the displayed fault message, and escalated to Siemens service for CT generator and gantry evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the fault occurred
Exact console message or fault code
Whether the issue occurred during startup, warmup, scout, helical scan, or attenuation correction CT
Whether emergency stop buttons were checked
Whether room door and radiation interlocks were verified
Gantry indicator lights or unusual sounds
Whether tube warmup completed or failed
Whether the table and gantry moved normally
Any unusual heat, smell, vibration, or noise
Whether the fault cleared after approved restart

title: Siemens Healthineers Biograph Horizon PET / CT System - Patient Table Indexing, Movement, or Scan Range Positioning Issue
description: Troubleshooting patient table movement, indexing, scan range, table position, or bed travel issues using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the PET / CT table. If the table is not moving correctly, indexing unexpectedly, stopping during travel, or not aligning to the planned scan range, stop table movement immediately. Expected outcome: The patient is protected from unexpected motion, incomplete positioning, or unsafe table travel. If patient safety is affected, remove the patient from the system using the site’s approved procedure before continuing.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Table or Positioning Issue
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when it occurred. Identify whether the issue involves: Table will not move; Table movement stops during travel; Table indexing is inaccurate; Scan range does not match the planned anatomy; PET and CT alignment appears incorrect; Table height or longitudinal travel is limited; Patient positioning landmarks do not match acquisition setup; Error occurs during scout/topogram, CT acquisition, PET acquisition, or whole-body workflow. Expected outcome: The reported symptom is clearly defined before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check for Patient, Accessory, or Room Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the table travel path for anything blocking movement. Check for: Patient straps or sheets caught near the table; IV lines, oxygen tubing, monitoring cables, or injector tubing under tension; Table pads, positioning aids, arm supports, or head holders interfering with travel; Trash cans, step stools, chairs, cables, or accessories near the table base; Patient body habitus or positioning contacting the gantry opening. Expected outcome: The table has a clear travel path with no external obstruction. If removing an obstruction restores normal table movement, verify operation with staff and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Patient Is Properly Positioned
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is centered and positioned according to the planned exam. Check that arms, legs, blankets, immobilization aids, and positioning accessories do not extend into areas that may interfere with gantry clearance or table travel. Expected outcome: Patient positioning does not create a physical movement limitation or scan range error.

steps.title: Confirm Table Load and Weight Distribution
steps.instructions: Verify that the patient and accessories do not exceed the site-approved table loading limits. Also check that the patient is not positioned unusually far off-center or unsupported. Expected outcome: The table is not being asked to move with excessive or poorly distributed load. If table movement problems only occur with heavier patients or unusual positioning, remove the system from service until evaluated.

steps.title: Check the Table Control Inputs
steps.instructions: Test table movement using the normal operator controls when safe. Check whether the issue occurs from: Gantry controls; Console controls; Table-side controls; Footswitch or hand control, if used; Workflow-driven automatic table movement. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering can determine whether the problem follows one control location or affects all table movement. If only one external control is affected, inspect that control for damage, stuck buttons, contamination, or cable strain.

steps.title: Inspect Emergency Stop and Safety Stop Conditions
