Confirm whether the analyzer is processing patient samples or running active tests.

Do not interrupt a run unless the analyzer has stopped, alarmed, or laboratory policy requires stopping the system.

If patient testing is affected, notify lab leadership so specimens can be redirected, repeated, or held according to lab procedure.

Expected outcome: Patient results and specimen handling are protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Error or Alarm
steps.instructions:
Review the analyzer message, event log, or operator report.

Identify whether the issue involves:

Wash station error
Fluidics error
Waste full or waste not draining
Probe wash failure
Wash fluid empty or low
Liquid level sensing issue
Vacuum, pressure, or pump-related alarm
Repeated assay failures related to washing or aspiration

Expected outcome: The complaint is separated into supply, waste, wash station, or internal fluidics behavior.

steps.title: Check System Status Before Opening Covers
steps.instructions:
Confirm the analyzer is in a safe state for inspection according to lab workflow.

Look for active motion, sample handling, or reagent movement before accessing external fluid areas.

Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed only when the instrument is safe to inspect.

steps.title: Check Wash Fluid and System Fluids
steps.instructions:
Verify that required wash fluid containers are present, filled, properly connected, and not expired if applicable.

Confirm the pickup tubing is fully inserted and not floating above the fluid level.

Inspect for kinked, pinched, loose, or disconnected tubing near the fluid containers.

Expected outcome: The analyzer has an available fluid source and can draw from it properly.

If refilling, reseating, or correcting tubing resolves the error, clear the alarm if appropriate, verify normal operation, and stop.

steps.title: Check Waste Container Level and Connection
steps.instructions:
Inspect the waste container or waste drain connection.

Confirm the waste container is not full, the cap or tubing is properly connected, and the drain path is not blocked or kinked.

If connected to facility drain, verify the drain line is seated, not elevated incorrectly, and not obstructed.

Expected outcome: Waste fluid has a clear path away from the analyzer.

If correcting the waste container or drain line resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for External Leaks or Fluid Accumulation
steps.instructions:
Look around the analyzer base, fluid bottle area, waste area, and accessible tubing paths for leaks.

Check for wet surfaces, crystallized residue, dried reagent, unusual pooling, or fluid under the instrument.

Expected outcome: Any obvious external leak or spill condition is identified before further use.

If active leaking is found, remove the analyzer from service and escalate for repair or vendor evaluation.

steps.title: Check Tubing Routing and Obvious Restrictions
steps.instructions:
Inspect accessible fluid and waste tubing for:

Kinks
Pinch points
Loose fittings
Cracks
Collapsed tubing
Fluid residue around connectors
Tubing pulled tight after bottle replacement

Expected outcome: External flow restrictions are ruled out before suspecting internal pumps, valves, or sensors.

steps.title: Verify Containers and Caps Are Installed Correctly
steps.instructions:
Confirm wash, water, waste, and other fluid containers are installed in the correct locations.

Check that caps, probes, pickup lines, and waste fittings are fully seated.

Expected outcome: The analyzer is not alarming because of a mispositioned bottle, cap, or fluid pickup assembly.

steps.title: Check for Recent Operator Changes
steps.instructions:
Ask the laboratory staff whether the error started after:

Replacing wash fluid
Emptying waste
Moving tubing
Cleaning the wash area
Loading reagents
Performing daily maintenance
Power cycling the analyzer

Expected outcome: The issue is linked, if possible, to a recent handling or maintenance step.

steps.title: Review Required Operator Maintenance Status
steps.instructions:
Confirm with lab staff that routine analyzer maintenance has been completed, including wash station cleaning, probe cleaning, waste handling, and any manufacturer-required daily or periodic maintenance.

Clinical Engineering should not take over assay maintenance unless trained and authorized, but missed maintenance can explain fluidics and wash errors.

Expected outcome: Maintenance-related causes are considered before escalating to hardware repair.

steps.title: Inspect the Wash Station Area Externally
steps.instructions:
Look for visible obstruction, dried residue, debris, misplaced parts, or fluid buildup around the wash station area.

Do not perform deep disassembly.

Expected outcome: External contamination or obstruction around the wash station is ruled out.

If cleaning by authorized lab staff resolves the issue, verify the analyzer returns to normal operation and stop.

steps.title: Restart or Reset Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions:
If allowed by lab policy and no specimens are at risk, have the operator perform the appropriate analyzer reset, prime, or startup sequence.

Watch whether the same wash, fluidics, or waste error returns during initialization.

Expected outcome: A temporary software or fluid prime issue is separated from a repeatable mechanical or fluidics failure.

steps.title: Run Available System Checks or Diagnostics
steps.instructions:
Use available operator-level or service-accessible checks according to site policy and authorization.

Useful checks may include fluidics prime, wash station check, waste check, liquid handling check, or related system diagnostics.

Expected outcome: Diagnostics either pass and support returning the analyzer to service, or fail and justify escalation.

steps.title: Verify Operation With Laboratory Control Process
steps.instructions:
After correcting an external cause, have lab staff follow their required quality control or verification process before patient testing resumes.

Clinical Engineering should not release an analyzer for patient testing based only on visual inspection if assay performance may be affected.

Expected outcome: Laboratory quality requirements are met before patient samples are processed.

steps.title: Stop if the Error Repeats
steps.instructions:
If the same wash station, fluidics, or waste error returns after fluids, waste, tubing, connections, and maintenance status are checked, stop troubleshooting.

Repeated errors may involve internal pumps, valves, sensors, wash station alignment, vacuum/pressure control, blocked internal fluid paths, or analyzer service issues.

Expected outcome: The device is not repeatedly placed back into service with an unresolved fluidics fault.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported an ADVIA Centaur XP/XPT wash station error during sample processing, with the analyzer unable to continue testing.
CCR.Cause: Waste container connection was found loose and the waste line was partially kinked, preventing proper drainage from the analyzer.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the waste connection, corrected the waste line routing, confirmed no external leaks, and had lab staff complete required system checks before returning the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or alarm code
Whether patient samples were active at the time
Waste tubing or drain line inspected
Tubing checked for kinks or leaks
Any visible fluid, residue, or unusual odor
Whether maintenance had recently been performed
Whether prime, reset, or diagnostic checks passed
Whether QC or lab verification was completed

title: Siemens Healthineers ADVIA Centaur XP / XPT Series Immunoassay Analyzer - Calibration, QC, or Assay Recovery Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed calibration, failed QC, or poor assay recovery by checking reagents, consumables, sample handling, and analyzer status first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether patient testing is currently being performed on the ADVIA Centaur XP / XPT. If calibration or QC is failing, do not allow affected assays to be used for patient result reporting until QC acceptance criteria are met. Expected outcome: Patient results are not released from an assay system that has not passed required quality checks.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Failure
steps.instructions: Review the reported issue with the laboratory staff. Determine whether the problem involves: Calibration failure; QC failure; Poor assay recovery; One specific assay failing; Multiple assays failing; Intermittent QC shifts; Reagent pack issue; Recent lot change; Recent maintenance or service activity. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a specific assay, reagent lot, calibration event, or system-wide performance concern.

steps.title: Check Assay Status Before Making Changes
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer status, assay status, reagent status, and any active error messages. Confirm whether the affected assay is disabled, expired, out of calibration, missing required reagent, or flagged by the system. Expected outcome: Obvious software, reagent, or assay status issues are identified before deeper troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify Reagent Pack Condition and Loading
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct reagent pack is loaded for the affected assay. Check that the reagent is not expired, empty, improperly seated, or outside onboard stability limits. Inspect for visible issues such as leakage, foam, bubbles, clots, or improper mixing if observable without opening internal assemblies. Expected outcome: The analyzer has valid, properly loaded reagent available for the affected assay. If replacing or reseating the reagent resolves the issue and QC passes, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Calibrator and QC Material Handling
steps.instructions: Verify that calibrators and QC materials are correct for the assay and lot in use. Check expiration dates, storage conditions, reconstitution requirements, thawing requirements, mixing, and time at room temperature. Confirm the correct material was placed in the correct sample position or rack location. Expected outcome: Calibration or QC failure is not caused by expired, mishandled, or misidentified material.

steps.title: Check Sample Cups, Racks, and Volumes
steps.instructions: Confirm that the calibrator, QC, or recovery sample has sufficient volume for the requested test. Inspect sample cups or tubes for bubbles, foam, clots, fibrin, evaporation, or incorrect cup type. Expected outcome: The analyzer can aspirate a valid sample without volume or sample integrity problems.

steps.title: Review Recent Lot or Assay Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the failure started after a new reagent lot, calibrator lot, QC lot, assay file update, or calibration adjustment. Confirm that the lot information entered in the system matches the physical reagent, calibrator, and QC material being used. Expected outcome: Lot mismatch or incorrect assay setup is ruled out before suspecting analyzer hardware.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Utility Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that the analyzer area is stable and appropriate for operation. Check for obvious environmental concerns such as room temperature extremes, recent power events, vibration, nearby construction, or liquid spills. Confirm the analyzer is connected to proper power and that any required UPS or power conditioning equipment is operating normally. Expected outcome: Assay recovery problems are not being caused by unstable external conditions.

steps.title: Review Maintenance Status
steps.instructions: Check whether daily, weekly, or scheduled maintenance is overdue. Confirm that routine cleaning, probe care, cuvette or waste handling, wash fluid checks, and system preparation steps were completed according to site procedure. Expected outcome: Calibration or QC failure is not related to missed routine maintenance.

steps.title: Check Consumables and Fluids
steps.instructions: Verify that required fluids, wash solutions, water supply, waste containers, cuvettes, tips, and other consumables are present and within acceptable status. Look for low fluid warnings, waste full warnings, blocked tubing indicators, or supply-related system messages. Expected outcome: The analyzer has the required fluids and consumables to perform accurate aspiration, washing, incubation, and detection.

steps.title: Repeat Calibration Only After Correcting Obvious Causes
steps.instructions: If the issue appears related to a specific assay, reagent pack, calibrator, or QC material, correct that issue before repeating calibration. Run calibration according to laboratory procedure using valid materials. Expected outcome: Calibration completes successfully and the assay becomes available for QC testing. If calibration passes and QC is acceptable, return the affected assay to service per laboratory policy and stop.

steps.title: Run QC After Calibration
steps.instructions: After successful calibration, have the laboratory run required QC levels for the affected assay. Confirm whether QC results fall within the laboratory’s established acceptable range. Expected outcome: The assay is verified before patient testing resumes. If QC passes, the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Compare Scope of Failure
steps.instructions: If QC or recovery still fails, determine whether the issue is limited to one assay or affects multiple assays. If one assay is affected, suspect assay-specific reagent, calibrator, QC, lot, or assay file issues. If multiple unrelated assays are affected, suspect a broader analyzer, sample handling, fluidics, wash, incubation, detection, or environmental problem. Expected outcome: The failure is classified as assay-specific or system-wide before escalation.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Analyzer Performance Clues
steps.instructions: Listen and observe during operation from a safe external position. Note unusual sounds, repeated sample aspiration errors, probe errors, wash errors, reagent handling issues, cuvette handling problems, or recurring system alarms. Expected outcome: Any external signs of mechanical, fluidic, or detection-related failure are documented for service.

steps.title: Do Not Release Patient Results Until QC Is Acceptable
steps.instructions: If calibration, QC, or assay recovery remains unacceptable, keep the affected assay unavailable for patient reporting. Do not override failed QC or release results from an affected assay unless laboratory leadership follows approved downtime or exception policy. Expected outcome: Patient result integrity is protected.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported repeated QC failures on the ADVIA Centaur XPT TSH assay after calibration.
CCR.Cause: Verified affected assay had a recently changed reagent lot; reagent pack and QC material were checked, and QC remained out of range after repeat calibration.
CCR.Resolution: Removed affected assay from patient reporting, documented reagent and QC lot information, and escalated analyzer for Siemens service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: QC direction of failure, high or low
Sample cup or rack issues found
Recent lot, assay file, or maintenance changes
Whether one assay or multiple assays were affected

title: Siemens Healthineers ADVIA Centaur XP / XPT Series Immunoassay Analyzer - LIS Communication, Result Transmission, or Host Interface Fault
description: Troubleshooting LIS communication, host interface, result transmission, network, cable, or configuration-related faults before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety and Result Integrity First
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is not in the middle of releasing urgent patient results. If testing is active, coordinate with laboratory staff before restarting, disconnecting cables, changing network settings, or clearing interface errors. Expected outcome: Patient results are protected, and no active testing or critical reporting workflow is interrupted.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Results not transmitting to the LIS; Orders not downloading from the LIS; Host communication offline; Intermittent result transmission; Results stuck in pending or transmit queue; Only certain tests or samples failing to transmit; Analyzer communication fault after a power outage, network change, or LIS downtime. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into outbound results, inbound orders, full host communication loss, or intermittent interface failure.

steps.title: Verify the Analyzer Is Safe to Evaluate
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the analyzer is processing samples, paused, in standby, or idle. Do not interrupt sample processing unless the laboratory confirms it is acceptable. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed at an appropriate time without risking sample loss or reporting delay.

steps.title: Check Whether the LIS or Host System Is Available
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff or IT whether the LIS is online and whether other analyzers are communicating normally. If multiple instruments are affected, the issue is more likely LIS, middleware, network, or interface engine related. Expected outcome: A site-wide host issue is ruled out before focusing on the ADVIA Centaur analyzer.

steps.title: Check the Physical Network Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ethernet cable at the analyzer and wall/network port. Confirm the cable is fully seated, not damaged, and connected to the correct network jack. Look for link/activity lights at the analyzer network port or connected switch port if visible. Expected outcome: The analyzer has a stable physical network connection. If reseating or replacing the network cable restores communication, verify result transmission with the lab and stop.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Network Cable and Port if Available
steps.instructions: Swap the Ethernet cable with a known-good cable approved for the lab network. If permitted by IT, test the analyzer on a known-good network jack assigned to the same instrument network. Expected outcome: Cable or wall-jack failure is ruled out. If communication works on a different cable or jack, document the finding and escalate the network drop issue to IT or facilities as appropriate.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether anything changed recently, including: LIS downtime; Middleware update; Network switch replacement; IP address change; Analyzer software update; Power outage; Analyzer relocation; Firewall or VLAN change; Host interface configuration change. Expected outcome: The communication issue is tied to a recent change when possible.

steps.title: Confirm Analyzer Network Settings
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer’s network or host communication settings with appropriate authorization. Confirm the analyzer has the expected: IP address; Subnet mask; Gateway, if used; Host/LIS destination address; Port settings; Communication protocol settings; Instrument ID or host identifier. Expected outcome: Analyzer communication settings match the LIS or middleware interface requirements. If settings are incorrect, coordinate correction with the laboratory, IT, LIS analyst, or Siemens support. Do not guess interface values.

steps.title: Check Host Interface Status on the Analyzer
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer status screen, communication menu, event log, or host interface messages. Look for indications such as host offline, transmission failed, connection timeout, rejected result, queued results, or communication error. Expected outcome: The analyzer provides a specific communication symptom that can guide the next step.

steps.title: Determine Whether Results Are Queued
steps.instructions: Check whether completed results are waiting in a transmit queue or pending host communication. If results are queued, confirm whether retransmission is available through the normal operator workflow. Expected outcome: Results are not lost, and pending transmissions are identified before resets or service actions. If retransmission succeeds, have lab staff verify receipt in the LIS and stop.

steps.title: Verify Whether Orders Are Downloading
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves order download failure, test whether new orders are reaching the analyzer. Confirm with laboratory staff that the order exists in the LIS and is being routed to the correct analyzer or middleware destination. Expected outcome: The issue is separated from barcode, accessioning, specimen routing, or LIS order-entry problems.

steps.title: Check Whether Only Certain Results Fail
steps.instructions: If most results transmit but some do not, review whether the failed results share a pattern. Common patterns may include: Reflex testing; Dilution results; Repeat results; Canceled tests; Result flags; QC or calibration data; Specific assay codes; Specific sample IDs. Expected outcome: A mapping, assay code, LIS rule, or middleware issue is considered before assuming analyzer hardware failure.

steps.title: Review Analyzer Event Logs
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer logs for communication-related events at the time of failure. Note exact error wording, time stamps, frequency, and whether the fault is continuous or intermittent. Expected outcome: The work order includes objective fault evidence instead of only “LIS down” or “not sending.”

steps.title: Check Power and Restart Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer and any associated workstation, data manager, or interface device are powered normally. If a restart is required, coordinate with laboratory leadership and follow approved site procedure. Expected outcome: Communication services are not restarted during active testing or without lab approval. If communication returns after an approved restart, verify result transmission and document the action.

steps.title: Involve IT or LIS Support When Needed
steps.instructions: If the analyzer appears powered, network-connected, and properly configured but cannot reach the host, involve IT or LIS support. Ask them to verify: Network port status; IP reachability; Interface engine status; Firewall rules; LIS/middleware listener status; Message rejection logs; Instrument routing configuration. Expected outcome: Network and host-side causes are evaluated before replacing analyzer parts.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Transmission Verification
steps.instructions: With laboratory approval, have staff send or retransmit a test result, control result, or approved non-patient verification transaction. Confirm whether the result appears in the LIS or middleware. Expected outcome: Communication recovery is verified end-to-end, not only by clearing an error message. If the result is received correctly, document the successful verification and return the analyzer to normal use.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the ADVIA Centaur XPT was completing immunoassay testing but patient results were not transmitting to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Ethernet connection and analyzer power were normal, but completed results were queued and the host interface showed communication timeout errors after LIS downtime.
CCR.Resolution: Coordinated with LIS/IT, verified host listener was restored, retransmitted queued results with lab approval, and confirmed results were received in the LIS.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected results, orders, or both
Whether other analyzers were also affected
Exact error codes or event log messages
Whether results were queued or rejected
Ethernet cable and wall jack condition
Whether electronic transmission was verified successfully

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System - Detector Head Motion, Positioning, Or Proximity Sensor Fault
description: Troubleshooting detector head motion, positioning, collision avoidance, or proximity sensor faults using safe external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is not in contact with the detector heads, table, gantry, or CT bore area. If the fault occurs during an active exam, have clinical staff stop motion, keep the patient still, and follow site safety procedures before moving the system. Expected outcome: The patient is protected from unintended detector, table, or gantry movement.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Motion or Positioning Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what motion failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Detector head will not move; Detector head stops during positioning; Detector head position is inaccurate; Proximity or collision warning appears; Detector head will not move close to patient; Gantry rotation or detector orbit stops; Table and detector alignment issue; Intermittent fault during setup or acquisition. Expected outcome: The problem is separated into motion control, positioning accuracy, proximity sensing, or operator workflow.

steps.title: Stop and Inspect for Obvious Obstructions
steps.instructions: Look around the detector heads, patient table, gantry opening, and floor area. Check for linens, straps, IV poles, cables, patient belongings, step stools, positioning aids, or anything blocking detector travel. Expected outcome: No external object is interfering with detector or gantry movement. If an obstruction is found and safely removed, retest motion only when the area is clear. If normal motion returns, document the obstruction and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Patient and Accessories Are Positioned Correctly
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is centered and not extending unexpectedly into detector travel paths. Check that arm boards, head holders, cushions, straps, or immobilization devices are properly secured and not triggering proximity sensors. Expected outcome: Positioning accessories are not falsely creating a collision or proximity condition.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Motion Disable Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that all emergency stop buttons are released. Check the console and system indicators for emergency stop, interlock, or motion-disabled messages. Expected outcome: Motion is not being blocked by an active safety stop or interlock condition. If an emergency stop was active, reset according to site procedure and verify the system returns to a safe ready state before testing motion.

steps.title: Review the Displayed Error or Warning Message
steps.instructions: Record the exact fault message, code, affected axis, detector head, or proximity sensor description shown at the console. Do not clear the message before capturing useful details for service documentation. Expected outcome: The fault information is preserved for troubleshooting and escalation.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is at risk and the exam workflow allows it, perform an approved system restart or application restart according to site procedure. Do not repeatedly reset the system to bypass a motion or collision safety fault. Expected outcome: Temporary software or initialization issues may clear, but recurring safety faults are not ignored.

steps.title: Test Detector Motion Without a Patient Present
steps.instructions: With the room clear and the detector travel area unobstructed, have qualified staff attempt basic detector positioning or home/reference movement. Observe whether the detector head moves smoothly, stops immediately, moves unevenly, or faults at a repeatable position. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified without risking patient contact. If motion works normally with the patient and accessories removed, the original issue may have been positioning, accessory, or proximity-related.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Mechanical Binding
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the detector head area, covers, rails, cable guides, and accessible motion paths for impact damage, loose covers, abnormal gaps, or signs of collision. Listen for unusual grinding, clicking, scraping, or motor strain during safe test movement. Expected outcome: No obvious external damage or mechanical interference is present. If unusual noise, binding, impact damage, or burning smell is present, stop testing and remove the system from service.

steps.title: Check Proximity Sensor Areas
steps.instructions: Inspect the detector face, proximity sensing surfaces, covers, and surrounding areas for debris, tape, cleaning residue, cracked covers, fluid contamination, or objects near the sensing zone. Expected outcome: Proximity sensors are not being blocked, contaminated, or falsely triggered by an external condition.

steps.title: Confirm Room and Table Conditions
steps.instructions: Check whether the patient table position, table height, gantry angle, detector configuration, or scan protocol is preventing motion. Some positioning faults may occur when the system is outside the allowed movement range or when detector motion is restricted by the selected workflow. Expected outcome: The system is being moved within its expected safe operating range.

steps.title: Attempt the Motion from Standard Operator Controls
steps.instructions: Verify whether the fault occurs from the gantry controls, table controls, hand control, or software console. Compare whether one control path fails while another works. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a control input, motion axis, detector head, or system-level fault.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Is Repeatable
steps.instructions: Determine whether the detector stops at the same position, same angle, same distance from the patient, or same point in the acquisition setup. Expected outcome: A repeatable fault suggests a sensor, encoder, calibration, axis, or mechanical limit issue rather than random operator error.

steps.title: Review Recent Events
steps.instructions: Ask whether the system recently had: A detector or gantry collision; Room cleaning or floor work; Service activity; Power interruption; Failed calibration or quality control; New accessories or positioning devices introduced; Repeated proximity warnings on specific exam types. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified before assuming component failure.

steps.title: Run Only Approved Operator-Level Checks
steps.instructions: If available to Clinical Engineering and allowed by site policy, review system status, error logs, and available motion/proximity diagnostics. Do not bypass safety circuits, defeat proximity sensors, open motion assemblies, or perform internal detector adjustments without proper service authorization. Expected outcome: External and software-visible causes are checked without compromising safety systems.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Symbia Intevo detector head stopped during patient positioning and displayed a proximity or motion fault.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no patient contact, obstruction, emergency stop condition, or accessory interference, and the detector fault repeated during room-clear motion testing.
CCR.Resolution: System was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, fault details were documented, and Siemens service was requested for detector motion/proximity system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector head involved, if known
Whether a patient was on the table
Whether motion stopped during setup, orbit, acquisition, or homing
Whether the issue occurs at the same position each time
Unusual sounds, vibration, heat, or smell

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System - Collimator Recognition, Seating, Or Exchange Issue
description: Troubleshooting collimator recognition, seating, exchange, lock, or automated collimator changer issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Intevo is not actively being used for a patient scan. If the issue occurs during setup for a clinical study, have Nuclear Medicine staff stop the exchange attempt and use approved downtime workflow or another available imaging system if needed. Expected outcome: No patient is scanned or positioned while collimator seating or recognition is uncertain.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Collimator Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what occurred during the collimator exchange. Determine whether the issue involves: Collimator not recognized; Wrong collimator displayed; Collimator exchange will not start; Collimator exchange stops mid-process; Collimator appears seated but system reports an error; Collimator door, bed storage, or changer movement issue; One detector affected or both detectors affected; Issue occurs with one collimator set or all collimators. Expected outcome: The failure is separated into a recognition, seating, exchange, or mechanical movement concern.

steps.title: Check the System State Before Reattempting Exchange
steps.instructions: Verify the system is idle and not in an acquisition, QC, calibration, reconstruction, or patient workflow. Confirm there are no active motion warnings, emergency stop conditions, gantry collision warnings, or room safety interlocks. Expected outcome: The system is in a safe state where collimator exchange can be evaluated.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: From outside the system, look for anything blocking the collimator changer path, detector movement, bed storage area, or exchange area. Check for linens, positioning aids, straps, phantoms, patient accessories, or loose items left near the detectors or table. Expected outcome: No external object is preventing the collimator from moving, seating, or locking. If an obstruction is found and safely removed, reattempt the exchange according to normal operator workflow. If the exchange completes and the collimator is recognized correctly, stop.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Collimator Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm the collimator being selected or exchanged matches the intended study protocol. Verify staff are not attempting to use a mismatched or unsupported collimator for the selected acquisition. Symbia Intevo systems may use automated collimator changer functionality, which Siemens describes as integrated collimator storage for two collimator sets in the system bed. Expected outcome: The selected collimator, stored collimator position, and study protocol are consistent.

steps.title: Check for Improper Seating or Incomplete Locking
steps.instructions: If safe to access, visually confirm the collimator appears fully seated and evenly aligned. Look for uneven gaps, tilted positioning, incomplete engagement, or signs that the collimator did not fully lock into place. Do not force the collimator or attempt to defeat mechanical locks. Expected outcome: The collimator is either clearly seated correctly or the issue is identified as a seating or locking problem.

steps.title: Check the Collimator Exchange Workflow
steps.instructions: Have trained Nuclear Medicine staff repeat the normal collimator exchange process only if the area is clear and the system allows it. Watch for: Error message timing; Movement stopping early; Unusual motor noise; Changer hesitation; Detector not aligning correctly; Collimator not reaching final seated position. Expected outcome: The failure point is identified without bypassing normal safety controls.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the system software appears to be holding an old recognition state or the exchange status does not clear, perform a controlled restart only if permitted by site procedure and the system is not in clinical use. Do not power cycle during active acquisition, image reconstruction, or patient positioning. Expected outcome: Temporary software state or communication errors clear after restart. If the collimator is recognized correctly after restart and exchange completes normally, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Collimator Position or Set If Available
steps.instructions: If the issue appears limited to one collimator set or storage position, compare behavior with another known-good collimator or position using normal operator workflow. Do not swap components beyond approved external handling. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to one collimator, one storage position, one detector side, or the overall changer/recognition system.

steps.title: Review System Error Messages and Logs
steps.instructions: Record the exact system message, code, detector side, collimator type, and when the fault occurred. Note whether the error appears during exchange start, movement, seating, lock confirmation, or recognition. Expected outcome: Repair escalation has useful fault detail instead of a general “collimator problem” note.

steps.title: Check for Signs of Mechanical or Safety Risk
steps.instructions: Stop troubleshooting immediately if there is: Grinding, binding, or repeated motor strain; Visible collimator misalignment; Dropped, damaged, or impacted collimator; Incomplete lock indication; Unusual vibration; Burning smell, heat, or electrical odor; Repeated failed exchange attempts. Expected outcome: The device is not operated in a condition that could damage the system or create a patient/staff safety hazard.

steps.title: Verify Final Recognition Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: If the issue is resolved, confirm the system correctly identifies the installed collimator and allows the intended acquisition setup. Confirm Nuclear Medicine staff are satisfied that the correct collimator is displayed for the selected study. Expected outcome: The system is returned to service only after collimator recognition and seating are verified.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Symbia Intevo would not recognize the selected LEHR collimator after an attempted exchange.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the collimator exchange path clear, but the collimator did not reach a confirmed seated and locked state during normal exchange workflow.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Symbia Intevo from service, labeled the system Out of Service, recorded the collimator error message and detector side, and escalated to Siemens service for collimator changer evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether one or both collimators are affected
Whether one storage position is affected
Whether the exchange starts, stops, or never initiates
Any visible misalignment or uneven seating
Alarm behavior, motion warnings, or lock indication behavior
Accessories, positioning aids, linens, straps, or phantoms removed from the exchange area
Power behavior before and after any controlled restart
Environmental factors near the gantry, detector heads, table, or collimator storage area
Indicator lights, detector-side messages, or system status indicators
Whether the issue cleared after controlled restart
Whether the final device status is in service or out of service

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System - SPECT Daily QC, Uniformity, or Center-of-Rotation Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed SPECT daily QC, uniformity, center-of-rotation, detector, phantom, or acquisition setup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Intevo is not being used for an active patient study. If daily QC, uniformity, or center-of-rotation testing fails before clinical use, do not release the system for patient imaging until QC status is resolved according to site policy. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not performed on a system with questionable SPECT image quality.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact QC Failure
steps.instructions: Review the failed QC result and determine whether the issue involves: Daily SPECT QC failure; Flood uniformity failure; Intrinsic or extrinsic uniformity issue; Center-of-rotation failure; Detector head mismatch; Unexpected count rate issue; Energy window or photopeak issue; Motion or gantry-related failure during acquisition; Processing or workstation error during QC review. Expected outcome: The failure is identified before repeating tests or requesting service.

steps.title: Verify the Correct QC Procedure Was Used
steps.instructions: Confirm the technologist used the correct daily QC workflow for the Symbia system and selected the correct detector, collimator, isotope, and protocol. Siemens provides a Symbia QC schedule job aid for recommended nuclear medicine quality control scheduling across Symbia scanners. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by the wrong QC test, wrong protocol, or missed required setup step. If this resolves the issue, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Check Source, Phantom, and Count Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the correct flood source, point source, or phantom was used for the specific QC test being performed. Confirm the source activity, source placement, distance, orientation, and expiration or decay status are appropriate for the procedure. Expected outcome: QC failure is not due to low activity, poor positioning, wrong source type, or improper phantom setup. If this resolves the issue, repeat QC and stop.

steps.title: Check Collimator Selection and Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the expected collimator is installed for the QC procedure. Inspect that the collimator is fully seated, latched, and recognized by the system if applicable. Look for obvious external damage, contamination, or incorrect collimator use. Expected outcome: The detector is being tested with the correct and secure collimator configuration. If this resolves the issue, repeat QC and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Detector Face and External Surfaces
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the detector face, collimator surface, and phantom/source area for contamination, fluid, adhesive residue, physical obstruction, or shielding material. Do not disassemble the detector or access internal detector components. Expected outcome: External contamination or obstruction is ruled out as a cause of nonuniformity or count loss. If this resolves the issue, clean according to approved site procedure, repeat QC, and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Room and System Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the room is clear of unexpected radioactive sources, recent spills, contaminated linen, radioactive waste, or high activity patients near the detector during QC. Confirm the system had adequate warm-up time if required by local procedure. Expected outcome: Background activity, contamination, or unstable operating conditions are not affecting QC results. If this resolves the issue, repeat QC after the room condition is corrected and stop.

steps.title: Review Detector and Gantry Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the detector heads, table, gantry, and source/phantom are positioned correctly for the selected QC test. For center-of-rotation testing, verify the point source or phantom is positioned according to the site’s approved procedure and is not shifted, tilted, or moving during acquisition. Expected outcome: COR failure is not caused by mechanical setup error or incorrect source alignment. If this resolves the issue, repeat QC and stop.

steps.title: Check for Motion, Collision, or Mechanical Interruption
steps.instructions: Ask whether the detector head, gantry, table, or source moved unexpectedly during the QC acquisition. Check for interrupted motion, limit messages, collision warnings, emergency stop activation, or gantry/table positioning errors. Expected outcome: The QC failure is separated from a mechanical motion or positioning problem. If mechanical motion is abnormal, remove the system from clinical use and escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm Acquisition Completed Normally
steps.instructions: Review whether the QC acquisition completed without aborting, freezing, or generating system errors. Check for workstation messages, detector communication errors, acquisition software faults, or processing failures. Expected outcome: The failure is separated into image quality failure versus acquisition or processing failure. If the acquisition or processing workflow failed, escalate as a workstation, communication, or acquisition subsystem issue.

steps.title: Repeat QC Once After Correcting External Causes
steps.instructions: After correcting any setup, source, phantom, room, or positioning issue, repeat the failed QC test once. Do not repeatedly rerun failed QC in an attempt to “pass” the system without identifying a cause. Expected outcome: A corrected external issue results in a valid passing QC result. If this resolves the issue, document the cause and final QC status.

steps.title: Compare With Recent QC History
steps.instructions: Review recent daily QC, uniformity, and center-of-rotation trends if available. Look for gradual degradation, sudden failure after service, detector head differences, repeat failures on the same head, or failures tied to a specific collimator or protocol. Expected outcome: The issue is recognized as either an isolated setup problem or a repeatable system performance concern.

steps.title: Check CT QC Status If Hybrid Imaging Is Involved
steps.instructions: If the complaint involves SPECT/CT alignment, hybrid workflow, or CT-related correction, verify CT daily QC/checkup status per site procedure. EANM guidance notes that daily CT QC for SPECT / CT Systems should be performed according to manufacturer-recommended procedures. Expected outcome: SPECT QC concerns are not confused with CT QC, attenuation correction, or hybrid alignment concerns. If CT QC also fails, remove the system from clinical use and escalate appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported that the Siemens Symbia Intevo failed SPECT daily QC with a uniformity/COR failure before patient imaging.
CCR.Cause: Checked QC setup, source positioning, room contamination concerns, collimator seating, detector positioning, and acquisition workflow; failure repeated after external causes were ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Symbia Intevo from clinical use, labeled the system Out of Service, documented failed QC behavior, and escalated for Siemens service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any gantry, table, or detector motion errors
Any workstation or acquisition error messages
CT QC status if hybrid imaging was involved

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System - CT Tube, Gantry, Exposure Abort, or Reconstruction Fault
description: Troubleshooting CT tube, gantry motion, exposure abort, or CT reconstruction faults on a Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Symbia Intevo is actively being used for a patient study. If the fault occurs during a patient exam, have clinical staff stop the scan according to site procedure and remove the patient from the scanner only when safe. Expected outcome: The patient is not left in the bore or on the table while equipment status is uncertain. If patient care or imaging workflow is at risk, stop troubleshooting and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact CT Fault
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: CT tube warmup failure; CT exposure abort; Gantry rotation fault; CT image reconstruction failure; CT scan does not start; CT scan starts but aborts; Error occurs during SPECT/CT attenuation correction; Error occurs only on certain protocols; Error occurs after system startup or after extended use. Expected outcome: The complaint is separated into a tube, gantry, exposure, protocol, workstation, or reconstruction problem.

steps.title: Check for Immediate Safety Indicators
steps.instructions: Inspect the scanner area for unusual noise, burning smell, visible damage, fluid intrusion, smoke, overheating, or abnormal gantry behavior. Listen for scraping, grinding, repeated relay clicking, or abnormal fan noise. Expected outcome: No unsafe physical condition is present. If there is smoke, burning smell, abnormal heat, mechanical impact, or unsafe gantry motion, remove the system from service and escalate for service immediately.

steps.title: Verify Emergency Stop Status
steps.instructions: Check whether any emergency stop button is engaged in the scan room, control room, or system area. Reset only if the area is safe and site procedure allows reset. Expected outcome: The system is not being held in a stopped or inhibited state by an engaged emergency stop. If resetting the emergency stop clears the issue and the system passes required checks, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Confirm System Power and Startup State
steps.instructions: Verify that the gantry, acquisition workstation, reconstruction workstation, CT subsystem, and related cabinets are powered normally. Check for tripped breakers, inactive power indicators, UPS alarms, or shutdown messages. Expected outcome: The CT and workstation subsystems are fully powered and communicating. If power is unstable or a breaker trips again after reset, remove the system from service and escalate.

steps.title: Check Room Interlocks and Door Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the scan room door, interlocks, and radiation safety controls are in the proper state for CT exposure. Verify that the system is not displaying a door, interlock, or exposure inhibit message. Expected outcome: CT exposure is not being prevented by a safety interlock. If an interlock or door condition resolves the issue, repeat the required functional check and stop.

steps.title: Check Gantry and Table Area for Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the gantry opening, table travel path, covers, accessories, positioning aids, straps, and patient support items. Confirm nothing is contacting the gantry, table, detector area, or moving components. Expected outcome: Gantry rotation and table motion are not blocked by external obstruction. If removing an obstruction resolves the fault, perform a safe test movement or site-approved system check before returning to service.

steps.title: Confirm Table Position and Patient Load Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the table is properly positioned, not at a travel limit, and not overloaded. Confirm the table is not misaligned, pinned against an accessory, or stopped at an unexpected position. Expected outcome: CT scan start is not prevented by table position or travel limit. If repositioning the table resolves the fault, document the condition and confirm normal operation.

steps.title: Review the Error Message or Event Log
steps.instructions: Record the exact error code, message text, time of failure, scan type, protocol, and whether the fault occurred before, during, or after CT exposure. Do not clear logs before capturing useful service information. Expected outcome: Service has specific fault details instead of a vague “CT not working” complaint.

steps.title: Confirm Whether the Problem Is Protocol-Specific
steps.instructions: Ask whether the failure occurs on all CT scans or only on a specific SPECT/CT protocol. If safe and approved by site procedure, attempt a basic non-patient CT test or manufacturer-approved QA workflow. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a general CT subsystem fault or a protocol/reconstruction workflow issue. If only one protocol fails, involve imaging leadership, applications support, or Siemens service as appropriate before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Check CT Tube Warmup or Readiness Status
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the system requires CT tube warmup, calibration, or readiness preparation before scanning. If the system indicates tube warmup is required, have qualified staff complete the normal warmup workflow. Expected outcome: CT exposure is not being blocked because the tube is not ready. If warmup fails, aborts, or repeatedly prompts, remove the system from clinical use and escalate.

steps.title: Check Exposure Enable and Scan Start Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the technologist is following the normal scan start sequence and that the system is not waiting for operator acknowledgement. Verify that exposure indicators, warning lights, and control room prompts behave normally. Expected outcome: The exposure abort is not caused by incomplete operator sequence, door status, or exposure inhibit. If the exposure starts normally after correcting the sequence or room condition, document and stop.

steps.title: Assess Whether the Fault Is CT Hardware or Reconstruction
steps.instructions: Determine whether raw CT acquisition completes successfully. If CT exposure completes but images fail to reconstruct, focus on workstation, storage, software, reconstruction queue, and network/storage availability. If CT exposure aborts before or during scan, focus on tube, generator, gantry, interlock, or CT subsystem faults. Expected outcome: The failure is separated into acquisition failure versus post-acquisition reconstruction failure.

steps.title: Check Workstation and Reconstruction Queue Status
steps.instructions: Inspect the acquisition and reconstruction workstation screens for frozen applications, full queues, failed reconstruction jobs, disk space warnings, database errors, or communication errors. Do not delete studies or reconstruction data unless authorized by site policy. Expected outcome: Reconstruction is not blocked by a workstation, queue, or storage issue. If a simple application restart is part of approved site workflow and clears the issue, verify reconstruction completes before returning the system to use.

steps.title: Check Network and Storage Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm the system can access required local storage, reconstruction resources, and archive destinations. Determine whether PACS, RIS, DICOM, or network storage problems are also being reported. Expected outcome: Reconstruction failure is not being caused by unavailable storage or external network dependency. If the issue is network or archive-related, coordinate with IT/PACS support and document that CT acquisition hardware was not the primary finding.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is involved and site procedure allows it, restart the affected workstation or system component using the approved shutdown and startup process. Avoid repeated power cycling. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication faults clear without masking a recurring hardware problem. If the same CT tube, gantry, exposure, or reconstruction fault returns after restart, remove the system from service and escalate.

steps.title: Verify Operation Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: After any correction, confirm the system completes the appropriate CT readiness check, QA workflow, or test scan per site policy. Verify that reconstruction completes and no related fault returns. Expected outcome: The system is confirmed functional before clinical use resumes. If the system cannot complete a safe verification check, do not return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Siemens Symbia Intevo aborted during the CT portion of a SPECT/CT study and CT images did not reconstruct.
CCR.Cause: Verified no patient was on the table, checked emergency stop, room door/interlock status, table position, gantry area, and workstation queue; CT exposure fault returned during approved system check.
CCR.Resolution: Removed system from service, labeled Out of Service, recorded error message and time of failure, and escalated to Siemens service for CT subsystem evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, exact CT error code, message, time and date of failure
Accessories swapped or checked, including positioning aids, straps, table accessories, and patient support items
Power behavior, including power indicators, tripped breakers, UPS alarms, shutdown messages, or restart history
Environmental factors, including room door status, interlock status, scanner area obstruction, smoke, overheating, fluid intrusion, abnormal noise, vibration, or gantry behavior
Indicator lights, including exposure indicators, warning lights, control room prompts, emergency stop status, workstation warnings, storage warnings, and network or DICOM status
Final device status, including whether the system was returned to service, removed from service, labeled Out of Service, or escalated to Siemens service

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Intevo SPECT / CT System - Acquisition Workstation, DICOM, Archive, or RIS/PACS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting Symbia Intevo workstation, DICOM, archive, RIS/PACS, network, or study transfer failures using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Intevo is not actively scanning, reconstructing, or transferring a clinically urgent study. If a patient is on the table or the exam is in progress, do not interrupt acquisition or workstation processes unless directed by qualified imaging staff. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not interrupt imaging, delay care, or risk study data loss.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask Nuclear Medicine or imaging staff what failed. Identify whether the issue involves: Acquisition workstation not responding; Study will not send to PACS; DICOM send failure; Archive failure; RIS worklist not loading; Modality worklist mismatch; Study stuck in queue; Images missing from PACS; Reconstructed images not transferring; System cannot reach network destination; Error occurred after a reboot, network change, or scheduled downtime. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into workstation, local network, DICOM destination, RIS/worklist, PACS/archive, or workflow-related failure.

steps.title: Verify the Study Is Complete and Available Locally
steps.instructions: Have qualified imaging staff confirm the study exists on the acquisition workstation and can be opened locally. Confirm whether raw data, reconstructed images, CT images, fused SPECT/CT images, or secondary captures are affected. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a communication/export problem, not a failed acquisition or missing local study. If the study is not available locally, stop and escalate as an acquisition or workstation data issue.

steps.title: Check for Active Patient or Study Processing
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation is not still reconstructing, processing, or finalizing the study. Some transfers may not begin until the exam or reconstruction step is complete. Expected outcome: A delayed transfer is not mistaken for a failed transfer. If the transfer completes after processing finishes, document the delay and stop.

steps.title: Check Workstation Power and Basic Operation
steps.instructions: Confirm the acquisition workstation, monitors, keyboard, mouse, and any related computer peripherals are powered on and responsive. Look for frozen screens, login prompts, application errors, or abnormal system behavior. Expected outcome: The workstation is available for normal use before network or PACS troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check Physical Network Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ethernet connection at the workstation, wall jack, network switch connection point if accessible, and any nearby patch cable. Confirm the cable is fully seated and not damaged, stretched, crushed, or disconnected. Expected outcome: A loose or damaged network connection is ruled out. If reseating the cable restores communication, confirm successful DICOM or worklist function and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Network Link Indicators
steps.instructions: Check for link/activity lights at the workstation network port or connected network equipment if visible. A missing link light may indicate a disconnected cable, bad patch cable, disabled wall jack, or network port issue. Expected outcome: The device has a basic physical network link. If there is no link light after cable and jack checks, escalate to IT/network support and document the finding.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Network Cable if Accessible
steps.instructions: Replace the external Ethernet patch cable with a known-good cable if the workstation uses an accessible patch cable. Do not move internal scanner, gantry, table, or cabinet wiring. Expected outcome: A simple external cable failure is ruled out. If the known-good cable restores communication, document the cable replacement and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Other Networked Systems Are Working
steps.instructions: Ask imaging staff whether other modalities in the department can send to PACS or receive RIS worklists. If multiple devices are affected, the issue is likely network, RIS, PACS, archive, or enterprise-system related rather than isolated to the Symbia Intevo. Expected outcome: The failure is classified as device-specific or broader system-wide.

steps.title: Check Whether the Failure Is One Destination or All Destinations
steps.instructions: Determine whether the Symbia Intevo can send to any DICOM destination. Examples: PACS fails but local archive works; Archive fails but PACS works; RIS worklist fails but image send works; All network functions fail. Expected outcome: The affected communication path is narrowed. If only one destination fails, involve PACS/RIS/IT support for that destination while Clinical Engineering verifies the device-side basics.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and Queue Status
steps.instructions: Have imaging staff show the exact workstation message or DICOM queue status. Look for errors such as failed association, destination unreachable, connection timeout, rejected study, invalid accession, storage commitment issue, or queue retry failure. Expected outcome: The error supports either network failure, destination failure, configuration mismatch, or patient/study data mismatch.

steps.title: Check for Study Demographic or Accession Problems
steps.instructions: Verify with imaging staff that patient name, MRN, accession number, exam type, and study date match the expected order. RIS/PACS failures can occur when the study information does not match the order or destination rules. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by incorrect demographics or mismatched order data. If corrected demographics or order reconciliation allows transfer, confirm the study arrives in PACS and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Modality Worklist Function
steps.instructions: If RIS worklist is the complaint, confirm whether the workstation can query the worklist and whether the expected patient/order appears. Check whether the issue affects all patients or only one order. Expected outcome: A single missing order is separated from a full RIS/worklist communication failure. If only one order is missing, escalate to Radiology IT, scheduling, or RIS support rather than replacing device hardware.

steps.title: Check Date and Time Consistency
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation date and time appear reasonable and match hospital time closely. Significant time mismatch can affect authentication, study matching, logs, and communication workflows. Expected outcome: Communication failure is not being caused by an obvious time/date issue. If the time is incorrect, follow approved site procedure for correction and retest communication.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether anything changed recently, including: Network maintenance; PACS upgrade; RIS downtime; IP address change; Hostname change; Firewall change; Workstation software update; Scanner reboot; New DICOM destination; Department move or network jack change. Expected outcome: Recent environmental or configuration changes are identified before suspecting scanner hardware.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: If the workstation appears frozen or communication services appear stuck, coordinate with imaging staff before restarting any workstation application or system. Confirm no acquisition, reconstruction, transfer, or patient exam is active. Expected outcome: A safe restart clears a temporary workstation or application communication issue without risking data loss. If communication works after restart, verify successful send/worklist/archive operation and document the restart.

steps.title: Retest the Failed Workflow
steps.instructions: Retest the exact failed function with imaging staff. Examples: Query RIS worklist; Send a completed study to PACS; Send to archive; Retry failed DICOM queue item; Confirm the study appears at the destination. Expected outcome: The original complaint is either resolved or reproducible.

steps.title: Escalate Based on Failure Pattern
steps.instructions: If physical network, workstation responsiveness, study completion, demographics, and basic workflow checks are acceptable, escalate appropriately. Use this logic: No network link: escalate to IT/network support and bench/vendor support if workstation network hardware is suspected. All network functions fail: involve IT and Siemens service. Only PACS/archive fails: involve PACS administrator and Siemens service as needed. Only RIS/worklist fails: involve RIS/Radiology IT and Siemens service as needed. Workstation freezes, crashes, or cannot process studies: remove from clinical use and escalate for workstation service. DICOM configuration appears incorrect: escalate to Siemens service or approved imaging informatics support. Expected outcome: The issue is routed to the correct support path without unnecessary hardware replacement.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Siemens Symbia Intevo acquisition workstation would not send completed SPECT/CT studies to PACS.
CCR.Cause: Verified workstation was responsive and study was available locally, but DICOM send queue showed repeated connection failures to the PACS destination while other networked systems were also affected.
CCR.Resolution: Confirmed external network cabling was seated, documented PACS communication failure, removed device from routine clinical workflow pending resolution, and escalated to Radiology IT/PACS support and Siemens service.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient scan was active or completed
Exact error message or queue status
Whether raw, reconstructed, CT, fused, or secondary images were affected
Whether RIS worklist, PACS send, archive, or all communication failed
Workstation restart performed or not performed
Recent network, PACS, RIS, or software changes
Destination affected, such as PACS, archive, RIS, or worklist server
Alarm behavior, alerts, or workstation messages observed
Accessories or external cables swapped during troubleshooting
Power behavior of the workstation and related peripherals
Environmental factors such as department network outage, jack change, downtime, or PACS/RIS maintenance
Indicator lights observed at the network port or connected equipment
Final device status: returned to service, limited use, escalated, or Out of Service

title: Siemens Healthineers Dimension EXL 200 Clinical Chemistry Analyzer - Sample Probe Aspiration, Clot Detection, or Liquid Level Sensing Error
description: Troubleshooting sample aspiration, clot detection, liquid level sensing, short sample, probe blockage, or sample handling errors before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety and Result Integrity First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether any patient samples were affected by the aspiration, clot, or liquid level sensing error. Do not release questionable results until the affected specimen and analyzer status have been reviewed by laboratory staff. Expected outcome: Patient results are protected from possible sampling or volume-related error.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Error or Symptom
steps.instructions: Review the Dimension EXL 200 error message, event log, or operator report. Determine whether the issue involves: Sample probe aspiration failure; Clot detection alarm; Liquid level sensing error; Short sample or insufficient sample volume; Repeated errors on one tube; Repeated errors across multiple tubes; Probe crash, obstruction, or abnormal movement; Error occurring during calibration, QC, or patient testing. Expected outcome: The problem is separated into a sample-specific issue, probe issue, or broader analyzer problem.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Follows One Specimen
steps.instructions: Ask the lab whether the same sample failed repeatedly or if many samples are failing. Inspect the affected tube for low volume, foam, bubbles, fibrin, clots, gel interference, improper tube type, or poor sample separation. Expected outcome: If the problem follows one tube, the issue is likely specimen-related. Have the lab recollect, centrifuge, aliquot, or process the sample per lab policy.

steps.title: Verify Sample Tube Placement and Rack Loading
steps.instructions: Confirm the tube is fully seated in the rack and positioned correctly for the analyzer. Check that the rack is appropriate, not damaged, and loaded in the correct orientation. Expected outcome: The analyzer can reliably locate the sample tube and access the specimen.

steps.title: Check Sample Volume and Cup Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the sample volume is above the minimum required level for the ordered testing and container type. If sample cups or pediatric cups are used, verify they are seated correctly and are compatible with the analyzer setup. Expected outcome: The probe reaches liquid properly without triggering a false low-volume or liquid level error.

steps.title: Inspect for Foam, Bubbles, or Surface Film
steps.instructions: Ask the lab to visually inspect the sample surface. Foam, bubbles, or film can interfere with liquid level sensing and aspiration. Expected outcome: If foam or bubbles are present, the sample should be corrected or replaced according to lab procedure before retesting.

steps.title: Check for Clots, Fibrin, or Poor Separation
steps.instructions: Have lab staff inspect the specimen for clots, fibrin strands, gel disturbance, or inadequate centrifugation. Do not manually manipulate specimens unless permitted by laboratory policy. Expected outcome: A specimen integrity issue is identified or ruled out before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample Probe Externally
steps.instructions: With the analyzer in a safe stopped or maintenance state, visually inspect the sample probe area. Look for obvious bending, dried residue, salt buildup, misalignment, sample splash, or obstruction around the probe path. Expected outcome: The probe appears clean, straight, and unobstructed. If the probe is bent or damaged, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Probe Contact or Collision Evidence
steps.instructions: Ask whether there was a recent sample tube misload, rack jam, probe strike, or sudden increase in sampling errors. A probe that contacted a tube, cap, rack, or obstruction may no longer aspirate or sense liquid correctly. Expected outcome: Recent mechanical contact is identified as a possible cause and the unit is not returned to service without evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm Covers, Doors, and Interlocks Are Properly Closed
steps.instructions: Verify all relevant covers, sample area doors, and access panels are fully closed. Some analyzer functions may stop, inhibit, or alarm if access covers are not properly secured. Expected outcome: The analyzer is in a valid operating state before additional troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Reagent, Diluent, Wash, and Waste Status
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer status for low fluids, empty containers, full waste, or fluidics-related alarms that could affect sampling. Expected outcome: Supporting fluids and waste paths are available, and the sample error is not secondary to a broader fluidics problem.

steps.title: Review Recent Maintenance or Cleaning Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether probe cleaning, consumable replacement, daily maintenance, or service activity occurred shortly before the error began. Confirm no caps, tubing, containers, racks, or accessories were left out of position. Expected outcome: A recent maintenance-related setup issue is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Run the Appropriate Operator-Level Probe Cleaning or Prime Routine
steps.instructions: Coordinate with the lab to run approved maintenance routines for sample probe cleaning, wash, or fluidics priming if available to Clinical Engineering at the site. Avoid internal disassembly or service-level calibration unless authorized and trained. Expected outcome: Minor residue, air, or external fluid path interruption is cleared. If the error resolves, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Sample or Control Material
steps.instructions: Have the lab repeat testing using an acceptable known-good specimen, control, or sample container appropriate for their workflow. Expected outcome: If the known-good sample runs correctly, the original sample or container was likely the cause.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Intermittent or Repeatable
steps.instructions: If the error repeats, note whether it occurs with all samples, one rack position, one container type, one assay, or after a specific number of tests. Expected outcome: A repeatable pattern is captured for service escalation.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Testing if Sampling Reliability Is Uncertain
steps.instructions: If the analyzer continues to produce aspiration, clot detection, or liquid level sensing errors after external checks, remove it from clinical use for affected testing. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not used for patient testing until sample handling reliability is verified.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported repeated sample probe aspiration and liquid level sensing errors on the Siemens Dimension EXL 200 during patient sample processing.
CCR.Cause: External troubleshooting found low-volume and foamy specimens in the affected rack; no visible probe damage or fluid supply issue was observed.
CCR.Resolution: Affected specimens were removed for laboratory review, rack loading and sample volume were verified, probe area was visually inspected, and analyzer operation was confirmed with an acceptable known-good sample before return to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue affected one sample or multiple samples
Sample tube type and rack position
Foam, bubbles, clot, fibrin, or gel interference observed
Whether controls or known-good samples passed

title: Siemens Healthineers Dimension EXL 200 Clinical Chemistry Analyzer - Reagent Cartridge / Flex Reagent Recognition or Loading Issue
description: Troubleshooting Flex reagent cartridge loading, recognition, barcode, inventory, seating, or reagent availability issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety and Result Integrity First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Dimension EXL 200 is actively processing patient specimens. If the reagent issue affects active testing, coordinate with the laboratory supervisor before interrupting the analyzer, cancelling tests, or removing reagents. Expected outcome: Patient testing is protected, and affected assays are not reported until reagent status is verified.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reagent Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what occurred during loading or testing. Determine whether the issue involves: Flex reagent cartridge not recognized; Reagent cartridge will not load; Barcode or cartridge ID not reading; Reagent inventory not updating; Reagent loaded but assay remains unavailable; Reagent expired, empty, or flagged unusable; Error occurs with one cartridge or multiple cartridges; Reagent compartment, loader, or carousel-related fault. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into a cartridge-specific problem, loading path problem, or analyzer recognition problem.

steps.title: Verify the Reagent Cartridge Is Correct for the Assay
steps.instructions: Confirm the Flex reagent cartridge matches the assay being requested and is approved for use on the Dimension EXL 200. Check that the cartridge is not expired, previously exhausted, visibly damaged, leaking, or contaminated. Expected outcome: The analyzer is being loaded with a valid reagent cartridge. If the wrong, expired, empty, or damaged cartridge is found, replace it with the correct cartridge and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Cartridge Externally
steps.instructions: Remove the cartridge only if safe and allowed by lab procedure. Check for cracked plastic, damaged label, missing barcode, debris, residue, dried reagent, or signs that the cartridge was not stored properly. Expected outcome: No obvious cartridge condition issue prevents recognition or proper seating. If damage or contamination is found, have the lab use a new cartridge and stop.

steps.title: Check Barcode or Label Readability
steps.instructions: Inspect the cartridge barcode or identification label. Make sure the label is flat, clean, complete, and not scratched, wet, wrinkled, or blocked by tape or residue. Expected outcome: The reagent identification can be read reliably by the analyzer. If the label is unreadable, replace the cartridge according to lab policy and stop.

steps.title: Reseat the Flex Reagent Cartridge
steps.instructions: Have the operator reload the cartridge using normal workflow. Confirm it is placed in the correct orientation and fully seated in the assigned reagent position. Expected outcome: The analyzer accepts the cartridge, updates inventory, and makes the assay available. If reseating resolves the issue, document the cartridge was not fully seated and stop.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Cartridge if Available
steps.instructions: If the issue follows one cartridge, have the lab try another valid cartridge of the same assay or a known-good cartridge if available. Expected outcome: The problem either follows the original cartridge or remains with the analyzer position/loading system. If only one cartridge fails, treat it as a cartridge or barcode issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Whether Multiple Reagents Are Affected
steps.instructions: Review whether the analyzer fails to recognize one cartridge, one reagent position, one assay, or all reagent cartridges. Expected outcome: The fault pattern is clear. A single cartridge points toward cartridge condition, barcode, expiration, or seating. Multiple cartridges may indicate a reader, loader, carousel, reagent compartment, software, or inventory communication issue.

steps.title: Check Reagent Compartment Access and Door Status
steps.instructions: Confirm reagent compartment doors, covers, and access points are fully closed after loading. Look for door open messages, latch issues, obstruction, or anything preventing the analyzer from completing reagent loading. Expected outcome: The analyzer recognizes that reagent loading is complete and safe to proceed.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions in the Loading Area
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the external loading path and accessible reagent area without internal disassembly. Look for misloaded cartridges, loose caps, packaging pieces, broken cartridge tabs, spilled reagent, or debris. Expected outcome: Nothing blocks cartridge seating, movement, or recognition. If debris or an incorrectly loaded cartridge is found and can be safely removed externally, correct it and retest.

steps.title: Review Analyzer Messages and Event Logs
