steps.instructions: Check the displayed error message, reagent status screen, inventory screen, and recent event history. Note whether the analyzer reports barcode read failure, reagent not present, invalid cartridge, position error, loader error, or reagent unavailable. Expected outcome: The error message supports the observed failure and guides whether the issue is cartridge-related or analyzer-related.

steps.title: Confirm System Date, Time, and Reagent Status
steps.instructions: Verify the analyzer date and time appear correct. Confirm the reagent is not being rejected due to expiration, lot status, onboard stability, calibration requirement, or QC lockout. Expected outcome: The cartridge is not being misidentified as invalid because of system status or assay availability rules.

steps.title: Check Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there was a recent reagent lot change, software restart, maintenance activity, power event, cartridge spill, or repeated loading error. Expected outcome: The timeline helps identify whether the issue began after a known change.

steps.title: Restart Only if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and laboratory leadership approves, perform a normal software-controlled restart or power cycle according to site procedure. Do not power-cycle during active specimen processing unless directed by lab policy or Siemens support. Expected outcome: Temporary software or inventory communication errors clear, and reagent recognition returns.

steps.title: Retest Reagent Recognition
steps.instructions: Reload the valid cartridge and confirm that the analyzer recognizes it, updates inventory, and allows the associated assay to proceed once calibration and QC requirements are met. Expected outcome: The reagent cartridge is recognized and available for testing. If the reagent loads successfully and all required checks pass, return the analyzer to service and document the result.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Dimension EXL 200 would not recognize a Flex reagent cartridge after loading.
CCR.Cause: Cartridge barcode and expiration were checked; issue persisted with multiple valid cartridges, indicating a likely analyzer-side reagent recognition or loading fault.
CCR.Resolution: Reagent cartridges were reseated, loading area was inspected, analyzer messages were reviewed, and the unit was removed from service for Siemens evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Flex reagent cartridge lot number and expiration date
Whether one cartridge or multiple cartridges failed
Whether the cartridge barcode was readable
Whether the cartridge was reseated or replaced
Any visible spill, debris, or cartridge damage

title: Siemens Healthineers Dimension EXL 200 Clinical Chemistry Analyzer - Cuvette, Reaction Vessel, or Incubation System Fault
description: Troubleshooting cuvette, reaction vessel, incubation, temperature, loading, and related analyzer handling faults using external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety and Result Integrity First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Dimension EXL 200 is actively processing patient specimens. If testing is delayed, results are questionable, or a run has stopped, notify laboratory staff so affected samples can be held, repeated, or redirected according to lab policy. Expected outcome: Patient results are not released from an analyzer with unresolved reaction vessel or incubation concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Fault or Symptom
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer message, operator report, or event log. Identify whether the issue involves: Cuvette supply or loading error; Reaction vessel handling fault; Incubation temperature alarm; Reaction area not reaching temperature; Cuvette jam or misfeed; Unexpected assay recovery or reaction timing issue; Repeated fault on startup or during a specific assay; Error after reagent, cuvette, or maintenance activity. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into a supply, transport, temperature, or assay-processing problem.

steps.title: Stop and Secure the Analyzer Before Handling Supplies
steps.instructions: Do not reach into the cuvette or reaction vessel area while the analyzer is moving or actively processing. Follow normal operator-safe pause or stop procedures before inspecting external-access areas. Expected outcome: The analyzer is safe to inspect without creating a motion, pinch, or contamination hazard.

steps.title: Check Cuvette or Reaction Vessel Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm that the correct cuvettes or reaction vessels are loaded and that the supply is not empty, overfilled, stuck, or loaded incorrectly. Remove obvious misloaded or damaged consumables only if accessible without disassembly. Expected outcome: The analyzer has a proper supply of correctly loaded reaction vessels. If the fault clears after correcting the supply, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Cuvette Jam or Misfeed
steps.instructions: Check the accessible cuvette path, loading area, and waste path for a visible jam, tipped cuvette, cracked vessel, or obstruction. Do not force mechanisms, remove covers beyond normal access, or bypass interlocks. Expected outcome: Any simple visible obstruction is cleared safely, or no external obstruction is found. If a jam is cleared and the analyzer returns to normal operation after lab verification, stop.

steps.title: Check Waste and Disposal Path
steps.instructions: Confirm that cuvette waste, reaction vessel waste, or related waste containers are not full, blocked, improperly seated, or preventing used vessels from exiting the analyzer. A full or mispositioned waste container can create downstream handling faults. Expected outcome: Used reaction vessels have a clear disposal path.

steps.title: Check Fluids Related to Reaction Processing
steps.instructions: Verify that required wash, water, and waste containers or drains are properly connected and not empty, full, kinked, or leaking. Reaction vessel or cuvette faults may appear when wash or waste handling cannot complete normally. Expected outcome: Required fluid and waste paths are available for normal reaction processing.

steps.title: Confirm Covers, Doors, and Interlocks Are Properly Closed
steps.instructions: Check that analyzer covers, supply doors, and operator-access panels are fully closed and seated. A door or interlock condition may stop motion or prevent reaction vessel handling. Expected outcome: The analyzer recognizes the system as closed and ready.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Temperature Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm that the analyzer is not exposed to direct airflow, blocked ventilation, excessive room temperature, or unusual heat around the instrument. Incubation faults may be affected by poor ventilation or environmental instability. Expected outcome: The analyzer environment supports stable incubation temperature.

steps.title: Allow Normal Warm-Up if the Analyzer Was Recently Powered On
steps.instructions: If the analyzer was recently restarted or powered down, allow appropriate warm-up time before judging incubation performance. Do not rush patient testing until the system indicates it is ready. Expected outcome: Incubation-related status stabilizes after normal warm-up.

steps.title: Review Recent Operator Maintenance or Supply Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the fault started after: Cuvette or reaction vessel refill; Waste container change; Cleaning or maintenance; Reagent replacement; Power interruption; Analyzer restart; Nearby service activity. Expected outcome: The timing of the fault points toward a recent handling, supply, or setup change.

steps.title: Restart Only if Safe and Approved by Lab Workflow
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and laboratory staff approve, perform a normal software-controlled restart or reset according to site procedure. Do not power-cycle during active testing unless the system is stopped and the lab agrees. Expected outcome: A temporary handling or sensor state clears, or the fault returns and requires further evaluation.

steps.title: Run Operator-Level Checks or System Readiness Tests
steps.instructions: Have lab staff perform any approved startup, readiness, or maintenance checks available to operators for cuvette handling, reaction processing, or incubation readiness. Clinical Engineering should observe the error behavior and document when it occurs. Expected outcome: The fault is either cleared by approved readiness checks or confirmed as repeatable.

steps.title: Verify with Laboratory Staff Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: If the issue appears resolved, confirm that the analyzer passes required readiness checks, QC, or lab-defined acceptance steps before patient testing resumes. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not returned to clinical use until the lab confirms acceptable performance.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported a Dimension EXL 200 cuvette/reaction vessel fault during sample processing, preventing affected chemistry testing from continuing.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the cuvette waste container was full and not allowing used reaction vessels to clear properly.
CCR.Resolution: Emptied and reseated the cuvette waste container, verified covers were closed, had lab staff run analyzer readiness checks, and returned unit to service after acceptable QC.

helpfulDetails: Exact cuvette, reaction vessel, or incubation error message
Whether fault occurred at startup, during testing, or after maintenance
QC status before return to service

title: Siemens Healthineers Dimension EXL 200 Clinical Chemistry Analyzer - Wash, Waste, Drain, or Fluidics Pressure Error
description: Troubleshooting wash, waste, drain, liquid handling, and fluidics pressure errors using external checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety and Result Integrity First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Dimension EXL 200 is actively processing patient samples. If the analyzer has stopped, produced fluidics errors, or may have affected sample processing, notify the laboratory supervisor before clearing errors or restarting operation. Expected outcome: Patient specimens and results are protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Error or Symptom
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer message, event log, or operator report. Determine whether the issue involves: Wash error; Waste full or waste not draining; Drain error; Fluidics pressure error; Probe wash failure; Cuvette wash issue; Reagent or sample fluid handling issue; Leak, overflow, or liquid buildup; Repeated error at the same step in processing. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to wash, waste, drain, or pressure-related fluidics before parts are suspected.

steps.title: Check for Visible Leaks, Overflow, or Liquid Buildup
steps.instructions: Inspect around the analyzer, waste area, wash area, sample/reagent area, and accessible tubing paths without opening internal covers beyond normal operator access. Look for liquid under the analyzer, wet tubing, overflowing waste, loose caps, or fluid pooling. Expected outcome: Any obvious leak or overflow is identified before the unit is restarted. If liquid is present near electrical areas, stop troubleshooting, remove the analyzer from service, and escalate.

steps.title: Verify Waste Container Status
steps.instructions: Check that the liquid waste container is not full and is properly seated. Confirm the cap, tubing, and waste line are connected correctly and not kinked, pinched, or pulled loose. Expected outcome: Waste can flow freely from the analyzer to the waste container or drain connection. If the waste container is full, empty or replace it per lab procedure, reset the condition, and recheck operation.

steps.title: Check the Drain Path
steps.instructions: If the analyzer drains to facility plumbing, verify the drain line is not kinked, blocked, elevated incorrectly, or disconnected. Confirm the drain receptacle is not backed up or overflowing. Expected outcome: Drain flow is unobstructed and not causing back pressure.

steps.title: Inspect External Fluid Containers and Connections
steps.instructions: Check any accessible wash solution, reagent water, system fluid, or cleaning solution containers used by the Dimension EXL 200. Confirm containers are filled, properly positioned, and connected with correct caps and tubing. Expected outcome: Required fluids are available and tubing is properly connected.

steps.title: Check for Kinked, Pinched, or Dislodged Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external tubing between containers, waste, drain, and analyzer connection points. Look for tubing caught behind the analyzer, sharply bent, compressed, or routed incorrectly after recent cleaning or movement. Expected outcome: Tubing is routed smoothly with no obvious restriction.

steps.title: Verify Recent Maintenance or Consumable Changes
steps.instructions: Ask lab staff whether any containers, waste lines, wash fluids, tubing connections, or maintenance items were changed before the error began. A fluidics issue immediately after maintenance often points to an open cap, loose tube, empty container, or improperly seated waste connection. Expected outcome: Recent changes are reviewed before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Check for Clogs at Accessible Waste or Wash Areas
steps.instructions: Inspect operator-accessible wash areas for dried residue, buildup, debris, or crystals. Do not force tools into probes, lines, pumps, or valves. Expected outcome: Obvious buildup is identified and cleaned only within normal lab maintenance procedures.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Level and Properly Positioned
steps.instructions: Verify the analyzer has not been moved, shifted, or pushed against tubing or wall connections. Confirm there is enough clearance for drain and waste lines behind or beside the unit. Expected outcome: Analyzer placement is not restricting fluid or waste flow.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If no leak, overflow, or electrical hazard is present, have the operator follow normal shutdown and restart procedures. Do not repeatedly clear fluidics errors without correcting the cause. Expected outcome: A normal restart may clear a temporary software or sensor state, but repeated alarms require escalation.

steps.title: Run an Appropriate Operator-Level Prime, Wash, or Maintenance Routine
steps.instructions: Have qualified lab staff perform the approved operator-level wash, prime, or fluidics maintenance routine for the affected area. Clinical Engineering should observe for abnormal sounds, slow drainage, leaks, repeated pressure alarms, or fluid not moving as expected. Expected outcome: Fluid movement returns to normal, or the fault repeats and confirms a persistent fluidics problem.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Is Isolated or System-Wide
steps.instructions: Determine whether the error occurs with one assay, one fluid path, one wash cycle, or across multiple operations. A single assay or maintenance step may suggest a localized reagent or wash issue. Multiple fluidics failures suggest a broader pressure, pump, drain, or sensor issue. Expected outcome: The fault pattern is documented before repair escalation.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Verification
steps.instructions: If the issue is corrected by emptying waste, correcting tubing, replacing an empty fluid container, or clearing an external drain restriction, confirm the analyzer completes the related wash or fluidics routine successfully. Expected outcome: The Dimension EXL 200 operates without repeat fluidics errors and the lab confirms acceptable analyzer status. If resolved, document the work order and stop.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported repeated fluidics pressure errors on the Siemens Dimension EXL 200 during wash operation.
CCR.Cause: Waste container connection was loose and the external waste line was partially kinked behind the analyzer.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the waste connection, corrected tubing route, verified waste flow, and had lab complete an operator-level wash routine with no repeat error.

helpfulDetails: Exact error code or message displayed
Waste line or drain line condition
Any visible leak or liquid buildup
Whether issue affected one assay or all testing
Abnormal pump sounds or repeated priming attempts
Whether lab verified QC or analyzer readiness before use

title: Siemens Healthineers Dimension EXL 200 Clinical Chemistry Analyzer - Calibration, QC, Assay Recovery, or LIS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting calibration, QC, assay recovery, and LIS communication failures using safe external and workflow checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Dimension EXL 200 is actively processing patient specimens. If calibration, QC, assay recovery, or LIS communication is failing, affected assays should not be used for patient result reporting until acceptable QC and communication status are confirmed. Expected outcome: Patient results are protected from being released from an analyzer or interface that is not functioning correctly.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the laboratory staff what failed and when it started. Determine whether the issue involves: Calibration failure; QC failure; Poor assay recovery; One specific assay failing; Multiple assays failing; Results not transmitting to LIS; Orders not downloading from LIS; Results stuck, rejected, or delayed; Recent reagent, calibrator, QC lot, software, middleware, or network change. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into analytical failure, reagent/QC workflow issue, or communication issue.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Status and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review the Dimension EXL 200 operator screen, status indicators, and any active alarms or messages. Look for messages related to reagent status, sample handling, calibration, QC, system readiness, host communication, or pending results. Expected outcome: The analyzer provides a specific fault area instead of troubleshooting the entire system broadly.

steps.title: Verify the Analyzer Is Ready and Not in a Hold, Error, or Offline State
steps.instructions: Confirm that the analyzer is online, initialized, and available for testing. If the system is paused, stopped, in standby, or showing a condition that prevents processing, follow approved operator recovery steps before repeating calibration, QC, or communication testing. Expected outcome: Testing or result transmission is not blocked by a basic analyzer state issue.

steps.title: Check Reagent Status for Affected Assays
steps.instructions: Verify that the required reagent cartridges or onboard reagents are present, recognized, properly seated, and not expired. Confirm sufficient reagent volume and that the reagent lot matches the expected calibration and QC setup. Expected outcome: Calibration or QC is not failing because of missing, expired, depleted, or mismatched reagent.

steps.title: Check Calibrator and QC Material
steps.instructions: Confirm that the correct calibrator or QC material was used for the affected assay. Check expiration date, lot number, storage condition, reconstitution requirements, mixing, and whether the material was allowed to reach proper handling condition per lab procedure. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by incorrect or compromised calibration/QC material.

steps.title: Confirm Lot and Assay Setup
steps.instructions: Review whether there was a recent reagent lot change, calibrator lot change, QC lot change, assay parameter update, or laboratory middleware change. A mismatch between reagent, calibrator, QC, and assay setup can cause repeated calibration or QC failures even when the analyzer hardware is functioning normally. Expected outcome: Lot-related or setup-related causes are identified before hardware failure is suspected.

steps.title: Repeat Calibration or QC Only When the External Cause Has Been Addressed
steps.instructions: If an obvious issue was found, such as expired QC, wrong lot selection, empty reagent, or improperly prepared material, correct that issue first. Then have the lab repeat calibration or QC according to site procedure. Expected outcome: Calibration or QC passes after correcting the external or workflow-related cause. If resolved, document the work order and stop.

steps.title: Check for Sample or Assay Recovery Pattern
steps.instructions: If the complaint is poor assay recovery, determine whether it affects: One patient sample; One assay; One reagent lot; One QC level; Multiple assays; All chemistry testing. Expected outcome: A single-sample or single-assay issue is separated from a broader analyzer performance issue.

steps.title: Inspect External Fluid, Waste, and Consumable Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that required fluids, wash solutions, cuvettes, sample cups, and waste containers or drains are available and not creating system interruptions. Look for visible leaks, kinked external lines, full waste containers, empty fluid containers, unusual odors, or fluid around the analyzer. Expected outcome: Analytical performance is not being affected by a basic fluidics, consumable, or waste condition.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Power Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that the analyzer is connected to the correct power source and that no recent power event occurred. Confirm that the analyzer area has not experienced excessive heat, vibration, water exposure, construction dust, or relocation. Expected outcome: External site conditions are ruled out as contributors to unstable calibration, QC drift, or communication interruption.

steps.title: For LIS Communication Failure, Confirm the Scope
steps.instructions: Ask the lab whether the issue affects only the Dimension EXL 200 or multiple analyzers. If multiple analyzers are affected, involve LIS, middleware, or IT support early because the issue may be outside the analyzer. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as analyzer-specific or part of a broader network/LIS event.

steps.title: Check Network Cable and Physical Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ethernet cable at the analyzer and wall/network port. Confirm the cable is firmly seated and not damaged. If permitted by site policy, try a known-good network cable and known-good network jack. Expected outcome: LIS communication is not failing due to a loose cable, damaged cable, or inactive wall port.

steps.title: Confirm Host or LIS Connection Status
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer’s host communication status, if available, and check whether the analyzer is online to the host. Confirm whether orders are downloading, results are transmitting, or only one direction of communication is failing. Expected outcome: The communication failure is narrowed to order download, result upload, bidirectional interface, middleware, or LIS-side issue.

steps.title: Check for Pending, Held, or Rejected Results
steps.instructions: Review whether results are stuck in a queue, held for review, rejected, or waiting for manual release. Not every LIS complaint is a network failure. Some are caused by result review rules, patient/order mismatch, sample ID mismatch, or middleware rejection. Expected outcome: Workflow and order-matching problems are ruled out before calling the analyzer interface failed.

steps.title: Coordinate With LIS or IT for Network Verification
steps.instructions: If the analyzer appears functional but communication still fails, contact LIS/IT to verify network port status, IP address, interface engine status, middleware status, and recent changes. Clinical Engineering should confirm the device-side physical connection and analyzer status, while LIS/IT confirms host-side communication. Expected outcome: Device-side and network-side responsibilities are separated clearly.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: If the analyzer or workstation appears locked, unresponsive, or communication has stopped, coordinate with the lab before restarting any analyzer, workstation, or interface component. Do not interrupt active testing unless the lab approves and patient testing impact has been addressed. Expected outcome: Recovery actions do not compromise active specimens or patient result integrity.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Verification
steps.instructions: After correcting the issue, have the lab verify successful operation using the appropriate site-approved method. This may include acceptable calibration, passing QC, successful assay recovery review, successful order download, or successful result transmission to LIS. Expected outcome: The analyzer is returned to service only after the reported failure is verified as resolved.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Dimension EXL 200 failed QC on multiple chemistry assays and patient results were not transmitting consistently to LIS.
CCR.Cause: Found affected reagent and QC lots were valid, analyzer was ready, network cable was secure, and LIS confirmed intermittent host interface communication errors.
CCR.Resolution: Verified analyzer-side connections and status, coordinated LIS interface review, confirmed successful QC and result transmission after host communication was restored, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact calibration, QC, assay recovery, or LIS complaint
Whether one assay or multiple assays were involved
Recent reagent, QC, calibrator, or software changes
Network cable and wall jack checked
Any unusual sounds, leaks, heat, odor, or visible damage

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Evo Nuclear Medicine System - Detector Head Motion, Positioning, or Proximity Sensor Fault
description: Troubleshooting detector head motion, positioning, collision avoidance, proximity sensor, or gantry movement faults before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is not in contact with the detector heads, table, gantry, or any moving part of the system. If the issue occurs during a patient setup or acquisition, stop motion and have clinical staff safely remove or reposition the patient according to site policy. Expected outcome: No patient is exposed to unintended detector, table, or gantry movement.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Motion or Positioning Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what motion failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Detector head will not move; Detector head stops during positioning; Detector head position does not match the selected protocol; Proximity or collision warning appears; Gantry rotation is inhibited; Detector orbit fails during acquisition; Table and detector alignment issue; Intermittent fault during setup only. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to detector motion, gantry rotation, table positioning, or proximity/collision sensing.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the detector head travel area, table area, gantry clearance, patient accessories, straps, cables, positioning aids, and nearby equipment. Remove anything that could block detector movement or trigger collision protection. Expected outcome: Motion path is clear and no external object is causing the fault. If this resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Verify the Patient Table Position and Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is properly positioned, locked, and not extended or angled in a way that conflicts with detector movement. Check that headrests, arm supports, straps, cushions, or patient positioning devices are not protruding into the detector path. Expected outcome: The table and accessories do not interfere with detector head travel.

steps.title: Check Detector Head Clearance and Starting Position
steps.instructions: Confirm both detector heads are in a safe starting position and are not already close to the table, patient area, or gantry structure. Use operator controls only as intended and avoid forcing motion. Expected outcome: Detector heads have adequate clearance for the selected motion.

steps.title: Review the System Message or Error Code
steps.instructions: Read the message on the acquisition console or system control interface. Note whether the fault references: Detector head motion; Gantry rotation; Collision detection; Proximity sensor; Limit switch; Table interlock; Positioning timeout; Motion controller or drive fault. Expected outcome: The displayed fault gives direction before parts or internal failures are assumed.

steps.title: Confirm Emergency Stop and Safety Interlocks Are Released
steps.instructions: Check that all emergency stop buttons are released and that any required covers, doors, or interlocked access points are properly closed. Reset only according to normal operator procedure. Expected outcome: Motion is not being inhibited by an active emergency stop or external safety interlock.

steps.title: Check Hand Controller, Gantry Controls, and Operator Input
steps.instructions: Verify whether the issue occurs from one control point or all control points. If motion fails only from one pendant, hand control, or control panel, inspect that control for stuck buttons, damage, loose cable connection, or liquid intrusion. Expected outcome: A local control issue is separated from a system-wide motion fault.

steps.title: Attempt a Normal Repositioning Sequence When Safe
steps.instructions: With no patient present and the area clear, have qualified staff attempt a normal detector or gantry repositioning sequence using standard controls. Do not repeatedly command motion if the system stops, alarms, or behaves unpredictably. Expected outcome: Motion either completes normally or the fault repeats in a controlled, patient-free condition.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Follows a Specific Motion
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue occurs during: One detector head movement; Both detector heads; Gantry rotation; Table movement; Detector approach toward the patient area; Only one study protocol; All positioning attempts. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps identify whether the issue is localized or system-wide.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes or Service Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent room changes, table service, detector service, software updates, power events, cleaning, collision events, or accessory changes. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified as possible contributors to motion or proximity sensor behavior.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Restart Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the system is idle, no patient is present, and site procedure allows it, perform a normal shutdown and restart of the acquisition system and gantry control system. Do not use power cycling to repeatedly clear an active mechanical or safety fault. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software-related motion lockout clears. If the fault returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: Verify Room Power and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the system has stable power and that no room equipment, carts, cables, or floor obstructions are affecting gantry or table movement. Look for unusual heat, odor, sounds, vibration, or visible damage near the gantry or detector assembly. Expected outcome: External power, environment, and room setup are not contributing to the fault.

steps.title: Do Not Bypass Proximity or Collision Protection
steps.instructions: Do not tape, defeat, disable, or bypass proximity sensors, collision sensors, limit switches, or safety interlocks. These protections are part of patient and staff safety. Expected outcome: Safety systems remain intact while troubleshooting is performed.

steps.title: Determine Whether the System Can Be Safely Returned to Use
steps.instructions: If the detector head, gantry, and table move normally with no repeated alarms, no abnormal sounds, and no safety warnings, follow site policy for functional verification before clinical use. If any motion fault repeats, motion is inconsistent, or a proximity warning appears without clear cause, remove the system from service. Expected outcome: The system is either verified safe for use or escalated appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Symbia Evo detector head stopped during patient positioning and displayed a proximity/collision warning.
CCR.Cause: Verified no patient was present, checked detector travel path, table position, accessories, emergency stops, and operator controls; fault repeated with clear motion path.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Symbia Evo from service, labeled Out of Service, documented repeated detector motion/proximity fault, and escalated for vendor evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was present when the issue occurred
Exact error message or fault code
Whether gantry rotation, table motion, or detector motion failed
Whether the fault occurred during setup, acquisition, or QC
Accessories or positioning aids in use
Whether issue occurred from one control station or all controls
Abnormal sounds, vibration, heat, or smell

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Evo Nuclear Medicine System - Collimator Recognition, Seating, or Exchange Issue
description: Troubleshooting collimator recognition, seating, lock, exchange, or detector setup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Evo is not actively being used for a patient study. If the issue occurs during patient setup, stop the exam workflow and move the patient away from the detector, table, gantry, and collimator area before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No patient is positioned near moving or improperly seated collimator components.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Collimator Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what happened during setup or exchange. Determine whether the issue involves: Collimator not recognized; Wrong collimator displayed; Collimator appears seated but system reports an error; Collimator exchange will not complete; Collimator lock or latch concern; Detector head reports a collimator mismatch; Error occurs with one specific collimator or multiple collimators; Issue occurs on one detector head or both. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to recognition, seating, locking, workflow, or a specific collimator.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Collimator Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm the collimator installed matches the intended protocol and detector configuration. Check that the collimator type selected or expected by the acquisition protocol matches the physical collimator installed. Expected outcome: The system and physical setup agree on the required collimator type. If the wrong collimator was installed or selected, correct the setup, repeat recognition, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Inspect the Collimator Seating Externally
steps.instructions: Visually confirm the collimator is fully seated and evenly positioned on the detector head. Look for obvious gaps, uneven alignment, foreign material, obstruction, or anything preventing full seating. Do not place hands near pinch points or attempt to force the collimator into position. Expected outcome: The collimator appears mechanically seated, aligned, and unobstructed.

steps.title: Check Collimator Lock or Latch Status
steps.instructions: Verify the collimator locking mechanism appears fully engaged according to normal user workflow. If the system reports the collimator is unlocked or not seated, have trained Nuclear Medicine staff repeat the normal seating or exchange process. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the collimator as seated and locked after a normal, safe exchange attempt. If recognition returns after reseating, document the issue and stop.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the collimator exterior, mounting surfaces, visible identification area, and detector-side seating area for damage. Look for bent edges, chipped surfaces, loose hardware, damaged labels, cracked covers, or signs the collimator was dropped. Expected outcome: No visible physical damage is found that would interfere with seating or recognition. If damage is found, remove the affected collimator from use and escalate for evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Does Not Follow One Collimator
steps.instructions: If safe and allowed by site policy, compare with another approved collimator of the correct type. Determine whether the fault follows the same collimator or remains with the detector head or exchange position. Expected outcome: The issue is isolated to either a specific collimator or the system recognition/seating path. If only one collimator fails, remove that collimator from service and label it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Does Not Follow One Detector Head
steps.instructions: If the system has multiple detector heads, determine whether the recognition fault occurs on one head or both. A single-head failure may suggest a detector-side recognition, seating, sensor, or lock detection issue. Expected outcome: The affected detector head or recognition location is identified.

steps.title: Check for Workflow or Software State Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not mid-protocol, mid-exchange, or in a partially completed acquisition setup. Return to a safe home/setup state if permitted by normal operator workflow, then attempt recognition again. Expected outcome: The system is in the correct state to recognize the installed collimator. If the issue clears after returning to the correct setup state, document the workflow condition and stop.

steps.title: Review System Messages and Event Details
steps.instructions: Record the exact error message, detector head involved, collimator type, protocol selected, and when the message appears. Note whether the error appears during startup, exchange, protocol selection, detector positioning, or acquisition setup. Expected outcome: Clear fault details are available for repair escalation if needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is present and the department agrees, perform a controlled system restart using approved site workflow. Do not power cycle during an active acquisition or while the system is in an unsafe mechanical state. Expected outcome: Temporary software recognition issues are cleared without creating a mechanical or patient safety hazard. If the collimator is recognized after restart, verify operation with the department and document the action taken.

steps.title: Do Not Force Movement or Exchange
steps.instructions: If the collimator will not seat, release, lock, or be recognized after normal external checks, stop troubleshooting. Forcing a collimator or repeating failed exchange attempts can create equipment damage and staff safety risk. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting stops before unsafe mechanical intervention occurs.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported the Symbia Evo would not recognize the installed collimator during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Verified the correct protocol was selected, reseated the collimator, and found the recognition fault followed one specific collimator.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the affected collimator from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the error behavior, and escalated for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue affected one collimator or multiple collimators
Whether issue affected one detector head or both
Whether the issue occurred after recent exchange, cleaning, or service

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Evo Nuclear Medicine System - Daily Uniformity, Flood, or Center-of-Rotation QC Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed daily uniformity, flood, detector QC, or center-of-rotation checks before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Evo is not being used for an active patient study. If daily QC, flood uniformity, or center-of-rotation testing has failed, do not release the system for patient imaging until QC status is resolved according to site policy. Expected outcome: No patient study is performed on a nuclear medicine system with questionable detector or rotational accuracy.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact QC Failure
steps.instructions: Review the operator report, QC screen, or failed test result. Identify whether the failure involves: Daily uniformity QC; Intrinsic or extrinsic flood failure; Center-of-rotation failure; Detector mismatch; Unexpected count rate; Energy peaking or photopeak issue; Gantry rotation issue during QC; Processing or workstation error during QC review. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to image quality, detector response, rotational alignment, acquisition setup, or processing.

steps.title: Confirm the System Was Not Used Clinically After the Failure
steps.instructions: Ask Nuclear Medicine staff whether any patient studies were performed after the failed QC. If studies were performed, notify the department lead or physicist according to site policy. Expected outcome: Any possible clinical impact is identified and escalated appropriately.

steps.title: Check the Room and System Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is powered on normally, fully initialized, and not displaying gantry, detector, table, or acquisition workstation errors. Look for warning indicators, unusual noises, abnormal detector motion, or startup faults. Expected outcome: The system is in a stable operating state before QC is repeated.

steps.title: Verify the QC Procedure Was Set Up Correctly
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist to confirm the correct daily QC protocol was selected. Check that the expected detector, collimator condition, acquisition type, matrix, energy window, and isotope selection were used. Expected outcome: The failed QC was not caused by an incorrect protocol or acquisition setup.

steps.title: Confirm Source Selection and Activity
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct flood source or point source was used for the QC test. Confirm the source activity is appropriate, not expired, and within the range expected by Nuclear Medicine or Physics procedure. Expected outcome: The detector is being tested with the correct source strength and isotope.

steps.title: Check Source Positioning
steps.instructions: For flood uniformity testing, confirm the flood source was positioned flat, centered, and at the correct distance from the detector. For center-of-rotation testing, confirm the point source was correctly positioned according to the site’s QC procedure. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by source misalignment or incorrect geometry.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Between Source and Detector
steps.instructions: Inspect the area between the source and detector for attenuation or shielding issues. Remove unnecessary objects, lead markers, loose shielding, patient positioning aids, or other materials that could interfere with the flood image. Expected outcome: The detector has a clear, unobstructed view of the source.

steps.title: Confirm Collimator Status
steps.instructions: Verify the correct collimator is installed for the intended QC procedure, if extrinsic testing is being performed. Confirm the collimator is fully seated, recognized correctly by the system, and not physically damaged. Expected outcome: QC is not failing because of the wrong, loose, or incorrectly recognized collimator.

steps.title: Review the Flood Image Appearance
steps.instructions: If available, review the failed flood image with Nuclear Medicine or Physics. Look for obvious nonuniformity patterns such as: Edge defects; Cold spots; Hot spots; Streaks; Detector-specific artifacts; Collimator-related patterns; Source positioning artifacts. Expected outcome: The image pattern helps separate a setup problem from a detector or collimator issue.

steps.title: Check Whether One Detector or Multiple Detectors Are Affected
steps.instructions: Determine whether the failure occurs on one detector head or both. If only one detector fails, compare the failed image and count statistics to the passing detector. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one detector path, shared setup, source condition, or system-wide acquisition problem.

steps.title: Check Count Rate and Acquisition Time
steps.instructions: Confirm the expected count rate was reached and the acquisition was not stopped early. Low activity, incorrect source distance, wrong isotope selection, or early termination can cause failed or unreliable QC results. Expected outcome: The QC acquisition collected enough valid counts for proper evaluation.

steps.title: Check Energy Peaking or Photopeak Status
steps.instructions: Review whether the detector was correctly peaked for the isotope used. If the photopeak is incorrect or unstable, coordinate with Nuclear Medicine or Physics before repeating QC. Expected outcome: Uniformity failure is not being caused by incorrect energy window or peaking conditions.

steps.title: Repeat QC Only After Correctable Setup Issues Are Addressed
steps.instructions: If an obvious setup issue is found, correct it and have qualified staff repeat the appropriate QC test. Examples include repositioning the source, selecting the correct protocol, removing an obstruction, or correcting collimator setup. If the repeat QC passes and the site’s release criteria are met, document the correction and return the system to service. Expected outcome: The issue is resolved by correcting an external or procedural cause.

steps.title: Check Center-of-Rotation Setup
steps.instructions: If center-of-rotation QC failed, confirm the correct source type, source position, detector configuration, gantry motion path, and acquisition protocol were used. Ensure nothing interfered with detector movement or gantry rotation during the test. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by incorrect point source placement, setup geometry, or motion obstruction.

steps.title: Check for Gantry or Detector Motion Faults
steps.instructions: Review whether the system displayed any motion, positioning, or limit errors during center-of-rotation acquisition. If motion was abnormal, do not continue repeated QC attempts without further evaluation. Expected outcome: A mechanical or positioning problem is identified before additional testing is attempted.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes such as: Collimator exchange; Detector service; Software restart or update; Power interruption; Room temperature change; Source replacement; New QC phantom or flood source; Recent failed or repeated calibration. Expected outcome: Recent changes help identify a likely cause or escalation path.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the issue appears related to workstation communication, acquisition software, or a frozen QC process, perform a controlled restart according to site procedure. Do not restart during active acquisition, motion, or patient use. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues are cleared before QC is repeated.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Failed QC Attempts
steps.instructions: If QC fails again after external setup, source, collimator, and acquisition checks are completed, stop troubleshooting. Repeated failures may indicate detector, gantry, calibration, electronics, or processing issues requiring service evaluation. Expected outcome: The device is not repeatedly tested without a safe path to resolution.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported that the Siemens Symbia Evo failed daily flood uniformity QC and could not be released for patient imaging.
CCR.Cause: Checked source setup, collimator seating, acquisition protocol, count rate, and detector status; repeat QC continued to fail on one detector.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Symbia Evo from service, labeled the system Out of Service, notified Nuclear Medicine leadership, and escalated for service and physics review.

helpfulDetails: Whether patients were scanned after failure

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Evo Nuclear Medicine System - Gantry Rotation, Table Movement, Or Acquisition Positioning Fault
description: Troubleshooting gantry rotation, patient table movement, detector positioning, or acquisition setup faults before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is on the table or positioned near the gantry. If the fault occurs during patient setup or acquisition, stop movement immediately and have clinical staff safely remove or reposition the patient according to site policy. Expected outcome: The patient is protected from unintended table, gantry, or detector movement.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Motion or Positioning Fault
steps.instructions: Ask Nuclear Medicine staff what movement failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Gantry rotation not starting; Gantry rotation stopping mid-motion; Patient table not moving; Table movement stopping unexpectedly; Detector head positioning issue; Acquisition will not start because position is not accepted; Collision, limit, or proximity warning; Intermittent positioning fault during one protocol. Expected outcome: The fault is separated into gantry, table, detector, positioning, or acquisition setup.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstructions Around the System
steps.instructions: Inspect the gantry, table path, detector travel area, floor area, and patient positioning accessories. Look for stools, step platforms, cables, linens, immobilizers, phantoms, or other items blocking motion. Expected outcome: No external object is preventing safe gantry, detector, or table movement. If an obstruction is found and removal restores normal motion, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Patient Table Is Clear and Properly Loaded
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not overloaded, unevenly loaded, or restricted by patient accessories. Check that pads, straps, arm boards, and positioning aids are not caught between moving sections. Expected outcome: The table can move freely without mechanical restriction.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Motion Enable Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that no emergency stop button is engaged. Confirm that any required motion enable controls, hand controls, foot controls, or operator controls are available and functioning normally. Expected outcome: The system is not being held in a stopped or inhibited state by an external safety control.

steps.title: Review the System Message or Error Prompt
steps.instructions: Check the operator console, acquisition workstation, or gantry display for the exact fault message. Note whether the system reports a limit, collision, table position, detector position, gantry position, interlock, or communication issue. Expected outcome: The displayed fault helps identify whether the issue is safety-related, position-related, or control-related.

steps.title: Check Table Docking, Alignment, and Travel Limits
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is properly aligned with the gantry and has not reached a mechanical or software travel limit. If the table is near an end stop or unusual position, have trained staff attempt normal return-to-home or repositioning through approved operator controls. Expected outcome: The table position is valid and not preventing acquisition setup.

steps.title: Check Gantry and Detector Position Before Acquisition
steps.instructions: Verify that the gantry and detector heads are in a safe, expected starting position for the selected study. Confirm that the selected protocol matches the required patient position, table orientation, and detector configuration. Expected outcome: The system accepts the setup and allows acquisition positioning.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Not Protocol or Setup Related
steps.instructions: Ask whether the fault occurs with one study type, one patient orientation, one acquisition protocol, or all movements. If only one protocol fails, compare setup requirements with a known-good protocol or have Nuclear Medicine verify the acquisition setup. Expected outcome: Incorrect protocol setup is ruled out before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Check Hand Control, Operator Panel, and Console Inputs
steps.instructions: Confirm whether movement fails from one control location or all control locations. If motion works from the console but not from a hand control, the issue may be isolated to the accessory or control path. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to system motion, user control input, or accessory control.

steps.title: Inspect Visible Cables and Connectors Externally
steps.instructions: Check visible table, gantry, detector, and control cables for loose connections, damage, strain, or signs of being pulled. Do not open covers or disturb internal cabling unless approved by service procedure and site policy. Expected outcome: No obvious external cable issue is causing intermittent motion or communication faults.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is on the system and the department agrees, restart the acquisition workstation and system using the approved site process. After restart, verify whether the gantry, table, and positioning functions return to normal standby status. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication lockup is cleared.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check
steps.instructions: With no patient on the table, have qualified staff test basic table movement, gantry positioning, and acquisition setup movement within normal safe limits. Observe for repeated stops, abnormal noises, jerky movement, position mismatch, or recurring error messages. Expected outcome: Motion is smooth, controlled, and repeatable without alarms.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Motion Attempts if Faults Recur
steps.instructions: If the gantry, table, or detector repeatedly stops, alarms, moves unpredictably, or reports position mismatch, stop troubleshooting. Continued attempts may create risk to patients, staff, or equipment. Expected outcome: The device is not forced through a possible motion control or safety fault.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine staff reported that the Symbia Evo gantry stopped during acquisition positioning and would not complete setup for the study.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no obstruction, emergency stop, table accessory interference, or incorrect protocol setup; the gantry positioning fault repeated during unloaded functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Symbia Evo from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the recurring positioning fault, and escalated for service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the fault occurred
Exact error message or system prompt
Gantry position when the fault occurred
Whether obstruction or accessory interference was found
Whether the issue affects one protocol or all protocols
Whether motion works from one control but not another
Any unusual noise, vibration, odor, heat, or jerky movement

title: Siemens Healthineers Symbia Evo Nuclear Medicine System - Acquisition Workstation, DICOM, Archive, or RIS/PACS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting Symbia Evo workstation, DICOM send, archive, RIS/PACS, worklist, or study transfer failures before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Symbia Evo is not actively acquiring a patient study before restarting workstations, disconnecting cables, or changing network connections. If a study is in progress, coordinate with Nuclear Medicine staff before taking any action that could interrupt acquisition, reconstruction, or image transfer. Expected outcome: Patient imaging and study data are protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: DICOM send failure; Study not reaching PACS; Archive failure; RIS worklist not loading; Patient demographics not importing; Images stuck in queue; Study transfer is slow; Acquisition workstation cannot reach the network; One destination failing or all destinations failing. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into workstation, network, DICOM destination, RIS, PACS, or archive workflow.

steps.title: Check Whether Patient Images Are Safely Stored Locally
steps.instructions: Confirm with the operator that the study is still available on the acquisition workstation or local system storage. Do not delete, reprocess, or repeat studies until image status is verified. Expected outcome: Existing patient data is protected before communication troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Workstation Errors
steps.instructions: Review the acquisition workstation screen for application errors, frozen software, warning messages, failed transfers, or pending jobs. If the workstation application is responsive, note the exact error message before clearing or restarting anything. Expected outcome: The failure message is captured for documentation and possible escalation.

steps.title: Verify Network Cable and Physical Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the Ethernet cable is securely connected at the acquisition workstation, network wall jack, switch port if visible, and any approved network interface point. Look for damaged cables, loose connectors, or missing link lights. Expected outcome: A loose or disconnected network path is corrected. If communication restores, confirm study transfer and stop.

steps.title: Check Workstation Network Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation shows an active network connection. Look for disconnected adapter status, disabled network connection, invalid IP address, or no network access. Expected outcome: The workstation has a valid network connection before DICOM or RIS/PACS troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Other Networked Devices Are Affected
steps.instructions: Ask Nuclear Medicine or imaging staff whether other systems can send to PACS, receive worklist, or access the archive. If multiple imaging systems are affected, involve IT, PACS, or network support before assuming a Symbia Evo fault. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either device-specific or part of a wider network/PACS outage.

steps.title: Check DICOM Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Verify the operator is sending to the correct destination, such as PACS, archive, processing workstation, or nuclear medicine review station. Confirm that the failed study was not accidentally queued to the wrong destination. Expected outcome: Incorrect destination selection is ruled out or corrected.

steps.title: Review the Transfer Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether studies are pending, failed, retrying, or blocked behind another failed transfer. If the system allows safe resend or retry from the queue, retry only after confirming patient data is intact and the destination is correct. Expected outcome: A stuck or failed transfer is identified and retried appropriately. If transfer succeeds, document and stop.

steps.title: Confirm RIS Worklist Behavior
steps.instructions: If the issue involves worklist, check whether the workstation can query RIS and whether scheduled patients appear correctly. Confirm the date, modality, station name, location, or query filters are not limiting the displayed worklist. Expected outcome: Worklist failure is separated from incorrect search filters or scheduling workflow.

steps.title: Verify Date and Time
steps.instructions: Check that the acquisition workstation date and time are correct. Incorrect time settings can interfere with worklist queries, study matching, archive behavior, and troubleshooting logs. Expected outcome: Time-related communication or matching problems are ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to: Network drops; IP address assignments; PACS destination; RIS interface; Firewall rules; Workstation replacement; Software updates; Department routing rules; DICOM AE title, port, or host configuration. Expected outcome: The issue is linked to a recent system, network, or workflow change if applicable.

steps.title: Test Basic Network Reachability if Available
steps.instructions: If permitted by site policy and system access, use approved workstation tools to confirm whether the system can reach the DICOM destination, gateway, or server. Do not make configuration changes without confirming the correct values with IT or PACS support. Expected outcome: Network reachability is confirmed or the issue is escalated to IT/PACS with clear findings.

steps.title: Check Archive or PACS System Status
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the archive or PACS destination is online, accepting studies, and not in maintenance or downtime. A receiving system problem can look like a nuclear medicine system failure. Expected outcome: The receiving endpoint is verified before blaming the acquisition workstation.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the acquisition workstation application is frozen or communication services appear unresponsive, coordinate with Nuclear Medicine staff before restarting the application or workstation. Make sure no active acquisition, reconstruction, or transfer would be interrupted. Expected outcome: A safe restart clears temporary software or communication lockups. If communication restores, confirm successful transfer and stop.

steps.title: Verify Successful Study Transfer
steps.instructions: After any corrective action, have staff confirm the study appears at the intended destination with correct patient demographics, image series, and accession information. Expected outcome: The issue is resolved only after the receiving system confirms the study is present and usable.

CCR.Complaint: Nuclear Medicine reported that Symbia Evo studies were not transferring from the acquisition workstation to PACS after completion.
CCR.Cause: Found studies held in the transfer queue after confirming the workstation network cable and DICOM destination were correct; PACS connectivity was restored after safe workstation application restart.
CCR.Resolution: Restarted the acquisition workstation application with Nuclear Medicine approval, resent the queued study, confirmed image arrival in PACS, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected DICOM send, RIS worklist, archive, or PACS transfer
Exact error message or failed job status
Whether the study remained stored locally
Whether one study or all studies were affected
Whether one destination or all destinations failed
Network cable and link light status
Whether the study arrived successfully in PACS

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Juniper Ultrasound System - Probe Not Recognized or Probe Connection Fault
description: Troubleshooting probe recognition, probe port, connector, seating, and transducer communication faults before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is not being used on an active patient before troubleshooting the probe connection. If the issue occurs during an exam, have clinical staff stop the study, move the patient to another ultrasound system if needed, and preserve any completed images according to department workflow. Expected outcome: The patient is not delayed or scanned with an unreliable probe connection.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Probe Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what happened and when the issue appeared. Determine whether the problem involves: Probe not detected; Probe detected intermittently; Wrong probe type displayed; Image unavailable after probe selection; Probe disconnects when cable is moved; One probe failing on one port; Multiple probes failing on the same port; All probes failing on the system. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a probe, port, connector, cable, or system-level recognition problem.

steps.title: Check for Active Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review the on-screen message, probe selection screen, and system status indicators. Note whether the system reports a probe connection fault, unsupported probe, unavailable probe, or no transducer connected. Expected outcome: The error message helps separate a physical connection issue from a compatibility or software recognition issue.

steps.title: Verify the Probe Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions: Power the system to a safe idle state and confirm the probe connector is fully inserted and locked into the probe port. Do not force the connector. The probe should seat smoothly and lock securely. Expected outcome: A loose or partially seated probe is corrected. If the probe is recognized after reseating, verify imaging function and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Connector Externally
steps.instructions: Remove the probe and inspect the connector pins, locking mechanism, shell, and strain relief. Look for bent pins, missing pins, debris, fluid residue, cracked housing, damaged latch components, or signs of impact. Expected outcome: Visible connector damage is identified before suspecting an internal system fault.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Cable and Transducer Housing
steps.instructions: Check the full probe cable length for cuts, crushed sections, sharp bends, exposed shielding, swelling, or strain relief separation. Inspect the transducer head for cracks, fluid intrusion, delamination, or evidence of being dropped. Expected outcome: A physically damaged probe is removed from service and not reused on patients.

steps.title: Check the Probe Port for Debris or Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the ACUSON Juniper probe port externally for debris, bent contacts, damaged latch points, liquid residue, or foreign material. Do not insert tools into the connector or attempt deep cleaning beyond approved external cleaning practices. Expected outcome: Port damage or contamination is identified. If the port appears damaged, remove the system from service and escalate.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Probe on the Same Port
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good, compatible Siemens probe to the same probe port. Expected outcome: If the known-good probe works, the original probe is likely defective or incompatible. If the known-good probe also fails, the port or system may be the cause.

steps.title: Try the Suspect Probe on Another Available Port
steps.instructions: If the system has another compatible probe port available, connect the suspect probe to that port. Expected outcome: If the probe works on another port, the original port may be faulty. If the probe fails on multiple ports, the probe or probe connector is suspect.

steps.title: Confirm Probe Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify that the probe model is approved for use with the ACUSON Juniper and the installed software configuration. A probe may physically connect but still fail recognition if it is unsupported, not enabled, damaged, or not compatible with the system software. Expected outcome: Compatibility is confirmed before hardware failure is assumed.

steps.title: Restart the Ultrasound System
steps.instructions: If no patient is active and the probe and port appear externally normal, perform a controlled system restart. After restart, reconnect the probe and check whether it appears in the probe selection menu. Expected outcome: A temporary software or initialization issue clears. If the probe is recognized and imaging is stable, document the restart and functional check.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is Intermittent
steps.instructions: With the probe connected and selected, gently position the cable in a neutral, stress-free position. Do not aggressively flex or twist the cable. Observe whether recognition drops out with normal handling. Expected outcome: Intermittent connection behavior is identified safely. A probe that disconnects during normal handling should be removed from service.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Imaging Verification
steps.instructions: If the probe is recognized, confirm that the probe produces an image using an approved phantom or non-patient test method per site practice. Check for image dropout, missing elements, excessive noise, or abnormal artifacts. Expected outcome: Probe recognition and basic imaging function are both verified before returning the system to clinical use.

steps.title: Review Recent Events
steps.instructions: Ask whether the probe was recently dropped, cleaned, soaked incorrectly, repaired, moved between rooms, or connected after a software update. Expected outcome: A recent handling, cleaning, compatibility, or configuration change may explain the recognition fault.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Using an Unreliable Probe
steps.instructions: If the probe is intermittently recognized, physically damaged, or produces unstable images, remove the probe from service even if it occasionally works. Expected outcome: Clinical staff do not rely on a probe that may disconnect or fail during an exam.

CCR.Complaint: Ultrasound staff reported that the ACUSON Juniper would not recognize the selected probe during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the probe was not detected on multiple ports, while a known-good compatible probe was recognized normally.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the suspect probe from service, labeled it Out of Service, verified the ACUSON Juniper recognized a known-good probe, and sent the failed probe for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue followed the probe or stayed with the port
Whether a known-good probe was tested
Whether the suspect probe was tested on another port
Any visible fluid, debris, bent pins, or physical damage

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Juniper Ultrasound System - Image Artifact, Dropout, Noise, or Poor Image Quality
description: Troubleshooting ultrasound image artifacts, dropout, noise, weak image quality, probe issues, and external setup problems before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is not being used for an active patient exam before troubleshooting.

If the image quality issue occurs during an exam, have clinical staff move the patient to another available ultrasound system if imaging is clinically urgent.

Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed or continued on a system producing questionable images.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Image Quality Complaint
steps.instructions:
Ask the sonographer what they are seeing and when it occurs.

Identify whether the issue involves:

Image dropout
Vertical or horizontal lines
Speckle, snow, or electronic noise
Weak penetration
Poor resolution
Shadowing not explained by anatomy
Color Doppler noise
Intermittent image loss
Artifact with one probe only
Artifact with all probes

Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to a probe, application, system setting, or possible internal imaging fault.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Follows One Transducer
steps.instructions:
Ask staff which probe was being used when the artifact appeared.

Test the same preset with another compatible transducer if available.

If the image quality improves with a different probe, remove the suspect probe from service.

Expected outcome: A single-probe issue is separated from a system-wide imaging issue.

If this resolves the issue, document the failed transducer and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Transducer Externally
steps.instructions:
Inspect the probe face, lens, strain relief, cable, and connector.

Look for cracks, swelling, cuts, exposed shielding, fluid intrusion, bent connector pins, or cable kinks.

Do not flex a damaged probe cable repeatedly while connected to the system.

Expected outcome: Physical probe damage or contamination is identified before blaming the ultrasound platform.

If damage is found, remove the probe from service and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Probe Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions:
Power down or follow site-approved probe connection procedure if the probe must be reseated.

Disconnect and reconnect the transducer, making sure the connector is clean, dry, aligned, and fully locked.

Expected outcome: Poor contact at the probe connector is ruled out.

If reseating restores normal image quality, return the unit to service after confirming stable imaging.

steps.title: Check for Gel, Fluid, or Debris at the Connector
steps.instructions:
Inspect the probe connector and system port area for gel, dust, liquid, or debris.

Do not use the system if fluid intrusion is suspected.

Expected outcome: Connector contamination is ruled out as a cause of dropout, noise, or intermittent imaging.

steps.title: Test Multiple Imaging Modes
steps.instructions:
Check B-mode first, then color Doppler, spectral Doppler, and any affected advanced imaging mode if applicable.

Determine whether the issue appears in all modes or only in one mode.

Expected outcome: The issue is separated into a general imaging problem or a mode-specific problem.

steps.title: Confirm Preset, Depth, Gain, TGC, Focus, and Frequency Settings
steps.instructions:
Have the operator verify that the correct exam type and transducer preset are selected.

Check depth, overall gain, TGC sliders, dynamic range, focus position, harmonics, and frequency selection.

Expected outcome: Poor image quality caused by inappropriate imaging settings is corrected.

If normal image quality returns after settings are corrected, stop.

steps.title: Compare Against a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions:
Use a known-good probe, known-good preset, and standard test target or phantom if available.

Compare the image against expected performance for that probe type.

Expected outcome: The image issue is confirmed as either reproducible or operator/setup related.

steps.title: Check for Electrical Noise Sources
steps.instructions:
Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is plugged into an approved hospital-grade outlet.

Look for nearby equipment that may introduce interference, such as electrosurgical units, beds, chargers, power strips, pumps, warming devices, or non-medical electronics.

Move unnecessary equipment away or test from another approved outlet if safe and permitted.

Expected outcome: External electrical interference is ruled out.

steps.title: Check the Display and Video Output
steps.instructions:
Determine whether the artifact is present on the live ultrasound image, saved images, external monitor, or only the system display.

If the saved image is clean but the screen appears distorted, suspect a display or video path issue rather than an imaging acquisition issue.

Expected outcome: Display-related distortion is separated from true imaging artifact.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions:
If no patient is connected and the exam workflow allows, perform a normal shutdown and restart.

Allow the system to fully boot before retesting the affected probe and preset.

Expected outcome: Temporary software or imaging-processing errors are cleared.

If image quality returns and remains stable, document the restart and final test result.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions:
Ask whether the issue started after:

Probe drop or cleaning event
Software update
System relocation
New room or outlet use
New transducer connection
Network or peripheral change
Recent service activity

Expected outcome: The timeline helps identify whether the problem is environmental, accessory-related, or service-related.

steps.title: Check for Overheating, Smell, or Abnormal Sounds
steps.instructions:
Inspect the system externally for blocked vents, unusual fan noise, excessive heat, burning smell, or liquid exposure.

Do not continue troubleshooting if overheating, odor, or liquid intrusion is present.

Expected outcome: Safety-related signs of equipment failure are identified early.

steps.title: Confirm Final Image Quality With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions:
After corrections, ask the sonographer to verify image quality using normal clinical workflow or a phantom if available.

Expected outcome: The system is not returned to service based only on a basic power-on check.

CCR.Complaint: Sonographer reported intermittent image dropout and noise while using the ACUSON Juniper with the abdominal transducer.
CCR.Cause: External troubleshooting found the artifact followed one transducer, and the probe cable showed damage near the strain relief.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the damaged transducer from service, labeled it for repair evaluation, verified normal image quality with a known-good probe, and returned the ultrasound system to service.

helpfulDetails: Probe model and serial number involved
Whether the issue followed one transducer or all transducers
Whether saved images also showed the artifact
Unusual heat, smell, or fan noise

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Juniper Ultrasound System - System Freeze, Slow Boot, Or Application Crash
description: Troubleshooting Juniper freezing, slow startup, software crashes, boot delays, or intermittent application lockups before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is not actively being used on a patient. If the system freezes during an exam, have clinical staff stop scanning, protect the patient, and move to another ultrasound system if imaging is still needed. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed or continued on an unstable imaging system.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Symptom
steps.instructions: Ask the sonographer or clinical user what happened and when. Determine whether the issue involves: Complete system freeze; Touchscreen or control panel not responding; Application crash; Slow boot; Delayed shutdown; Image acquisition freezing; Exam screen locking up; Network send or storage delay; Intermittent issue after long use. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into startup, application, user interface, imaging, storage, or network-related behavior.

steps.title: Check for an Active Patient Exam or Unsaved Study
steps.instructions: Before powering off or restarting, confirm whether an exam is open and whether images or measurements may be unsaved. If possible, allow clinical staff to save or document the exam status before rebooting. Expected outcome: Patient study data is protected before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart if Safe
steps.instructions: If the system is frozen and no patient is connected, attempt a normal shutdown from the user interface. If the system does not respond, follow site-approved hard shutdown procedures only after confirming no active patient use or pending critical data. Expected outcome: The system restarts cleanly and returns to normal operation. If this resolves the issue, document the reboot and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Verify AC Power and Power Cord Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the AC power cord is fully seated at the wall, power brick if applicable, and system connection.
