Confirm the outlet is hospital-grade, powered, and not controlled by a switched wall outlet. Expected outcome: The system receives stable AC power. If power was loose or unstable and correcting it resolves the freeze or boot issue, stop.

steps.title: Check Battery or Internal Power Status if Applicable
steps.instructions: If the system was running on battery or had recently been unplugged, check battery charge status and whether the issue occurred during low battery operation. Low or unstable power can cause slow booting, unexpected shutdown behavior, or system instability. Expected outcome: The issue is not being caused by low battery or unstable power transfer.

steps.title: Remove Nonessential External Accessories
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential USB drives, external keyboards, printers, barcode scanners, footswitches, and other accessories. Restart the system with only required probes and power connected. Expected outcome: A faulty or incompatible external accessory is ruled out. If the system boots normally after removing an accessory, test accessories one at a time to identify the cause.

steps.title: Check Probe Connection and Probe Behavior
steps.instructions: Inspect the connected transducer for damaged cable jacket, bent connector pins, fluid contamination, or loose seating. If the crash occurs when a specific probe is selected, disconnect that probe and test with a known-good compatible probe. Expected outcome: The issue is not being triggered by a damaged or problematic transducer.

steps.title: Check the Control Panel and Touchscreen Response
steps.instructions: Confirm whether only the imaging application is frozen or whether the entire user interface is unresponsive. Check touchscreen, trackball, keyboard, rotary controls, and soft keys. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to a software lockup, input device issue, or full system hang.

steps.title: Check System Storage and Exam Queue Status
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the system has a large number of stored studies, failed transfers, or pending exams. Review available storage status if accessible from the system menus. Full storage, large unsent queues, or repeated failed transfers may slow booting, shutdown, or application performance. Expected outcome: Storage or workflow backlog is identified before assuming a computer hardware failure.

steps.title: Check Network Connection
steps.instructions: If the freeze or delay occurs during study send, worklist query, PACS transfer, or shutdown, disconnect the network cable or disable wireless if permitted by site policy and test local system operation. Confirm with IT or PACS support whether the network, DICOM destination, or worklist server is available. Expected outcome: The issue is separated between local system performance and network-related delay.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and Event Details
steps.instructions: Record any displayed error code, application message, warning, or abnormal boot screen. Note whether the system restarts by itself, shows a Windows or application-level error, or remains stuck at a Siemens startup screen. Expected outcome: Specific failure information is captured for service evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether anything changed before the problem started, including: Software update; Network change; DICOM destination change; New probe; New USB device; Power outage; System relocation; Cleaning event; Recent service visit. Expected outcome: A recent external or configuration change is identified as a possible cause.

steps.title: Allow the System to Boot Fully Before Judging Performance
steps.instructions: If the complaint is slow boot, time the startup from power-on to usable imaging screen. Compare the behavior to staff reports and any known normal boot behavior for that location. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as an abnormal boot delay rather than normal startup time.

steps.title: Check Ventilation and Overheating Signs
steps.instructions: Inspect the air vents, fan area, rear panel, and cart area for blocked airflow, dust buildup, unusual heat, or fan noise. Do not open the system or perform internal cleaning beyond approved external cleaning. Expected outcome: External airflow restriction or overheating concern is identified.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check After Reboot
steps.instructions: Once the system boots, confirm basic operation: System reaches imaging screen; Touchscreen and controls respond; Probe is recognized; Image displays normally; Exam can be started; Images can be stored; DICOM send or worklist functions operate if applicable. Expected outcome: The system is either returned to service after passing functional checks or held for further evaluation.

steps.title: Monitor for Recurrence
steps.instructions: If the system appears normal after restart, ask staff to report whether the issue returns with the same probe, same exam type, same network workflow, or after a certain amount of use. Expected outcome: Intermittent faults are tracked with enough detail to support repair escalation if needed.

CCR.Complaint: Sonographer reported the ACUSON Juniper froze during exam setup and was slow to reboot after restart.
CCR.Cause: Verified no active patient was connected, checked AC power, removed USB accessory, inspected probe connection, and found the system continued to intermittently freeze after startup.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the ultrasound system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented boot behavior and error details, and escalated for bench evaluation or vendor service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the system was in active patient use
Exact freeze, crash, or boot symptom
Whether issue follows a specific probe
Fan noise, heat, or blocked vents
Whether the issue recurred after restart

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Juniper Ultrasound System - Trackball, keyboard, touchscreen, or control panel not responding
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive ultrasound controls, touchscreen, keyboard, trackball, or control panel input issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is not actively being used for patient scanning. If controls become unresponsive during an exam, have clinical staff stop the exam, safely remove the transducer from the patient, and move the patient to another available ultrasound system if imaging is still needed. Expected outcome: No patient care depends on a system with unreliable operator controls.

steps.title: Confirm Which Input Is Not Responding
steps.instructions: Ask the sonographer exactly what is not working. Determine whether the issue involves: Trackball not moving the cursor; Keyboard keys not responding; Touchscreen not responding; Touchscreen responding inaccurately; Control panel buttons not working; Knobs, sliders, or soft keys not responding; Entire control panel frozen; System image still live but controls locked; Complete system freeze. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one input device, one section of the control panel, or the whole system.

steps.title: Check Whether the System Is Frozen or Only the Controls Are Affected
steps.instructions: Look at the display and determine whether the ultrasound image, clock, system messages, or on-screen indicators are still updating. If the screen is frozen and no controls respond, the issue may be a system software or processing lockup rather than a single control failure. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is separated between a full system freeze and a localized input problem.

steps.title: Remove the System From Clinical Use if Controls Are Unreliable
steps.instructions: If the trackball, keyboard, touchscreen, or control panel does not respond consistently, do not allow the system to be used for patient exams. Ultrasound operation requires reliable depth, gain, freeze, measurement, annotation, patient entry, and image storage controls. Expected outcome: The system is not used if operator control is unsafe or unreliable.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the trackball, keyboard, touchscreen, knobs, and control panel surface. Look for gel, cleaning residue, fluid spills, paper labels, probe covers, debris, stuck keys, or anything physically blocking normal movement. Carefully clean external surfaces according to site-approved cleaning procedures. Expected outcome: External contamination or obstruction is removed if present.

steps.title: Check the Trackball Movement
steps.instructions: Rotate the trackball in all directions and observe whether the cursor or selection point moves normally. Check whether the trackball feels sticky, loose, jammed, or physically damaged. If cleaning around the trackball restores movement, verify normal operation and stop. Expected outcome: The trackball either responds normally or remains suspect after external checks.

steps.title: Check Keyboard and Control Panel Buttons
steps.instructions: Press several known commonly used keys or buttons such as Freeze, Gain, Depth, Measure, Patient, Store, or Review, depending on what staff reported. Do not perform a patient exam during this test. Expected outcome: You determine whether one button, one area, or the entire control panel is affected.

steps.title: Check Touchscreen Response
steps.instructions: Touch several areas of the touchscreen and compare the response to the operator’s input. Determine whether the touchscreen is completely unresponsive, delayed, inaccurate, or only failing in one area. Inspect for screen protector damage, moisture, gel, or pressure from an object contacting the screen edge. Expected outcome: Touchscreen failure is confirmed as either environmental, alignment-related, or likely hardware/software related.

steps.title: Check for Stuck Keys or Continuous Input
steps.instructions: Look for a key, knob, encoder, or touchscreen area that appears stuck or continuously activating. A stuck input can prevent other controls from responding correctly. Expected outcome: Any obvious stuck control is identified before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Check External USB Accessories
steps.instructions: If a USB keyboard, barcode scanner, wireless adapter, or external storage device is connected, remove nonessential accessories and retest the control panel. A faulty USB accessory can sometimes cause input delays, system lockups, or unexpected behavior. Expected outcome: The system input issue either clears after removing accessories or remains unchanged.

steps.title: Check Power and Docking Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is on stable AC power and that the power cord is fully seated. If the system is operating on battery, connect to AC power and allow the system to stabilize. Expected outcome: Low power, unstable AC, or battery-related behavior is ruled out.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart
steps.instructions: If the system is not actively scanning a patient and no data is at risk, perform a normal shutdown and restart using the approved user interface if possible. If the interface is completely frozen, follow site policy for safely powering down the system. Expected outcome: Temporary software lockup clears after restart, or the issue returns and requires further evaluation.

steps.title: Retest the Reported Control Function After Restart
steps.instructions: After the system boots fully, retest the exact control that was reported. Confirm trackball movement, keyboard input, touchscreen response, and control panel button response. If all controls function normally and the issue does not return, document the restart and return the unit to service only if it passes functional checks. Expected outcome: The system is either restored or confirmed to have a repeatable fault.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or System Logs Available to Clinical Engineering
steps.instructions: Review any visible system messages, startup warnings, touchscreen prompts, control panel errors, or input-related alerts. Record the exact wording or code if available. Expected outcome: Any useful fault information is captured before escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Without scanning a patient, verify that the system can: Navigate menus; Adjust gain and depth; Freeze and unfreeze imaging; Enter or edit patient information; Use measurement controls; Store or review an image if appropriate; Respond to touchscreen and physical controls consistently. Expected outcome: The system either passes a practical operator-control check or is removed from service.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Reboots as a Fix
steps.instructions: If the issue returns after restart, affects multiple controls, or occurs intermittently, do not rely on repeated power cycles. Intermittent control failure can affect patient workflow and may lead to incomplete exams, incorrect measurements, or unsaved images. Expected outcome: A recurring control issue is treated as a service problem, not a temporary inconvenience.

CCR.Complaint: Sonographer reported that the ACUSON Juniper trackball and touchscreen were intermittently not responding during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no spill, obstruction, or connected USB accessory issue; fault returned after restart during functional control testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ultrasound system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented failed trackball and touchscreen response, and sent unit for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient exam was in progress
Whether issue was intermittent or constant
Whether gel, fluid, or cleaning residue was present

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Juniper Ultrasound System - DICOM send, worklist, network, or export failure
description: Troubleshooting ultrasound DICOM send, modality worklist, network connection, archive, USB, or image export failures before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ACUSON Juniper is not being used for an active patient exam before changing network settings, restarting the system, or testing exports. If images from an active exam have not transferred, verify with clinical staff that the study is preserved locally before powering down or attempting corrective action. Expected outcome: Patient images and exam data are protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication or Export Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the sonographer or department staff what failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: DICOM send failure, study not reaching PACS, modality worklist not loading, patient demographics not importing, network disconnected message, wireless or wired network issue, USB export failure, CD/DVD or external media export issue, if equipped, one study failing or all studies failing. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into DICOM, worklist, general network, or removable media export failure.

steps.title: Check Whether the Exam Is Still Stored Locally
steps.instructions: Confirm the study, images, clips, or report data are still available on the ultrasound system. Do not delete or clear local studies until transfer status is confirmed by the department or PACS team. Expected outcome: Clinical data is preserved while the transfer problem is investigated.

steps.title: Check Physical Network Connection
steps.instructions: If the system uses wired Ethernet, inspect the network cable at the ultrasound system and wall jack. Confirm the cable is fully seated, undamaged, and connected to the correct network port. If available, try a known-good network cable and a known-working wall jack assigned for medical device network use. Expected outcome: Loose cable, damaged cable, or inactive wall port is ruled out.

steps.title: Check Network Link Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for link/activity lights at the Ethernet port, docking connection, or network adapter area if visible. No link light may indicate a disconnected cable, inactive wall port, disabled network drop, docking issue, or network adapter problem. Expected outcome: Basic network link status is confirmed before changing system configuration.

steps.title: Confirm the System Is on the Correct Network
steps.instructions: Verify whether the ACUSON Juniper is connected to the intended hospital network, VLAN, or wireless profile used for imaging systems. If the device was recently moved, confirm the new room’s network jack supports ultrasound DICOM and worklist traffic. Expected outcome: The failure is not caused by using a non-imaging network port or unauthorized network connection.

steps.title: Check IP Address and Network Configuration
steps.instructions: Review the system network configuration with site-approved settings. Confirm: IP address, subnet mask, gateway, DNS, if used, DHCP/static assignment, hostname, if required, wireless SSID and credentials, if used. If DHCP is used and the system was moved between locations, confirm it received a valid address for the current site. Expected outcome: Incorrect IP configuration or failed address assignment is identified.

steps.title: Check for Duplicate or Held IP Address Concerns
steps.instructions: If the ultrasound was moved from another location and the same settings are expected to work, consider whether the previous network lease, port security, or IP reservation may still be affecting connectivity. Coordinate with IT to confirm the device MAC address, IP assignment, switch port status, DHCP reservation, and whether the address is blocked, duplicated, or assigned to another device. Expected outcome: Network-side assignment issues are ruled out before suspecting the ultrasound system.

steps.title: Test Basic Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Use available system network tools, if accessible, or coordinate with IT to confirm whether the system can communicate on the network. Check whether the device can reach the gateway, PACS destination, worklist server, or DICOM node according to site workflow. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to local network access, DICOM destination communication, or server-side availability.

steps.title: Verify DICOM Destination Settings
steps.instructions: Review the configured PACS or archive destination. Confirm: AE Title, IP address or hostname, port number, destination enabled status, correct archive selected, compression or transfer settings, if site-specific. Compare settings to a known-working ultrasound system of the same department if available. Expected outcome: Incorrect DICOM destination settings are corrected or ruled out.

steps.title: Confirm PACS or Archive Availability
steps.instructions: Contact PACS or imaging informatics to verify the archive is online and accepting studies from the ACUSON Juniper. Ask whether other ultrasound systems are sending successfully. If multiple devices are affected, the issue is more likely PACS, network, or interface-related rather than a single ultrasound failure. Expected outcome: System-specific failure is separated from wider PACS/network downtime.

steps.title: Verify Modality Worklist Settings
steps.instructions: If worklist is not loading, check the configured worklist server information. Confirm: Worklist AE Title, server IP address or hostname, port number, scheduled station name, if used, department or modality filters, date range or query filters, correct modality selection. Expected outcome: Worklist failure caused by incorrect query or server configuration is identified.

steps.title: Check Patient Query Workflow
steps.instructions: Ask the operator how they are searching the worklist. Confirm they are using the correct date, modality, location, and patient search criteria. A worklist may appear failed if the query filters are too narrow or if the exam was not scheduled correctly upstream. Expected outcome: User workflow or scheduling mismatch is ruled out before hardware escalation.

steps.title: Try a Test Send or Known Good Study
steps.instructions: With department approval, attempt to send a non-critical test study or previously confirmed study to the configured archive. Check whether the send fails immediately, remains queued, or appears complete but does not reach PACS. Expected outcome: Transfer behavior helps identify whether the failure is local, network-related, or PACS-side.

steps.title: Check the DICOM Queue or Transfer Status
steps.instructions: Review the system’s export, storage, or DICOM send queue if available. Look for failed, pending, retrying, or completed statuses. Note any displayed error message exactly as shown. Expected outcome: The failure is documented with enough detail for IT, PACS, or vendor escalation.

steps.title: Check System Date and Time
steps.instructions: Verify the ultrasound system date, time, and time zone are correct. Incorrect time settings can affect worklist queries, study matching, certificate-based communication, audit logs, or patient record association. Expected outcome: Date and time mismatch is ruled out.

steps.title: Restart Network-Related Workflow Safely
steps.instructions: If no patient exam is active and local studies are protected, restart the DICOM send process, worklist query, or export function. If the system appears locked or communication services do not recover, perform a controlled system restart according to facility policy. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication lockup is cleared.

steps.title: Check USB Export Media
steps.instructions: If the issue is USB export failure, inspect the USB flash drive. Confirm the drive is: fully inserted, not physically damaged, not write-protected, compatible with site policy, properly formatted according to department requirements, approved for patient data handling. Try a known-good hospital-approved USB device if permitted. Expected outcome: Failed removable media is ruled out.

steps.title: Check USB Port Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the USB port externally for bent contacts, looseness, debris, or signs of damage. Do not force a USB device into a damaged port. If one port fails, try another approved port if available and permitted by policy. Expected outcome: A port-specific issue is identified without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Confirm Export Format and Destination
steps.instructions: Verify the operator selected the correct export type, such as DICOM, JPEG, AVI, still image, clip, report, or study package depending on site workflow. Confirm the selected destination is correct and that the media has enough available space. Expected outcome: Export failure due to format, destination, or storage space is ruled out.

steps.title: Check Local Storage Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the ultrasound system is not low on local storage. A full or nearly full local database may interfere with study saving, export, or transfer workflow. If storage cleanup is needed, coordinate with department leadership and confirm studies are archived before deletion. Expected outcome: Storage-related communication or export problems are identified safely.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to: network ports, PACS servers, worklist server, IP addresses, firewall rules, wireless credentials, system software, department location, ultrasound configuration. Expected outcome: The issue is linked to a recent change when applicable.

steps.title: Coordinate With IT or PACS Support
steps.instructions: Provide IT or PACS support with the device hostname, IP address, MAC address if available, AE Title, destination AE Title, destination IP/port, room location, and exact error message. Ask them to confirm network reachability, firewall rules, DICOM association attempts, and whether studies are arriving but failing reconciliation. Expected outcome: External network and server-side causes are properly investigated.

steps.title: Escalate If Communication Hardware or Software Fault Is Suspected
steps.instructions: If correct settings, network path, PACS availability, and media are verified but the ACUSON Juniper still cannot communicate or export, remove the unit from clinical use if patient care workflow is affected. Expected outcome: The device is escalated for bench evaluation or vendor service rather than repeated ineffective troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: Sonography reported the Siemens ACUSON Juniper could not send completed studies to PACS and the DICOM queue showed repeated failed transfers.
CCR.Cause: Verified local studies were saved, Ethernet cable was connected, but the ultrasound was connected to a network jack not configured for imaging DICOM traffic.
CCR.Resolution: Moved the system to an approved imaging network port, confirmed worklist query loaded, test study sent successfully to PACS, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: PACS destination IP and port verified

title: Siemens Healthineers Multix Impact Radiographic System - Detector Not Detected, Not Ready, Or Wireless Detector Connection Fault
description: Troubleshooting detector recognition, readiness, charging, seating, and wireless communication issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the Multix Impact is not being used for an active patient exam.

If the detector becomes unavailable during a patient exam, stop the exam workflow and have clinical staff move the patient to another available imaging room or detector if imaging is urgent.

Expected outcome: No patient imaging depends on a detector that is not ready or not reliably connected.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Detector Complaint
steps.instructions:
Ask the radiographer what message or behavior was seen.

Determine whether the issue involves:

Detector not detected
Detector not ready
Wireless detector disconnected
Detector not charging
Detector battery low
Detector not assigned to the room
Detector works in the wall stand but not table Bucky
Detector works wired but not wirelessly
Intermittent loss during positioning or exposure setup

Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to detector power, battery, charging, wireless connection, docking, assignment, or system communication.

steps.title: Check Whether an Exam Is Still Open or Hung
steps.instructions:
Verify whether the workstation is stuck in an incomplete exam, exposure setup, or detector selection screen.

Have the user safely close or cancel the incomplete workflow if appropriate.

Expected outcome: The system returns to a normal ready state and allows detector selection.

If this resolves the issue, document the workflow fault and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Detector Is Selected
steps.instructions:
At the acquisition workstation, confirm that the selected detector matches the detector being used for the exam.

Check whether the system is expecting the table detector, wall stand detector, wireless detector, or another configured detector.

Expected outcome: The correct detector is selected and the system recognizes its expected location.

If this resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Inspect Detector Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions:
Check the detector battery level, battery indicator, or detector status display if available.

Confirm the detector is powered on and not in a depleted or standby condition.

Expected outcome: The detector has adequate battery power and is awake.

If the detector was simply off, asleep, or depleted, charge or power it appropriately and recheck operation.

steps.title: Check Detector Charging Contacts and Charger Seating
steps.instructions:
If the detector is not charging, inspect the charging bay, dock, or charging contacts externally.

Look for dirt, debris, damaged contacts, poor seating, or obstruction that prevents proper contact.

Expected outcome: The detector seats fully and shows normal charging behavior.

If cleaning external debris or reseating restores charging, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Reseat the Detector in the Bucky or Holder
steps.instructions:
Remove and reseat the detector in the table Bucky, wall stand, or detector tray.

Confirm the detector is fully inserted, aligned, and latched if applicable.

Expected outcome: The system recognizes the detector position and shows it ready.

If reseating resolves the issue, document detector seating as the cause and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector and Tray for External Damage
steps.instructions:
Visually inspect the detector housing, corners, handle area, connector area, tray guides, and Bucky slot.

Look for cracks, impact damage, bent guides, loose parts, missing labels, or signs the detector was dropped.

Expected outcome: No visible damage or obstruction is present.

If damage is found, remove the detector from service and escalate for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Wireless Communication Issues
steps.instructions:
Confirm the detector is within normal room range and not blocked by unusual shielding, storage carts, metal barriers, or being used outside its intended room.

Verify the issue is not caused by the detector being too far from the system or blocked from communicating.

Expected outcome: The detector reconnects when positioned normally within the imaging room.

If this resolves the issue, document the wireless range or location issue and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Room Assignment or Detector Pairing Status
steps.instructions:
Check whether the detector appears assigned to the correct Multix Impact room or system.

A detector associated with another room, another workstation, or a different exam setup may not be available.

Expected outcome: The detector is assigned to the correct system and appears available for selection.

If detector assignment appears wrong or unavailable, escalate to imaging support, PACS/application support, or Siemens service depending on site workflow.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions:
If no patient exam is active and clinical staff approves downtime, perform a controlled restart of the acquisition workstation or detector according to site policy.

Do not interrupt active image acquisition or unsent patient images.

Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software lockup clears and the detector returns to ready.

If the issue clears after restart, document the restart and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Test With Another Detector if Available
steps.instructions:
Use a known-good compatible detector, if available and approved for that room.

Determine whether the issue follows the detector or stays with the room/system.

Expected outcome:

If the known-good detector works, the original detector is suspect.
If no detector works, the room, workstation, Bucky interface, or wireless communication path is suspect.

steps.title: Test the Suspect Detector in Another Approved Location if Allowed
steps.instructions:
If site configuration allows, check whether the suspect detector is recognized by another compatible system or charger.

Do not cross-use detectors in a way that violates department configuration, licensing, infection control, or clinical workflow.

Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to the detector itself or the original imaging room.

steps.title: Check for Related System Messages
steps.instructions:
Review the workstation status, detector readiness message, generator inhibit message, or system event notification visible to the user.

Do not clear or ignore repeated detector readiness faults without documenting them.

Expected outcome: Any specific fault message is captured for repair escalation.

steps.title: Confirm No Obvious Environmental or Network Change Occurred
steps.instructions:
Ask whether wireless changes, access point work, room renovation, detector replacement, workstation updates, or network downtime occurred recently.

Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified as possible causes of detector communication failure.

If the issue started after a known change, escalate with that detail included.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Multix Impact wireless detector was not detected and remained unavailable for patient exams.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the detector battery was depleted and the detector was not fully seated in the charging dock.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the detector in the charger, verified charging indication, confirmed detector recognition at the workstation, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Detector reseated in Bucky, wall stand, tray, or charger
Error message or detector status captured
Suspect detector tested in another approved location
Any recent network, workstation, or room configuration changes
Visible detector damage, cracks, bent contacts, or signs of impact
Final device status: returned to service, detector removed, or room labeled Out of Service

title: Siemens Healthineers Multix Impact Radiographic System - Tube Stand, Wall Stand, Table, or Bucky Positioning Error
description: Troubleshooting positioning, movement, alignment, table, wall stand, tube stand, or Bucky readiness errors before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Multix Impact is not actively positioning around a patient before troubleshooting movement or alignment issues. If the issue occurs during patient setup, stop all movement and have clinical staff safely move the patient away from the table, wall stand, tube stand, and detector area if needed. Expected outcome: No patient is exposed to unintended system movement, collision risk, or delayed imaging on an unreliable system.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Positioning Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the radiologic technologist what failed and when it happened. Determine whether the issue involves: Tube stand movement; Tube angulation; Wall stand positioning; Table movement; Table lock or release; Bucky movement; Detector tray seating; Alignment or centering error; System not recognizing the selected position; Exposure inhibited due to positioning status. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to the tube stand, wall stand, table, Bucky, detector tray, or positioning interlock.

steps.title: Check for Patient, Staff, or Object Interference
steps.instructions: Inspect the table, wall stand, tube stand, floor path, and Bucky area for anything blocking travel. Look for stools, step platforms, cables, linen, patient belongings, foot pedals, detector cables, or accessories interfering with movement. Expected outcome: Movement path is clear. If obstruction removal resolves the error, verify normal operation with staff and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify All Mechanical Locks and Releases Are Fully Engaged or Released
steps.instructions: Check whether the tube stand, wall stand, table, and Bucky locks are in the correct state for the requested movement. Confirm manual release handles, brake releases, detents, and locking controls are not stuck halfway between positions. Expected outcome: The system recognizes a valid lock or release state. If the error clears after correcting a lock position, document and return to service if function is normal.

steps.title: Check Tube Stand Position and Alignment
steps.instructions: Confirm the tube stand is not at a travel limit, angled unexpectedly, or positioned outside the valid range for the selected exam. Return the tube stand to a normal centered or detented position if appropriate and safe. Expected outcome: The tube stand position is recognized and the system allows normal positioning or exposure readiness.

steps.title: Check Wall Stand Position and Detector/Bucky Seating
steps.instructions: Inspect the wall stand and Bucky assembly for proper seating, full travel engagement, and obvious misalignment. Confirm the wall stand Bucky is not between detents or partially positioned. Expected outcome: The wall stand and Bucky report a valid position. If reseating or repositioning resolves the issue, perform a basic functional check before returning to use.

steps.title: Check Table Position and Tabletop Travel
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not at a travel limit or blocked by accessories. Check that the tabletop is not binding against rails, patient supports, compression accessories, cassette holders, or nearby equipment. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth, controlled, and recognized by the system.

steps.title: Verify Detector Tray or Bucky Is Fully Inserted
steps.instructions: If the error involves the Bucky or detector area, confirm the tray is fully closed and properly latched. A partially seated detector tray or Bucky can prevent the system from confirming readiness. Expected outcome: The Bucky or detector tray reaches its proper seated position and the positioning error clears.

steps.title: Check the Selected Exam and Positioning Requirements
steps.instructions: Review the selected exam protocol with the technologist. Some positioning errors may occur if the selected protocol expects a specific detector, wall stand, table Bucky, tube angle, SID, or orientation. Expected outcome: The selected exam setup matches the physical system setup. If the wrong protocol or detector selection caused the issue, correct the workflow and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check System Messages and Indicator Status
steps.instructions: Review the operator console, workstation, or generator display for specific positioning, lock, collision, table, wall stand, or tube stand messages. Record the exact wording or error code before clearing it. Expected outcome: The displayed message helps identify whether the issue is operator setup, positioning mismatch, safety interlock, or likely equipment fault.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If no patient is present and no movement is occurring, coordinate with radiology staff and perform a controlled system restart according to site practice. Do not power cycle during an active exam, image transfer, exposure sequence, or while a patient is positioned. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or position recognition faults may clear. If the fault returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: With clinical approval and no patient present, verify safe movement of the affected area only. Check the reported function, such as tube stand travel, wall stand Bucky movement, table movement, tabletop locks, or positioning recognition. Expected outcome: The system moves normally, stops appropriately, and no positioning error returns.

steps.title: Do Not Force Movement
steps.instructions: If the tube stand, wall stand, table, or Bucky feels stuck, binds, makes abnormal noise, or reports repeated position errors, do not force it. Forcing movement can worsen mechanical damage, create alignment issues, or compromise patient safety. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting stops before causing further damage or unsafe movement.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Multix Impact displayed a positioning error and would not recognize the wall stand Bucky position for chest exams.
CCR.Cause: Found the wall stand Bucky not fully seated in its detent, preventing the system from confirming the selected position.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the Bucky, verified wall stand position recognition, confirmed tube alignment readiness, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact positioning error or message displayed
Whether issue involved tube stand, wall stand, table, or Bucky
Whether a patient was present when the fault occurred
Tube angle, SID, or positioning status
Whether locks or brakes engaged normally
Any unusual noise, binding, vibration, or delayed movement
Whether power cycle cleared the issue

title: Siemens Healthineers Multix Impact Radiographic System - Exposure Inhibit, Generator Fault, or Failed X-Ray Exposure
description: Troubleshooting exposure inhibit, generator fault, failed exposure, or X-ray not ready conditions before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether a patient is positioned at the table, wall stand, or near the tube stand. If the system will not expose, do not repeatedly attempt exposures while the patient remains positioned. Have clinical staff safely move the patient away or use another radiographic room if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: The patient is not held in position or exposed to repeated failed exposure attempts.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Exposure Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what occurred and when. Determine whether the issue involves: exposure inhibit, generator fault, prep but no exposure, no prep or no exposure, exposure switch not responding, X-ray tube not ready, detector not ready, Bucky not ready, door interlock or room ready warning, collimator, SID, tube angle, or positioning inhibit, or fault occurs on table only, wall stand only, or all exams. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into workflow, positioning, detector, room interlock, exposure switch, or generator-related behavior.

steps.title: Check for Active Error Messages or Inhibit Conditions
steps.instructions: Review the system console, acquisition workstation, generator status, and any visible room indicators. Look for messages such as exposure inhibited, system not ready, generator fault, detector not ready, Bucky not selected, door open, or positioning not accepted. Expected outcome: The displayed message guides troubleshooting instead of guessing at the cause.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Exam, Detector, and Image Receptor Are Selected
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected exam protocol matches the intended table, wall stand, detector, or Bucky. Make sure the technologist has selected the correct image receptor and that the detector status is ready. Why this matters: Radiographic systems may inhibit exposure if the selected receptor does not match the physical setup. Expected outcome: The selected exam and receptor match the actual patient setup. If this resolves the inhibit, complete a test exposure per site policy and stop.

steps.title: Check Room Door and Exposure Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Confirm the X-ray room door is fully closed and that any door-closed indicator is active. Inspect the door switch area externally for obvious obstruction or damage. Do not bypass interlocks. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the room as safe for exposure. If the door was not fully closed and exposure readiness returns, stop.

steps.title: Verify Tube Stand, Table, Wall Stand, and Bucky Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the tube stand, table, wall stand, and Bucky are positioned within normal operating ranges. Check for obvious misalignment, extreme angulation, improper SID, or a tube/detector position that does not match the selected exam. Expected outcome: The system accepts the mechanical position and no longer reports a positioning-related inhibit.

steps.title: Check Collimator and Field Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the collimator is powered, responsive, and not displaying a fault. Verify the light field operates normally and that the collimator is not reporting a blade, filter, or lamp-related error. Expected outcome: Collimator status does not prevent exposure readiness.

steps.title: Inspect the Exposure Hand Switch and Prep/Expose Behavior
steps.instructions: Check the exposure hand switch for physical damage, loose connection, stuck button feel, or strain at the cable. Have the technologist describe whether the system reaches prep but fails at exposure, or whether prep never starts. Expected outcome: A damaged or nonresponsive hand switch is identified as a likely external cause. If a known-good approved exposure switch is available and site policy allows, swap it for testing.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Stop Controls
steps.instructions: Confirm no emergency stop button is engaged at the operator console, table, wall stand, or room control area. Reset only according to site procedure and only after confirming the area is safe. Expected outcome: The system is not being held in a safety-stop condition.

steps.title: Verify System Power and Generator Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm the room system, generator, operator console, acquisition workstation, and detector components are powered normally. Look for abnormal indicator lights, tripped local breakers, shutdown warnings, cooling warnings, or generator status messages. Expected outcome: All major system components show normal powered status.

steps.title: Check for Tube Heat, Cooling, or Warm-Up Messages
steps.instructions: Review the system for tube heat, cooling delay, warm-up required, or exposure not permitted messages. If the system indicates a required warm-up or cooling delay, follow the approved clinical workflow and site policy. Do not force repeated exposure attempts. Expected outcome: Exposure is attempted only when the system reports the tube and generator are ready.

steps.title: Confirm Detector Readiness
steps.instructions: Check whether the selected detector is detected, connected, charged if wireless, and ready for acquisition. If the detector is not ready, troubleshoot the detector issue separately before continuing exposure troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Exposure is not being inhibited by an unavailable detector or image receptor.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is present and clinical workflow allows, restart the acquisition workstation or system using the normal shutdown and startup process. Do not power-cycle during image acquisition, active patient positioning, or while unsaved study data may be at risk. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication-related inhibit clears after a controlled restart.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Test Exposure if Cleared
steps.instructions: After correcting an external issue, coordinate with Radiology staff to perform an approved test exposure or system readiness check according to site policy. Confirm that prep, exposure, image acquisition, and image display complete normally. Expected outcome: The Multix Impact exposes successfully and returns to clinical use only after safe function is verified.

steps.title: Stop if the Fault Repeats or Indicates Generator Failure
steps.instructions: If generator faults, exposure aborts, failed prep/expose behavior, tube readiness faults, or unexplained exposure inhibits continue after external checks, stop troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The system is treated as potentially having an internal generator, tube, high-voltage, control, or interlock issue requiring qualified service evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Multix Impact displayed an exposure inhibit and would not complete an X-ray exposure at the wall stand.
CCR.Cause: Verified the room door interlock was not showing ready because the door was not fully latched; no generator fault remained after the door was secured.
CCR.Resolution: Secured the room door, confirmed system ready status, performed a test exposure with Radiology, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Table, wall stand, or free detector use
Exposure inhibit or generator fault message
Any unusual sound, heat, smell, or shutdown

title: Siemens Healthineers Multix Impact Radiographic System - Image artifact, failed detector calibration, or poor image quality
description: Troubleshooting image artifacts, poor image quality, detector calibration failures, and external setup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Multix Impact is not actively being used for a patient exposure. If poor image quality or artifact is noticed during an exam, have clinical staff stop using the room and move the patient to another available imaging room if repeat imaging is clinically needed. Expected outcome: No patient receives unnecessary repeat exposure from a system with unresolved image quality concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Image Quality Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what they are seeing and when it occurs. Determine whether the issue involves: Lines or bands in the image; Grid artifact; Noise or grainy image; Image lag or ghosting; Clipped anatomy; Poor contrast; Incorrect brightness; Failed detector calibration; Failed flat-field or offset correction; Artifact on one detector only; Artifact on all exams or only certain techniques. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to detector, technique, grid, positioning, workstation processing, or calibration-related behavior.

steps.title: Remove the Room from Clinical Use Until Image Quality Is Verified
steps.instructions: Do not allow continued patient imaging until the artifact or calibration concern is evaluated. Poor image quality can cause repeat exposures, missed anatomy, or questionable diagnostic images. Expected outcome: The room remains controlled until Clinical Engineering and Radiology staff determine it is safe to return to use.

steps.title: Check Whether the Artifact Appears on One Exam, One Body Part, or All Images
steps.instructions: Review recent images with the technologist or imaging supervisor. Compare whether the artifact appears: On one patient image only; On multiple patients; With one detector; With one table or wall stand position; With one technique range; With both table and wall stand images. Expected outcome: A single-image issue may suggest positioning or technique, while repeated artifacts suggest detector, grid, calibration, processing, or system fault.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector Exterior
steps.instructions: Remove the detector from use if safe and visually inspect it. Check for: Cracks; Dropped detector evidence; Bent corners; Loose handle or housing damage; Liquid contamination; Debris on the detector face; Damaged labels or covers interfering with positioning. Expected outcome: Detector damage or contamination is identified before assuming an internal failure. If physical damage is found, stop troubleshooting, remove the detector from service, label it Out of Service, and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Clean the Detector Surface if Contamination Is Present
steps.instructions: If dust, contrast media, tape residue, body fluid, or cleaning residue is visible, clean the detector according to site-approved cleaning procedures. Allow the detector surface to dry fully before retesting. Expected outcome: Surface contamination is removed as a possible cause of artifact. If this resolves the image artifact, verify image quality with Radiology staff and document the correction.

steps.title: Check Detector Seating and Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the detector is properly seated in the table Bucky, wall stand, or detector holder. Make sure it is fully inserted, aligned, and not tilted, partially latched, or mispositioned. Expected outcome: Detector position is correct and not creating cutoff, grid mismatch, or alignment-related image quality problems.

steps.title: Check Grid Use and Grid Alignment
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct grid is being used for the exam type and detector location. Look for signs of grid misalignment, wrong grid selection, or grid cutoff, especially if the complaint involves density variation, lines, or poor exposure uniformity. Expected outcome: Grid-related artifact is ruled out before suspecting detector failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Exam Protocol and Technique Were Used
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist to confirm the selected exam protocol, patient size setting, detector selection, AEC use, and exposure technique. Incorrect protocol or technique can cause poor contrast, excessive noise, underexposure, overexposure, or failed image appearance that may look like equipment failure. Expected outcome: User setup and technique selection are verified before deeper troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check AEC Chamber Selection and Positioning
steps.instructions: If the issue involves inconsistent brightness, poor exposure, or repeat images, verify that the appropriate AEC chamber was selected and that anatomy was properly centered over the selected chamber. Expected outcome: Exposure control issues caused by positioning or chamber selection are ruled out.

steps.title: Check Collimation and SID
steps.instructions: Verify that collimation, source-to-image distance, tube position, and detector alignment match the selected exam. Misalignment can cause cutoff, poor exposure response, anatomy clipping, or image processing problems. Expected outcome: Tube, detector, and patient positioning are confirmed as reasonable for the exam.

steps.title: Verify the Issue With a Controlled Test Image
steps.instructions: Coordinate with Radiology staff to perform a non-patient test exposure using an approved phantom or site test object if available. Do not perform unnecessary exposures in an occupied room or around unprotected staff. Expected outcome: The artifact can be confirmed under controlled conditions without exposing a patient.

steps.title: Compare Table, Wall Stand, and Free Detector Use
steps.instructions: If the Multix Impact uses different detector positions, test whether the issue occurs in one location or all locations. For example: Table Bucky only; Wall stand only; Wireless detector only; Same detector in multiple positions; Different detector in the same position. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to detector, Bucky/grid, stand, workstation processing, or system-wide image chain.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Detector if Available
steps.instructions: If the site has another compatible detector and it is safe to test, use a known-good detector under the same general setup. Expected outcome: If the artifact follows one detector, the detector is suspect. If the artifact remains with multiple detectors, the issue may involve calibration, grid, generator, workstation processing, or system electronics.

steps.title: Check Detector Battery and Wireless Status if Applicable
steps.instructions: For wireless detector use, verify the detector battery is adequately charged and the detector is connected to the system. A weak battery or unstable wireless connection may cause detector readiness issues, transfer errors, or incomplete image acquisition. Expected outcome: Detector power and communication are stable before calibration or image quality testing continues.

steps.title: Check Workstation Image Processing Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that the image is not being affected by incorrect processing, window/level adjustment, wrong exam protocol, incorrect patient size setting, or post-processing presets. Compare the raw or original image appearance if available through normal clinical workflow. Expected outcome: Display or processing settings are ruled out as the source of the poor image quality complaint.

steps.title: Review Calibration or Detector Correction Error Messages
steps.instructions: If calibration failed, record the exact message, detector involved, time of failure, and whether the failure occurred during offset, gain, flat-field, or detector correction workflow. Do not repeatedly attempt calibration without understanding the failure pattern, because repeated failures may indicate a detector, exposure, setup, or system issue. Expected outcome: The calibration failure is documented clearly for repair escalation if needed.

steps.title: Confirm Calibration Setup Conditions
steps.instructions: If site policy allows Clinical Engineering to support detector calibration setup, verify only external setup items: Correct detector selected; Detector positioned correctly; No objects in the beam path; Proper tube-to-detector alignment; Correct SID; Clean detector surface; Stable detector battery or docked status; Room clear and safe for exposure. Expected outcome: Calibration is not failing due to obvious setup or environmental issues.

steps.title: Restart the Workstation Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the issue appears related to image processing, calibration software lockup, or detector communication, coordinate with Radiology staff before restarting the workstation or system. Confirm no active patient study, unsent image, or pending exam would be interrupted. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues are cleared without risking patient data loss.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Interference Sources
steps.instructions: Look around the room for new equipment, portable electronics, damaged cables, loose covers, or anything recently changed near the detector, table, wall stand, generator area, or workstation. For wireless detectors, consider whether new wireless interference or room changes occurred around the time the issue started. Expected outcome: External environmental causes are considered before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Review Recent Service, Drops, or Room Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the detector was dropped, the room was recently cleaned, accessories were changed, software was updated, or the table/wall stand was serviced. Expected outcome: The issue is tied to a recent event if possible, which helps guide repair escalation.

steps.title: Verify Image Quality With Radiology Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: If an external cause is corrected, have qualified Radiology staff review a test image or appropriate QA image according to site policy. Expected outcome: The system is returned to clinical use only after image quality is acceptable. If the issue is resolved, document the findings and stop.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported repeated line artifact and poor image quality on Multix Impact table images.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the artifact persisted after detector cleaning, detector reseating, grid check, and controlled test imaging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the affected detector/system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented test conditions and artifact behavior, and escalated for Siemens service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether artifact appeared on table, wall stand, or wireless detector images
Whether one detector or multiple detectors were affected
Exam type and technique used when issue occurred
Whether the detector was recently dropped

title: Siemens Healthineers Multix Impact Radiographic System - Workstation, DICOM Send, Worklist, or Archive Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting Multix Impact workstation, DICOM send, worklist, archive, network, or image transfer failures before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Multix Impact is not actively acquiring patient images before restarting the workstation, disconnecting cables, or changing network connections. If images were already acquired, verify with Radiology staff whether the images are stored locally and whether patient care depends on immediate image availability. Expected outcome: Patient imaging and completed studies are protected before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what failed and when it occurred. Determine whether the issue involves: Worklist not loading; Patient demographics not importing; DICOM send failure; Images stuck in send queue; Study not reaching PACS; Archive destination unavailable; Wireless or wired network issue; Workstation frozen or application error; One exam failing or all exams failing. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into worklist, image send, archive, workstation, or network-related failure.

steps.title: Check Whether the System Can Still Acquire Images
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the system can still perform exposures and store images locally. Do not assume the entire radiographic system has failed if only DICOM, worklist, or archive communication is affected. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either a communication problem only or a broader workstation/system problem.

steps.title: Verify the Workstation Is Powered On and Responsive
steps.instructions: Check that the acquisition workstation, monitor, keyboard, mouse, touch interface, and system console are powered and responding normally. If the workstation is frozen, coordinate with the technologist before restarting. Confirm no active acquisition or unsaved image review is in progress. Expected outcome: The workstation is operational enough to review studies, queues, and network status.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Application Messages
steps.instructions: Review the workstation screen for DICOM, network, archive, worklist, database, or storage-related alerts. Note the exact wording of any error message before clearing or restarting anything. Expected outcome: The reported issue is matched to a visible system message that can be documented or escalated accurately.

steps.title: Verify Network Cable Connection if Wired
steps.instructions: Inspect the network cable at the workstation, wall jack, floor box, or network drop if accessible. Confirm the cable is fully seated, not damaged, not stretched, and connected to the correct network port. If available, check for link/activity lights at the network port. Expected outcome: A loose or disconnected network cable is corrected. If communication returns, document the cable issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Wireless Network Status if Applicable
steps.instructions: If the Multix Impact workstation uses wireless communication for worklist, PACS, or archive access, check whether the wireless connection shows connected status. Confirm the issue is not isolated to a known wireless dead zone, access point issue, or temporary network outage. Expected outcome: Wireless status is confirmed before assuming workstation or DICOM configuration failure.

steps.title: Compare With Other Imaging Systems
steps.instructions: Ask Radiology staff whether other X-ray rooms, mobile systems, CT, or other modalities are also having worklist or PACS send issues. If multiple systems are affected, the problem may be network, PACS, RIS, worklist server, or hospital interface related rather than a Multix Impact failure. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to this system only or identified as a broader department/network problem.

steps.title: Check Worklist Function Separately From DICOM Send
steps.instructions: Attempt to refresh or query the worklist if site workflow allows. If worklist fails but DICOM send still works, the problem may involve RIS, modality worklist, network routing, or worklist configuration. If DICOM send fails but worklist works, the problem may involve PACS/archive destination, DICOM queue, storage commitment, or destination availability. Expected outcome: Worklist and image transfer are evaluated as separate communication paths.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Exam Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the exam was selected correctly and that patient demographics are complete enough for the expected workflow. Incorrect, incomplete, or manually entered demographics can sometimes cause study matching, archive, or PACS routing problems. Expected outcome: The failure is not due to incorrect patient selection, incomplete exam information, or workflow mismatch.

steps.title: Review the DICOM Send Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether images are queued, failed, pending, or already marked as sent. If images are stuck in queue, note whether all destinations are failing or only one PACS/archive destination is affected. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as a queue issue, destination issue, or failed send attempt.

steps.title: Retry Send Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the workstation allows a normal resend and Radiology workflow permits it, retry sending one affected study or image set. Avoid repeated blind resends if the queue is backing up, because this may create duplicate studies or overload the destination. Expected outcome: A temporary send failure clears, or the repeated failure is confirmed for documentation and escalation.

steps.title: Check Local Storage or Database Warnings
steps.instructions: Look for messages indicating local storage full, database error, archive unavailable, or exam save failure. A workstation with limited local storage or database problems may still acquire images but fail to process, send, or archive normally. Expected outcome: Storage or database warnings are identified before deeper troubleshooting.

steps.title: Restart the Workstation Only After Protecting Patient Data
steps.instructions: If the workstation is unresponsive or communication services appear locked, restart only after confirming with Radiology staff that no active exam, unsaved image, or urgent patient workflow will be interrupted. After restart, verify application startup, worklist query, and DICOM send status. Expected outcome: A software or communication service lockup clears. If communication returns, document the restart and successful verification.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, and System Identification
steps.instructions: Confirm the workstation date and time appear correct. Incorrect system time or identification can interfere with worklist queries, study matching, archive routing, or network authentication. Expected outcome: Obvious date/time mismatch is ruled out or documented for escalation.

steps.title: Verify Destination-Specific Failure
steps.instructions: If multiple DICOM destinations are configured, check whether failure occurs with PACS only, archive only, printer only, or all destinations. A single failed destination usually points toward PACS/archive/network configuration or destination availability. All destinations failing may suggest workstation network communication failure. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to one destination or the entire communication path.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to the workstation, network jack, IP address, PACS, RIS, firewall, wireless access points, or hospital network. Recent IT or PACS changes are common causes of sudden worklist or DICOM communication failure. Expected outcome: A change-related cause is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Coordinate With PACS, IT, or Radiology Informatics
steps.instructions: If external checks are normal but communication still fails, contact the appropriate support group with the system name, room, IP address if available, destination affected, error message, and time of failure. Expected outcome: Network, PACS, RIS, or DICOM destination issues are escalated with useful details.

steps.title: Verify Function After Correction
steps.instructions: After any correction, confirm the workstation can perform the affected function. Depending on the original complaint, verify: Worklist refresh; Patient demographics import; DICOM send; Study arrival in PACS; Archive completion; Failed queue cleared or properly managed. Expected outcome: The reported communication function is restored and confirmed with Radiology staff.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Multix Impact worklist would not load and completed exams were not sending to PACS.
CCR.Cause: Workstation was responsive, image acquisition was available, but DICOM send queue showed repeated failed transfers while the network connection and cable were verified.
CCR.Resolution: Verified no active patient exam, checked workstation and network connections, confirmed issue was isolated to PACS communication, escalated to Radiology Informatics/vendor support, and labeled system limited use pending repair.

helpfulDetails: Whether the system could still acquire and store images locally
Whether worklist, DICOM send, archive, or all communication functions failed
Whether images were stuck in queue, failed, or pending
Whether one study or all studies were affected
Whether one destination or all destinations failed
Network cable condition and link light status
Whether other imaging systems had the same issue
Recent IT, PACS, RIS, or network changes
Alarm behavior, application messages, or communication alerts observed
Accessories swapped or external connections checked
Power behavior of the workstation, console, monitor, and network equipment
Environmental factors such as network outages, wireless dead zones, or recent room changes
Indicator lights, network link lights, or wireless status indicators
Final device status: in service, limited use, or out of service

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Elara Max Mobile X-Ray System - System Will Not Drive, Steer, Brake, or Release From Park
description: Troubleshooting mobile X-ray drive, steering, brake release, park lock, and transport readiness issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the MOBILETT Elara Max is not positioned near a patient, bed, doorway, slope, or crowded hallway before troubleshooting drive or brake issues. If the unit is stuck in a patient care area, secure the area and do not force movement. Expected outcome: The system is stable, and no patient, staff member, or equipment is at risk from unexpected movement.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Mobility Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what happened and when the issue started. Determine whether the issue involves: Unit will not drive forward or reverse; Steering is stiff or unresponsive; Brake will not release; Unit will not come out of park; Drive assist does not engage; Brake releases briefly then re-engages; Unit powers on but will not move; Issue occurs only after charging or after long battery use. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to drive, steering, brake release, park status, power, or operator-control behavior.

steps.title: Check for Immediate Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect around the wheels, drive base, casters, and lower frame. Look for power cords, linens, trash, tape, floor mats, bed sheets, IV tubing, or debris caught near the wheels. Expected outcome: The system is free of external obstructions that could prevent movement or steering. If removing an obstruction resolves the issue, verify safe movement and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Fully Powered On
steps.instructions: Check that the system is awake, powered on, and not in shutdown, standby, transport lockout, or startup mode. Allow the system to complete its startup sequence before testing drive or brake release. Expected outcome: Mobility controls are only tested after the system is fully ready. If normal operation returns after startup completes, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Status
steps.instructions: Review the displayed battery charge level or power status. A low battery condition may prevent drive assist, brake release, imaging readiness, or normal transport behavior. Expected outcome: The unit has enough charge to support powered movement. If the battery is low, connect the system to a known-good AC outlet and allow charging before retesting.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Cord Is Disconnected Before Transport
steps.instructions: Confirm the MOBILETT Elara Max is not still plugged into the wall outlet or charging station. Check that the AC cord is fully stored and not stretched, trapped, or pulling against the unit. Expected outcome: The unit is physically free to move and not inhibited by the power cord. If disconnecting and properly storing the cord resolves the issue, verify drive and brake function, then stop.

steps.title: Check for Active Error Messages or Inhibit Indicators
steps.instructions: Review the system display for brake, drive, park, movement, battery, door, detector, or system-ready messages. Do not clear messages without recording them first. Expected outcome: Any active mobility-related fault is captured before reset or escalation.

steps.title: Confirm the Operator Controls Are Being Used Correctly
steps.instructions: Verify that the operator is using the intended drive handle, enable control, brake release, or transport controls according to normal site workflow. Check whether the issue is repeatable with Clinical Engineering operating the unit safely in an open area. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed as equipment-related rather than an incomplete control sequence. If proper operation resolves the complaint, provide user education and document the result.

steps.title: Inspect the Drive Handle and Control Area Externally
steps.instructions: Check the drive handle, grip area, buttons, switches, and control surfaces for obvious physical damage, liquid contamination, sticking, cracks, loose parts, or impact marks. Do not disassemble the handle assembly during floor troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No obvious external control damage is present. If damage is found, remove the unit from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop or Safety Stop Status
steps.instructions: Verify whether any emergency stop, safety stop, or movement inhibit control has been activated. Reset only if it is safe and allowed by site procedure. Expected outcome: The unit is not being held in a safety-inhibited state. If resetting the safety stop restores movement, verify normal brake and drive behavior before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Verify All Covers, Doors, and Storage Areas Are Secure
steps.instructions: Check that detector storage, accessory compartments, panels, and any transport-related doors or covers are fully closed and latched. Some systems may inhibit movement or report readiness issues if a required component is not secured. Expected outcome: No open or unsecured external component is preventing safe transport.

steps.title: Test Movement in a Safe Open Area
steps.instructions: With the area clear, test forward movement, reverse movement, steering response, brake release, and stopping behavior. Move the unit only a short distance. Expected outcome: The unit moves smoothly, steers normally, and stops predictably. If movement is jerky, delayed, uncontrolled, noisy, or inconsistent, stop testing and remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check for Brake Drag or Wheel Resistance
steps.instructions: If the unit attempts to move but feels held back, listen and feel for dragging, scraping, uneven rolling, or one wheel resisting movement. Do not force the system to roll if brakes appear engaged. Expected outcome: Wheels rotate freely when the system is properly released for transport. If brake drag or wheel resistance remains, escalate for bench or vendor evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Sounds, Heat, or Smell
steps.instructions: During limited testing, check for grinding, clicking, motor strain, electrical smell, hot surfaces, or abnormal vibration near the drive base. Expected outcome: No signs of mechanical binding, drive motor strain, or electrical overheating are present. If any of these are present, stop immediately and remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle if Safe
steps.instructions: If the system is not in active clinical use and no patient data or procedure is at risk, perform a normal shutdown and restart. Retest drive, steering, brake release, and park release after the system fully boots. Expected outcome: Temporary software or readiness lockups are cleared. If the issue returns or remains, do not repeatedly power cycle the unit.

steps.title: Confirm Final Operational Status
steps.instructions: Before returning the unit to service, verify: Unit drives forward and reverse; Steering responds normally; Brake releases correctly; Brake re-engages correctly; Park function works as expected; No active mobility-related error remains; Battery status is acceptable; Unit stops safely and predictably. Expected outcome: The system is safe for mobile transport and clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the MOBILETT Elara Max would power on but would not release from park or drive during transport.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no wheel obstruction, AC cord issue, or operator-control error; brake release remained inactive after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MOBILETT Elara Max from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the brake/drive fault, and escalated for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Battery level at time of failure
Whether the unit was plugged in or recently removed from charge
Whether AC outlet or charging cord was checked
Any active drive, brake, park, or system-ready message
Whether forward and reverse both failed
Whether brake release clicked, attempted, or stayed locked
Any unusual sound, vibration, heat, or smell
Whether the issue occurred near a slope, elevator, or tight space
Whether a power cycle changed the symptom

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Elara Max Mobile X-Ray System - Detector not detected, not charging, or wireless connection fault
description: Troubleshooting detector recognition, charging, docking, battery, and wireless communication issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the MOBILETT Elara Max is not being used for an active patient exam. If the detector fault occurs during an exam, have clinical staff stop the study, protect the patient, and move to another available imaging device if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: No patient imaging depends on a detector with unreliable communication or charge status.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Detector Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist what happened and when the issue appeared. Determine whether the issue involves: Detector not detected; Detector detected intermittently; Detector not charging; Detector battery low or depleted; Wireless connection lost; Detector unavailable for exposure; Detector connected but not ready; Issue occurs with one detector or multiple detectors. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to recognition, charging, battery, wireless communication, or detector readiness.

steps.title: Check Whether the Detector Is Assigned to the Correct System
steps.instructions: Confirm the detector belongs with, or is paired to, the MOBILETT Elara Max being used. If multiple mobile X-ray systems or wireless detectors are in the area, verify the detector has not been swapped between systems. Expected outcome: The detector is associated with the correct mobile unit and not being searched for by the wrong system.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector Externally
steps.instructions: Remove the detector from patient use and visually inspect it. Check for: Cracked housing; Bent or damaged corners; Liquid contamination; Damaged charging contacts; Missing labels or asset tags; Signs of being dropped; Unusual heat, smell, or swelling. Expected outcome: The detector has no obvious physical damage that would make continued use unsafe. If damage, swelling, overheating, or liquid intrusion is found, stop troubleshooting and remove the detector from service.

steps.title: Check Detector Battery Status
steps.instructions: Review the detector battery indication on the system display, detector indicator lights, or charging interface if available. If the detector battery is low, place the detector on the approved charger or docking location and allow it to charge. Expected outcome: The detector shows charging activity and eventually reaches a usable charge level. If the detector is fully discharged, it may need time on charge before it can communicate reliably.

steps.title: Verify the Detector Is Properly Seated for Charging
